1	# Linguics
2	
3	Italian, atomised. A three-strand language-learning website built around a granular bucket taxonomy: every mistake the system records goes into a named bucket, and per-bucket history shows what a learner is getting right or wrong over time.
4	
5	This is the working repo for the project. The live prototype is in [`housing/`](./housing/) and the design documents live alongside.
6	
7	## What's where
8	
9	- [`PROJECT.md`](./PROJECT.md): what the project is and why.
10	- [`DESIGN.md`](./DESIGN.md): architecture, data model, marking algorithms, AI prompting structure.
11	- [`DECISIONS.md`](./DECISIONS.md): the rationale log. Read before reversing a decision.
12	- [`OPEN_QUESTIONS.md`](./OPEN_QUESTIONS.md): things deliberately not yet decided.
13	- [`ROADMAP.md`](./ROADMAP.md): what gets built, in what order.
14	- [`AUTHOR_BRIEF.md`](./AUTHOR_BRIEF.md): the brief that goes to author chats for content production.
15	- [`data/buckets/`](./data/buckets/): the canonical bucket trees (adjective agreement, passato prossimo).
16	- [`data/`](./data/): authored question / item batches and bucket-suggestion proposals.
17	- [`housing/`](./housing/): the working prototype. Open `housing/index.html` directly for a quick look, or serve over http for the full content.
18	
19	## Trying the prototype
20	
21	### Quick look (local file)
22	
23	Open [`housing/index.html`](./housing/index.html) directly in a browser. You'll get the inline samples (a few grammar questions and translation items) running end-to-end against the deterministic grammar engine and the stubbed translation marker.
24	
25	### Full content (local server)
26	
27	```
28	python -m http.server 8000
29	```
30	
31	Then open `http://localhost:8000/housing/`. The housing fetches the canonical content files (~130 grammar questions, 30 translation items, ~95 buckets) and the cells in the live stats panel start filling up.
32	
33	### Hosted demo (GitHub Pages)
34	
35	If this repo is on GitHub, enable Pages (Settings → Pages → Source: deploy from `main`, root). The site will appear at `https://<your-username>.github.io/<repo-name>/`. The root redirects to `housing/`, fetch loads the full canonical content from `data/`, and everything works the same as the local-server flow but over https.
36	
37	## Interacting
38	
39	- Type your answer; Enter to mark, Enter again to advance.
40	- Italian accents: on-screen buttons, or apostrophe shortcuts (`e'` → `è`).
41	- Stuck on vocab? Click the help button (or press `/`) for a per-word reveal. Each help is recorded as a miss against the appropriate vocab bucket, so the learner's gaps are tracked separately from the grammar skill being tested.
42	- Translation has inline annotations: highlight a word and tag it `<g>` (guess), `<s>` (sense), or `<f>` (flair).
43	- The right-hand panel shows the live bucket tree. Green intensity is correct/attempted; blue intensity is correct/total events. Last-N strip shows the most recent events as `✓` (green) and `✗` (red). White means untouched.
44	- localStorage holds your attempt history. Clear it from devtools to start over.
45	
46	## Status
47	
48	This is a research prototype. The translation marker is currently stubbed (returns canned per-markpoint responses driven by substring tells in each item). When the Anthropic API backend lands, the stub will be replaced with a real call.
49	
50	Login isn't wired up yet; everything is per-browser localStorage. Authoring is also offline (questions and items are produced by separate chats and committed to `data/`).
51	
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\PROJECT.md`

**Output:**

```json
1	# PROJECT.md
2	
3	The "what is this and why does it exist" document for Linguics. Read first.
4	
5	## What it is
6	
7	A web application for learning a foreign language with the same fine-grained, atomised understanding-of-mistakes that the PreIB Physics question engine brings to physics. Italian first. Multi-language-ready data model, but no second language at launch.
8	
9	Three strands of practice, kept conceptually separate, with shared identity (one login) and shared analytics (one history per learner).
10	
11	1. **Grammar** (no AI needed to mark). Author-written bank of short-form questions whose answers are a closed set of one- or two-word forms. Verbatim correctness, with a curated list of accepted variants per mark point, deterministically markable in the browser. Bulk-authored by purpose-built chats.
12	2. **Vocabulary** (handled by a sister project). The Memoriser (memtool / Smithics) is being built independently and will own this strand. Linguics links to it, shares login with it, and surfaces its data when relevant, but does not duplicate it.
13	3. **One-sentence translation** (AI-marked). A single Italian or English sentence in, a learner's attempt at translating it out. The AI's job is *not* "is this right." The AI's job is to decompose the attempt into a fixed list of atomised mark points (vocabulary, agreement, tense, mood, word order, preposition, register, idiom, sense), say which fired and which didn't, and explain partials briefly. Marks happen in both directions: EN→IT and IT→EN.
14	
15	## Why it exists
16	
17	Existing language tools score in a binary way (right or wrong) or in a coarse way ("close, but..."). Neither helps a learner build a model of *what specifically* they don't know. The physics engine fixes that for physics by making every mark point its own named bucket; over hundreds of attempts you can see "I keep losing the 'force per collision' point" rather than "I keep getting kinetic theory wrong."
18	
19	This project applies the same idea to language. A learner who writes *Lui è andato al mercato e ha comprato il pane* gets credited for: subject-verb agreement (è), choice of essere with andare, past participle agreement (andato → masculine singular), correct preposition (al), correct gender on pane (il, masculine). If they write *Lui ha andato al mercato e ha comprato la pane*, they get credit for the parts they got right (correct verb forms, correct preposition), and the AI flags two specific misses: auxiliary choice (essere not avere with andare) and gender (pane is masculine). Both misses are stored as discrete events against named mark points, so the system can show the learner over time which patterns are stable, which are improving, which are stuck.
20	
21	## What's different about translation specifically
22	
23	A translation is harder to atomise than a physics short-answer because:
24	
25	- There is no single correct surface form. *Il libro è sul tavolo* and *Il libro sta sul tavolo* and *Il libro si trova sul tavolo* can all be right depending on register and intent.
26	- A learner may be aiming at different things: a literal word-by-word render, a guess from context, a search for the sense or rhythm of the original. The same surface form is "right" or "wrong" depending on which they're trying to do.
27	
28	So the translation strand carries a small bit of learner metadata per attempt:
29	
30	- **Intent**: `literal` | `guess` | `sense`. Literal is "I'm trying to map every word." Guess is "I have no idea, I'm taking a stab." Sense is "I'm reaching for what it would actually sound like."
31	- The AI weighs strictness against intent: in `literal` mode, missed vocabulary items count harder, near-synonyms count softer. In `sense` mode, idiom and register count harder, exact word-for-word doesn't. In `guess` mode, the report is more diagnostic and less penalising.
32	
33	For v1, only `literal` is mandatory; the other two intents are advanced and may be deferred. The data model supports all three from day one.
34	
35	## Audience
36	
37	Two horizons.
38	
39	- **Now**: the author, plus a small invited group (testers, family, a few pupils).
40	- **Later**: real students or clients of a language teacher, with persistent per-user history and progress views. The product is designed so that adding the second horizon doesn't require a data migration; it requires the same building blocks, just more of them.
41	
42	This rules out a "static site + localStorage" architecture from the start.
43	
44	## Sister projects and the wider picture
45	
46	- **memtool / Smithics**: spaced-repetition app, separate codebase at `G:\My Drive\physicalsmithness files\memtool`, FastAPI + SQLite + Google Sheets. Owns the vocabulary strand and the login. Linguics uses the same Google Cloud OAuth client and is added as a second redirect URI on it. Sign in once, work in either.
47	- **PreIB Qs Project**: static physics question bank. Provides the structural pattern: schema v0.4 markPoints, normalisation pre-pass, coverage map by subtag. The Linguics grammar strand is in the same family; the translation strand is the same family with AI in the mark-point firing decision.
48	- **Calculation Automation**: physics calculation-checker prototypes. Same family of "decompose, locate the mistake, attribute it specifically."
49	- **Chemistry / Maths Papers Categorisation**: structured taxonomies of past-paper questions. Same kind of granularity, applied to question content rather than to learner answers.
50	
51	## What this project is not
52	
53	- Not a course. There is no syllabus walkthrough, no lesson plans, no "today's vocab list."
54	- Not a chat tutor. It does not have free-form conversation; it has atomised, gradable tasks.
55	- Not a vocabulary tool. The Memoriser owns that.
56	- Not a textbook replacement. The learner brings the input (a sentence to translate, a grammar question to attempt); the system marks and remembers.
57	
58	## Distinguishing features
59	
60	What this project has that the obvious comparators (Duolingo, Babbel, Lingoda, Italki) do not:
61	
62	1. **Atomised mark points** with a named bucket for every kind of miss. Not "wrong"; "lost the auxiliary-essere mark point on andare."
63	2. **Per-mark-point history**: a learner can see, per bucket, the timeline of hits and misses. Per-mistake mastery, not per-question mastery.
64	3. **Translation intent** as first-class metadata. The system knows whether you were translating literally or for sense, and grades accordingly.
65	4. **Translation in both directions** treated symmetrically, with comparable buckets on each side. EN→IT and IT→EN share the same mark-point taxonomy where applicable (vocabulary item, gender, tense), with direction-specific buckets where needed (idiom-in-target, register-in-target).
66	5. **Cross-strand identity**: a learner's vocab progress (in the Memoriser) and grammar progress (here) inform each other. Specifically, the translation strand can read the Memoriser's "this learner has rated `pane` as 5 understanding" and surface that when the learner misses `pane` in a translation. ("You marked yourself as 5 on this word.")
67	
68	## Document map
69	
70	- **PROJECT.md** (this file). What it is and why.
71	- **DESIGN.md**. The "how it works" doc. Data model, marking algorithms, AI prompting structure, API surface, login flow.
72	- **OPEN_QUESTIONS.md**. Things deliberately not decided yet.
73	- **ROADMAP.md**. What comes next, in what order, why.
74	- **DECISIONS.md** (to be created when the first decision is reversed).
75	
76	Conventions follow the Memoriser's: absolute dates, design intent in the docs, code as implementation. If the docs and the code disagree, fix one of them.
77	
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\OPERATING_MODEL.md`

**Output:**

```json
1	# Operating model for multi-chat AI-collaborative projects
2	
3	A portable description of how to run a project that uses several specialised AI chats coordinated by one human routing node. Distilled from running the Linguics Italian-learning project (~10 chats over several months). Generic and project-shape-agnostic; the role names and conventions should map to any project that fits the underlying pattern.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## When this model applies
8	
9	You probably want this protocol if:
10	
11	- One human (the project owner) coordinates several AI chat conversations.
12	- The work is large enough that no single chat can hold the whole project in context.
13	- Different parts of the work are different kinds: design decisions, implementation, content authoring, data pipelines, analytics.
14	- The chats can't talk to each other directly; the human is the routing node.
15	- The project lives in a shared folder that all chats can read and write to.
16	
17	If your project is small enough that one chat can carry it, you don't need this. If your project is too large for one human to coordinate, this model still doesn't quite scale (you'd need more routing humans or richer protocols).
18	
19	---
20	
21	## Chat roles
22	
23	A typical role distribution. Names below are suggestions; the structure matters more than the labels.
24	
25	**Architect chat.** Owns design documents, ratifies decisions between other chats, mediates disputes, issues briefs and dispatches. Does NOT write production code or author substantive content. Writes contracts and rulings.
26	
27	**Implementation chat.** Builds the user-facing deliverable (web app, document, model — whatever your project ships). Reads architect rulings and applies them. Reports back on what was built. Often the most code-heavy chat.
28	
29	**Content / author chats.** One per content domain. Produces the substantive material the project is about (questions, articles, data entries, code modules). Each authors against a brief written by the architect. You can have many of these.
30	
31	**Infrastructure / pipeline chat.** Builds data pipelines, scripts, automations. Often distinct from the user-facing implementation; lives at a lower level of the stack.
32	
33	**Analytics / measurement chat.** Builds whatever measurements the project needs (coverage statistics, mastery metrics, quality scores). Usually emerges as the project matures and people want to know "how are we doing".
34	
35	You don't need all of these from day one. Start with architect plus one content authoring plus one implementation chat. Add others as needs emerge. If a chat's job grows too big, split it; if two chats' jobs blur, merge them.
36	
37	---
38	
39	## Inter-chat protocol
40	
41	The single most load-bearing convention in this model. Chats can't talk to each other directly. They talk via shared versioned files. The human routes who reads what when.
42	
43	### Folder
44	
45	- `inter_chat/` at the project root. Gitignored so chat-thread artefacts don't leak into the public repo.
46	
47	### Filename
48	
49	- Pattern: `<ChatA>_<ChatB>_<topic>.md` with the two chat names alphabetised.
50	- One file per chat-pair per topic. Don't conflate multiple topics in a single file; start a new thread per topic.
51	- Examples: `Architecture_Implementation_render_bug.md`, `Architecture_ContentAuthor_dispatch.md`.
52	
53	### Versioning inside the file
54	
55	- Filename stays stable across the entire conversation.
56	- Internal sections: `## v1, YYYY-MM-DD, <Author chat>`, `## v2, YYYY-MM-DD, <Other chat>`, etc.
57	- Header at top of file:
58	
59	```markdown
60	# <ChatA> ↔ <ChatB>: <topic>
61	
62	**Status:** OPEN | CLOSED (with brief reason)
63	**Current version:** vN
64	```
65	
66	- Status moves to CLOSED when the thread's question is resolved. Reopen by appending a new version section and changing status back to OPEN.
67	
68	### Routing
69	
70	1. The author chat writes the file. Summarises in chat to the human, asks human to route to recipient.
71	2. Human opens the recipient chat, says "go look at `inter_chat/<file>.md`".
72	3. Recipient chat reads, responds by appending a new `## v(N+1)` section to the same file. Bumps the **Current version:** marker.
73	4. Author chat (or another chat as appropriate) reads the response next time the human routes them back. The "check for replies at start of each turn" habit handles this.
74	
75	### Communication discipline
76	
77	- **Write the file. Don't paste blurbs into chat for the human to copy.** The human routes by saying "look at the file"; that's enough.
78	- **Always summarise in chat what was added to a file**, with push-backable points flagged. The human needs to see what's been written on their behalf without opening the file every time.
79	- **When making rulings on the user's behalf, spell out each ruling's substance in plain language**, not just verbs like "ratified" or "closed". Use friendly category labels, not internal codes.
80	
81	---
82	
83	## Architect chat operating rules
84	
85	The architect is delegated authority to make rulings between chats, but the human retains visibility and can override anything.
86	
87	### Threshold for consulting the human
88	
89	Roughly three tiers, in increasing autonomy:
90	
91	- **Architectural decisions, schema changes, brief edits, new categories or buckets that affect future content** → preview the substance in chat, get a nod before committing.
92	- **Tactical decisions inside an existing rule (small clarifications, choosing between sensible defaults, applying a precedent)** → make the call, report the substance, allow override after the fact.
93	- **Closing threads with no new ruling (acknowledging good work, confirming a self-closed thread)** → just do it, summarise briefly.
94	
95	When uncertain, lean towards consulting. The human should never feel surprised by something the architect committed to.
96	
97	### Reporting substance, not status
98	
99	After any architect-side write, the in-chat report to the human must:
100	
101	- Name the file written.
102	- Describe each ruling in plain language with the practical consequence (what changes downstream, who has to do work, what gets retrofitted).
103	- List the rulings most likely to want pushback, ordered by size.
104	- NOT just say "I ratified the proposal" or "I closed the thread" or "I made six rulings". Those hide the substance and prevent the human from spotting anything off the rails.
105	
106	The temptation to summarise grows with the number of rulings. Resist it. A 7-thread engagement round needs a 7-block substance report.
107	
108	### Routing call after each pass
109	
110	At the end of any architect-side pass (a coherent round of inter_chat writes, brief edits, or new threads):
111	
112	- List which chats need to be opened up and pointed at their new content.
113	- List which threads are still pending from prior passes for FYI.
114	- Note which chats have nothing new.
115	
116	The human uses this to decide who to route next. Without the routing call, the human has to guess from the filesystem.
117	
118	---
119	
120	## Project documents at the root
121	
122	Beyond `inter_chat/`, the architect chat maintains canonical project docs at the project root. Other chats read these as the source of truth for cross-cutting facts.
123	
124	| File | Purpose |
125	|---|---|
126	| `PROJECT.md` | What this project is and why. One page; high-level. |
127	| `DESIGN.md` | How the system fits together: architecture, data model, key algorithms. Living document; edit when the design changes. |
128	| `DECISIONS.md` | Append-only log of rationales. Each entry: date, decision, `**Why:**` line, optional `**How to apply:**` line. Never rewrite past entries; mark SUPERSEDED if amended. |
129	| `OPEN_QUESTIONS.md` | Things deliberately not decided yet. Each entry: question, options, why it isn't decided, what would trigger a decision. When resolved, move to DECISIONS.md. |
130	| `ROADMAP.md` | Current milestones and rough order. |
131	| `<ROLE>_BRIEF.md` | One per non-architect role that authors substantive content (e.g. `AUTHOR_BRIEF.md` for content chats, `IMPL_BRIEF.md` for the implementation chat). Versioned; revision marker at the top; full change log in DECISIONS.md. |
132	| `INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL.md` | This protocol, instantiated for the project. Every chat reads it once before working. |
133	
134	Sub-folders:
135	
136	- `data/` for content files, schemas, outputs from pipelines.
137	- A folder per major implementation surface (e.g. `frontend/`, `backend/`, `housing/`, `worker/`).
138	- `tools/` for scripts, pipelines, helpers.
139	- `inter_chat/` for shared threads, gitignored.
140	
141	---
142	
143	## File-tool caveats
144	
145	A few practical lessons from running multiple chats writing to a shared filesystem.
146	
147	### Don't put the active project on a cloud-sync drive
148	
149	OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox-style sync layers race with active writes. When multiple chats edit the same file in succession, sync conflicts and partial-write artefacts appear (files truncated mid-write, NUL padding appearing past the end-of-file marker). The fix: project lives on a truly local path; back up via `git push` to a remote, not via cloud sync. Cloud sync is for documents that change occasionally; not for actively-edited project trees.
150	
151	### Atomic-write defensively
152	
153	Curator chats writing data files should use the `tmp + fsync + os.replace()` atomic-write pattern. Add a defensive `rstrip('\x00')` (or equivalent) before write — trailing NUL padding has shown up multiple times from sources we never definitively identified.
154	
155	The implementation chat's fetch/load layer should also defensively trim trailing whitespace and NULs before parsing JSON or similar. Two-sided defence: the author side prevents the problem if possible, the reader side recovers if it leaks through.
156	
157	### File truncation can happen silently
158	
159	Watch for data files getting truncated mid-write, especially after a series of rapid edits. Validate syntactically after writes (run a JSON parser, check line counts, run `node -c` on JavaScript). Recovery is via git history or by re-running the script. Don't trust "successfully written" reports without a syntactic check on important files.
160	
161	---
162	
163	## Memory and context conventions
164	
165	Each chat has its own context. To survive across sessions and across resets:
166	
167	- **Project-wide facts** go in `DESIGN.md` / `DECISIONS.md` / `OPEN_QUESTIONS.md` (every chat reads these).
168	- **Per-chat agent memory** (Cowork memory, Claude Code memory, or equivalent) for chat-specific facts: role description, recent context, preferences the human has expressed to this chat.
169	- **The architect's memory** should hold the operating-rule preferences of the human (consultation threshold, reporting style, routing convention, naming preferences, style rules like "no em-dash-space" or whatever).
170	
171	When a chat is woken up after a long pause, the first thing it should do is re-read `INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL.md` and any open threads in `inter_chat/` addressed to its role. This recovers the current state of the project's rules and any in-flight work.
172	
173	---
174	
175	## Common patterns
176	
177	### Dispatch packets
178	
179	When the architect wants a new content batch authored, they write a dispatch packet for a fresh chat:
180	
181	- The role's brief (canonical rulebook)
182	- Topic-specific notes (scope, coverage target, hot spots, cross-references)
183	- Relevant data files (bucket trees, schemas, existing content for reference)
184	
185	The author chat consumes these and returns the content per the brief's schema. Pattern repeats for every new content domain.
186	
187	### Retrofit asks
188	
189	When a rule changes mid-project, the architect opens a retrofit thread to each affected chat asking them to audit their batch and apply the new rule. Common pattern; expect it several times in a project's lifecycle.
190	
191	The retrofit thread should include: the rule change with worked example, the scope (which items/files affected), what specifically to do, how to report back.
192	
193	### Audit-with-substance reports
194	
195	When a chat reports back on a batch of work, it should give counts AND substance. Not "30 items revised", but "30 items revised; the X family collapsed cleanly; one borderline case in the Y family kept multi-slot because Z; here are 3 specific cases flagged for spot-check". Same discipline as the architect's substance-reporting rule, applied by content chats to their own work.
196	
197	### Status-of-the-world reports
198	
199	Periodically, the architect (or a content chat) writes a current-state summary across their domain. Useful when starting a new sub-project ("where are we?") or before a long pause ("what should the next session pick up?"). Often saved as a `STATUS_2026-MM-DD.md` file at the root for the human's reference.
200	
201	---
202	
203	## Adapting to other project shapes
204	
205	The protocol above is portable but the role distribution should match your project's shape:
206	
207	- A research project might have architect + literature-review chat + analysis chat + writing chat.
208	- A software project might have architect + frontend chat + backend chat + ops chat + design chat.
209	- A book project might have architect + chapter author chats + editor chat + factcheck chat.
210	- A data product might have architect + ingestion chat + transformation chat + visualisation chat + analytics chat.
211	
212	The architect role and the inter-chat protocol generalise unchanged; the other chats are domain-specific. Start small (architect + 1-2 others), grow as needed.
213	
214	The single biggest predictor of success is the human staying disciplined as the routing node. Don't try to do the architect's job yourself (the architect is delegated for a reason), but don't delegate routing either (the human is the only one who can see the project across all chats). The protocol works when the human routes deliberately and the architect reports honestly.
215	
216	---
217	
218	## Things that go wrong, and how
219	
220	A few failure modes observed and how to recognise them:
221	
222	- **Architect drift.** The architect makes too many tactical calls without reporting substance. Symptom: the human starts feeling out of the loop. Fix: re-up the substance-reporting discipline, possibly tighten the consultation threshold.
223	- **Stale chats.** A chat hasn't been routed in days; the human doesn't know what state it's in. Fix: routing call should mention "no new content for X, Y, Z" so the human knows which chats are dormant.
224	- **Folder confusion.** A chat is connected to a stale folder (e.g. an old project copy) and writes invisibly. Fix: include "confirm your folder is `<path>`" at the top of important threads; periodically audit for files in unexpected places.
225	- **Convention drift across chats.** Different chats use slightly different conventions for the same thing (e.g. different bucket-id shapes, different file-naming patterns). Fix: catch in code review or audit; codify in the brief.
226	- **Memory contradicting current state.** A chat acts on stale memory and produces wrong work. Fix: chats should sanity-check memory against current files before acting on remembered facts.
227	- **Two chats trying to be the same role.** Easy to spawn duplicates accidentally. Fix: name chats explicitly by role; the human should be able to list which chat is which at any time.
228	
229	---
230	
231	## A starter file set for a new project
232	
233	When starting a new project under this model, the architect chat should create these files in the first session:
234	
235	1. `PROJECT.md` (what and why)
236	2. `DESIGN.md` (initial sketch; will grow)
237	3. `DECISIONS.md` (empty with header)
238	4. `OPEN_QUESTIONS.md` (empty with header)
239	5. `ROADMAP.md` (initial milestone list)
240	6. `INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL.md` (this document, tailored to the project's role names)
241	7. `inter_chat/` (empty folder)
242	8. `.gitignore` with `inter_chat/` and any other working-thread file patterns excluded
243	9. Any role-specific briefs the project will need (one per non-architect role)
244	
245	That's the seed. Everything else grows organically as the work proceeds.
246	
247	---
248	
249	This document is portable. Copy it to a new project, adapt the role names and the brief structure to fit, and the operating model carries over. The lessons that took a few months to learn the hard way are encoded here; reading it should save the next project at least one round of pain on each.
250	
```

**Output:**

```json
1	# Wake instruction — paste this into a seat
2	
3	Short by design: it has to survive being pasted thirty times. Everything it relies on is in
4	`INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL.md` (self-check, discharge stamps, Rev 24 class tokens) and `_status/README.md`.
5	
6	---
7	
8	**Get up to date and declare your status. Do not trust your memory of these files — re-read them now.**
9	
10	1. **Self-check — your queue is a grep, not a memory.** Grep your own seat name across:
11	   `inter_chat/*.md` (both `Next: <you>` and mentions inside other seats' threads), `DECISIONS.md`,
12	   `AUTHOR_BRIEF.md`, `OPEN_QUESTIONS.md`, your own `DISPATCH_*.md`.
13	   **Then grep your class tokens too** — `all-authors` (if you author items) and `all-seats`. A ruling that
14	   binds everybody names *nobody*, so your name alone will never find it. This is how criterion 17 went
15	   unread until 418 items were bare.
16	
17	2. **Triage every hit. Most of the first sweep is noise, and that is expected.**
18	   - **Discharged?** Say so and cite the evidence (thread + version, or the item on disk). **Do not stamp it
19	     yourself** — report it and Architecture stamps the clause. Self-reported discharge is the thing that failed before.
20	   - **A standing rule** ("new items do X")? Then it is **not queue** — you comply by reading the brief when
21	     you author. Do not list it.
22	   - **A real outstanding task?** That, and only that, is your queue.
23	
24	3. **Tell Smith, in chat, before you write anything**: your queue, what you need from him, what you propose next.
25	   **Put any decision at the END of your chat message, IN PROSE, as a lettered choice with options and your
26	   recommendation** — and mirror it in your `_status` file as `needs_from_smith: decision`.
27	   **NEVER use the interactive question popups.** They write nothing to disk, so the board and the architect's
28	   ledger cannot see the decision; they halt your turn; and they strand you if Smith steps away. A decision that
29	   only ever existed in a popup is a ruling with no record. The file never replaces speaking to him.
30	
31	4. **Then write `_status/<YourSeat>.md`** per `_status/README.md` — a strict subset of what you just told him.
32	   Include `classes:`. Write it bash-side (atomic temp + rename); the Edit tool truncates on this mount.
33	
34	If your queue is empty, say so plainly — "nothing owed" is a useful declaration, not a failure.
35	
36	---
37	
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL.md`

**Output:**

```json
1	# Inter-chat communication protocol
2	
3	Read this once before working on Linguics, then apply. Every chat (architecture, housing, vocab, code, coverage, per-topic author chats) follows it.
4	
5	## The rule
6	
7	The user routes between chats. Chats don't talk to each other directly. When chat A has something to say to chat B (a ruling, a brief, a piece of feedback, a question, a reply), chat A writes it to a **shared versioned file** in `inter_chat/`. The user then directs chat B to read that file. When chat B responds, it writes its response into the SAME file as a new version section.
8	
9	## File location and naming
10	
11	All inter-chat conversation files live in `inter_chat/`. The directory is gitignored so chat-thread artefacts don't leak into the public repo.
12	
13	Filename pattern: `<ChatA>_<ChatB>_<topic>.md`, with chat names **alphabetically sorted** so the from/to direction doesn't matter. Examples:
14	
15	- `Architecture_Vocab_marker_semantics.md` (architect ↔ vocab on marker semantics)
16	- `Architecture_Housing_bucket_id_shape.md` (architect ↔ housing on bucket-id shape)
17	- `Code_Vocab_translation_pipeline.md` (code ↔ vocab on the translation pipeline)
18	- `Architecture_PronounAuthor_dispatch.md` (architect ↔ pronoun author chat on dispatch)
19	
20	One file per chat-pair per topic. Don't conflate multiple topics in one file; start a new thread for each topic.
21	
22	## Versioning inside the file
23	
24	Filename stays stable across the conversation. The version number lives inside the file as section headers. Pattern:
25	
26	```markdown
27	# <ChatA> ↔ <ChatB>: <topic>
28	
29	**Status:** OPEN | CLOSED
30	**Next:** <Seat whose turn it is>   # REQUIRED. The filename is alphabetical and so erases direction; this line restores it.
31	**Current version:** vN
32	
33	---
34	
35	## v1, YYYY-MM-DD, <Author chat>
36	
37	(content)
38	
39	## v2, YYYY-MM-DD, <Other chat>
40	
41	(content)
42	
43	## v3, YYYY-MM-DD, <First chat again>
44	
45	(content)
46	```
47	
48	Whoever writes next reads the file, scrolls to the latest section to see what's open, and appends a new section with the incremented version number, the date, and their chat name. The **Current version:** at the top should also be bumped.
49	
50	When a thread is fully resolved, change **Status:** to CLOSED at the top so others can see at a glance which threads are live.
51	
52	## Check for replies at the start of each turn
53	
54	Before responding to the user on a topic that has an open inter-chat thread, check the relevant file. If the latest version is from another chat (i.e. a response since you last looked), read it and incorporate. Mention in your in-chat summary what you found.
55	
56	~~If there's no relevant open thread, no need to check anything.~~ **SUPERSEDED 2026-07-17.** That rule only ever found what a seat already knew to look for. It is replaced by the unconditional **self-check** below: work that binds you lives in DECISIONS.md and AUTHOR_BRIEF.md as often as in a thread, and a seat that checks only `inter_chat/` will miss it. Proof: PrepositionAuthor had ONE thread naming it while 55 items commissioned by criterion 20 sat in DECISIONS, in no thread at all — and criteria 15/16 sat unread for seven weeks for exactly this reason.
57	
58	## In-chat summary alongside the file write
59	
60	The user must always know what's been written to a file on their behalf. Every time you write or update an inter-chat file:
61	
62	- Identify the file (path + topic).
63	- Summarise what you added (one paragraph or a short list).
64	- Flag the points the user might want to push back on before the file goes out.
65	- Note any open threads with other chats that this update touches.
66	
67	Don't quote the file's content back as a copy-pasteable blurb. Don't tell the user to "paste this into" anywhere. The user routes by saying "go look at `inter_chat/Architecture_Vocab_X.md`" or similar.
68	
69	## What goes in chat vs what goes in a file
70	
71	Chat is for talking to the user. File is for talking to other chats.
72	
73	- "What do you think of this approach?" → chat.
74	- "Here's a ruling on the marker semantics for vocab to apply" → file.
75	- "I'm not sure whether to do A or B, which do you prefer?" → chat (clarification).
76	- "Here's a brief for code to execute on the next pass" → file.
77	- A summary of what you wrote in the file, after you wrote it → chat.
78	
79	If you're unsure whether something is for the user or for another chat, ask the user.
80	
81	## Race conditions
82	
83	If two chats both happen to be writing to the same file at the same time, the later write can clobber the earlier one. In practice this is rare because the user routes one chat at a time. The mitigation if it ever bites: each chat reads the file fresh before writing, includes only their addition, and saves. If a clobber is noticed, recover from git history.
84	
85	## Legacy chat files
86	
87	Historical conversations from before this protocol (files at the project root with prefixes like `REPLY_TO_*`, `NOTE_TO_*`, `FEEDBACK_*`, `BRIEF_for_*`, `DISPATCH_*`, `ARCHITECTURE_FEEDBACK_*`, `DECISIONS_FROM_*`, `REQUEST_to_*`, `PATCH_REQUEST_for_code*`) are grandfathered. They stay in place as the historical record. Don't migrate them. New threads use the `inter_chat/` convention.
88	
89	## Summary for the new chat
90	
91	When you're routed to read this for the first time:
92	
93	1. Apply the convention from your next turn onwards.
94	2. Look in `inter_chat/` to see if there are any open threads addressed to you.
95	3. If yes, read the latest version sections and respond by adding a new version to the same file.
96	4. Always summarise to the user in chat alongside the file write.
97	
98	---
99	
100	## The self-check (unconditional): your queue is a NAME-GREP across six surfaces
101	
102	Estate standard from 2026-07-17 (MetaProject `COORDINATION_BOARD.md`; Linguics is the pilot).
103	
104	**"What do I owe" is not a thread lookup. It is a grep for your own seat name across every coordination surface:**
105	
106	1. `Next: <me>` in `inter_chat/*.md` — threads where it is my turn.
107	2. `<me>` anywhere else in `inter_chat/*.md` — work parked inside other seats' threads.
108	3. `<me>` in `DECISIONS.md` — next-touch clauses and "How to apply" lines that BIND me.
109	4. `<me>` in `AUTHOR_BRIEF.md` — standing brief obligations.
110	5. My own `DISPATCH_*.md` at root — my standing brief and queued asks.
111	6. `<me>` in `OPEN_QUESTIONS.md` — asks parked for me.
112	7. **My CLASS TOKENS** (`all-authors`, `all-seats`, ...) across the same surfaces — a ruling that binds everybody names NOBODY, so greps 1-6 cannot see it. Declare your classes in `_status/<Seat>.md` (`classes:`). See AUTHOR_BRIEF Rev 24 and its binding register.
113	
114	One string, six greps, nothing tallied, nothing remembered. **The grep tells you what to LOOK at; you judge
115	which hits are live work and which are historical.** It surfaces candidates, not a worklist — replacing invisible
116	work with unfiltered noise would be no better.
117	
118	## Re-read whenever you assert (provenance, not schedule)
119	
120	Not "on wake": there is no session boundary a chat can detect, and a chat is not blind, it is **stale** — it
121	remembers what the files said when it last read them and cannot tell they have changed.
122	
123	> **If you are about to state what is on disk — what you owe, what is outstanding, who to fire next — re-read it
124	> in that same turn.** If you are not making a disk claim, do not.
125	
126	Most turns make no disk claim. Cheapness is what makes it enforceable. This rule exists because the architect told
127	Smith "nothing is waiting on you" with 27 threads open, having reconstructed the answer from memory of the pass.
128	
129	## Three states of a ruling, and the third is the worst
130	
131	- **Proposed, never accepted** — visible.
132	- **Accepted, never delivered** — the seven-week gap; Rev 21(iii)'s next-touch clause is the fix. It at least knows it is waiting.
133	- **Asserted live, never ruled** — *claims to be finished, so nothing goes looking for it.* An architect's report of a
134	  state is not evidence of that state. Verify from disk before asserting, including against your own prior claims.
135	
136	## The status file never replaces speaking to Smith
137	
138	Each seat writes `_status/<Seat>.md` (schema: `_status/README.md`) **in addition to** its closing chat message, as a
139	strict SUBSET of it. If it is in the file, it was said in the chat first. The file exists so a machine can read across
140	all seats at once, never so a seat can go quiet.
141	
142	## Discharge stamps: queue = name-grep MINUS discharged
143	
144	The self-check above fails symmetrically to the rule it replaced, and PrepositionAuthor proved it on itself:
145	
146	- **`Next:` alone -> false negatives.** Work commissioned in DECISIONS or the brief, named in no thread, stays invisible. Criteria 15/16 sat at 0/92 for seven weeks. Architecture's own record.
147	- **Name-grep alone -> false positives.** A next-touch clause names its bound seat **forever**, because nothing edits the clause when it is answered; the evidence of discharge lives in the bound seat's thread, which the grep reads as *more hits*, not as retirement. A board seeded from the grep alone would have fired a wake instruction at PrepositionAuthor for a queue of **zero** — its three named obligations were all discharged before the packet proposing them was written.
148	
149	**The stamp (from PrepositionAuthor, adopted verbatim).** When a knock is answered, **the architect stamps the source clause**, appended to the DECISIONS "How to apply" line:
150	
151	```
152	[discharged: PrepositionAuthor, 2026-07-16, cue_meaning_framing v2 — 6 reframed, 48 exempt]
153	```
154	
155	**Queue = name-grep MINUS discharged stamps.** One string, derived from disk, nothing remembered by anyone. The stamp is the **pull-side twin of the next-touch clause**: the clause delivers the knock, the stamp records that the door was answered.
156	
157	- **Architecture owns the stamp**, because per Rev 21(iii) it owns the clause. **Seats do not stamp themselves** — self-reported discharge is precisely the class of thing that already failed.
158	- **Nothing is ever deleted.** The record is append-only; discharge is an ADDITION. Queues are computed by subtraction at read time, never by erasing the past.
159	- **The first sweep will be the noisiest thing the board ever shows.** Historical clauses carry no stamps, so discharged work surfaces as apparent queue. Triaging and stamping those is the pilot's real, one-off cost. **Do not read a fat first board as a fat backlog.**
160	
161	## Version numbers are labels, not ordering (ruling, 2026-07-17)
162	
163	`v1, v2, v3...` assumed Smith routes strictly one chat at a time and that each writer had read the latest state. **Both assumptions are dead** — he runs seats in parallel now — and this thread collided twice in one day: PrepositionAuthor and Architecture both wrote `v2`, then MetaProject and Architecture both wrote `v3`.
164	
165	**Ruling:**
166	- **The real key is `date + author`** (add a time if you have one). `vN` is a human label. **Collisions are legal and expected.**
167	- **Ordering is FILE ORDER.** The file is append-only and read top to bottom; physical order already carries what the integer only pretended to.
168	- **Never renumber**, including to tidy a collision. A collision is evidence of parallel routing and stays on the record.
169	- **The number was never the safeguard, and renumbering would not have saved anyone.** Both collisions here happened because the writer appended without reading the section above. **The fix is Rule 2 (re-read whenever you assert), not arithmetic.** `**Next:**` already carries the only thing the version number was load-bearing for: whose turn it is.
170	
171	## Rulings that affect everybody (Rev 24)
172	
173	Smith's question — *"if you write something that affects everybody, do you have to name every single thread?"* — exposed that greps 1-6 find a universal ruling **never**, because it names no seat. Six of criteria 13-20 name nobody; criterion 17 is what that costs (418 bare items, and a seat commissioned to fix by hand what delivery should have done).
174	
175	- **Standing rule vs retrofit task.** Most universal rulings are STANDING ("new items do X"): comply by reading the brief when you author. **Standing rules never enter a queue** — otherwise every board shows all twenty criteria forever and the ritual dies of noise, which is how the last one died. Only a RETROFIT is a task.
176	- **Class tokens, not name lists.** A clause declares `binds: all-authors` (or explicit seats). Seats grep their name + their declared classes. No list to maintain; a new seat inherits by declaring its class, which a 31-name list could never do.
177	- **Discharge scales**: a class clause accumulates one stamp per seat; each seat subtracts its own. The architect then gets a **compliance table** — bound class MINUS stamped seats — which is what Rev 21(iii) promised and could not compute.
178	
179	## Verify before you ASK, not just before you assert (2026-07-17, from Smith)
180	
181	Rule 2 says re-read before you state what is on disk. **It applies equally to what you ASK Smith for.** Architecture told Smith to "push r11-r15" for several turns running. He had already pushed. `git rev-list --count @{u}..HEAD` returns **0**, and `LL_BUILD` in the committed HEAD reads **r16** — the ask was stale by three builds, and one command would have shown it. Smith: *"stop moaning at me to do a push. I've usually done it, and you just can't see it."* He was right about the nagging; he was too generous about the cause. It was not that we cannot see it. It is that nobody looked.
182	
183	**The `claude_can_verify:` field in `_status/` exists to stop exactly this, and it was being filled in from assumption.** Housing declared *"push + deploys only Smith can do"*. Half true:
184	
185	| ask | verifiable by Claude? | how |
186	|---|---|---|
187	| **git push** | **YES** | `git rev-list --count @{u}..HEAD` (0 = pushed); `git show HEAD:<file>` for the shipped build id |
188	| worker redeploy (wrangler) | no | outside the repo |
189	| Apps Script deploy | no | outside the repo |
190	| "does it feel right in my browser" | no | genuinely only Smith |
191	
192	**Rules:**
193	1. **Before putting an item in `needs_from_smith`, try to verify it.** If it is verifiable, verify it and drop the ask. An external-action ask that Claude could have checked is a nag, and nags train Smith to ignore the board — which is how the last convention died.
194	2. **`claude_can_verify:` must be REASONED, not guessed** — same rule as `classes:`. State how you would check, or say plainly that you tried and could not. "Only Smith can" is a claim requiring evidence like any other.
195	3. **Never re-ask a standing external action without re-checking it that turn.** A repeated stale ask is worse than no board at all.
196	
197	## Asking Smith for a decision: prose in chat, never a popup (2026-07-17)
198	
199	**Rule: put decisions at the END of your chat message, in prose, as a lettered choice with options and your recommendation, and mirror them in `_status` as `needs_from_smith: decision`. Never use the interactive question popups.**
200	
201	A popup **writes nothing to disk**. So the decision is un-greppable by the self-check, un-stampable by the discharge mechanism, invisible to the status board, and gone if Smith steps away mid-turn — it also halts the seat. A ruling that only ever existed in a popup is a ruling with no record, which is the exact disease this whole protocol treats. No carve-out for project seats.
202	
203	**The wording that caused this was ours, and it is worth keeping as the lesson.** Wake step 3 used to read *"state any decision as a discrete choice with options, never as prose"*. It meant *make the options explicit rather than burying them in a paragraph*. It READ as *don't use the chat, use a form* — because a popup is, literally, a discrete choice with options. **We specified the CONTENT of a decision and left the CHANNEL unspecified, and the app layer's defaults filled the vacuum.** Seats following the line to the letter were complying, not defying.
204	
205	This is the fourth instance of one pattern in a single day: `stub` meaning three different things; `all-authors` undefined until Vocab reasoned it out; `prompt_supplies_base_form` named for the prompt but behaving on the error space; and now this. **Wording in this estate is followed literally, and it should be. When behaviour goes wrong, suspect the words before the reader.**
206	
```

#### Thinking

Now I have a very good understanding of this project. Let me also quickly look at the ROADMAP.md and ESTATE_STATE.md to understand where things are headed and the broader context.

This is a remarkably sophisticated multi-chat AI-collaborative project for building an Italian language-learning web application. The key features:

1. **What it is**: "Linguics" - an Italian language-learning website with atomised mark points (like a physics question engine applied to language learning). Three strands: Grammar (deterministic marking), Vocabulary (sister project), Translation (AI-marked).

2. **How it's run**: A multi-chat system on Claude, with ~20+ specialized "seats" (chats) coordinated by the user ("Smith") as the routing node. There's an Architecture chat, a Housing (implementation) chat, many author chats (one per grammar topic), and various utility seats.
   
3. **Current state**: The project is quite mature with hundreds of authored grammar and translation items across many topics. Most author seats have delivered their batches and are waiting for Architecture to stamp and ratify them, with 38 threads currently pending Architecture's review. The system has extensive governance structures in place—inter-chat protocols, status boards, class tokens, discharge stamps—to manage this coordination.

4. **The user's situation**: They've run out of Claude credits and want me to take over various chats. They're understandably hesitant about pasting in chat histories and want me to clearly mark any files I edit as mine.

Now I'm thinking through what I can realistically help with here.

I can read and understand the full project structure, take on specific seat roles by reading the relevant briefs and dispatches, author content following the established schemas, handle Architecture rulings if they want me to, work on the implementation side, run audits and status checks, and continue specific threads by reading the inter_chat files and responding appropriately.

The main limitations are that I don't have persistent memory between sessions the way Claude chats do—though the project's file-based protocol actually mitigates this significantly since everything lives on disk. Each conversation starts fresh, but the wake instruction protocol is designed exactly for this scenario. I can't run multiple seats simultaneously in one conversation, and context window limits mean very large threads might need to be read in chunks.

The project's design is actually remarkably well-suited for this kind of handoff.

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\ESTATE_STATE.md`

**Output:**

```json
1	# Estate state — RUN THIS, never remember it
2	
3	Before claiming ANY of: what is started, what is unstarted, which seats to fire, how many items
4	exist, who has delivered — run this and read the output. It is the item-level ground truth,
5	derived from disk. It exists because the architect twice told Smith to "stand up" seats
6	(Connective, Indefinite) that had delivered 15 hours earlier, having cached an early "0 items"
7	read across turns. A number about estate progress rots the instant it is written; only the
8	command survives.
9	
10	```bash
11	cd "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)" && python3 - <<'EOF'
12	import json,glob,os
13	def items(topic):
14	    g=t=0
15	    for kind,var in [('grammar_questions','g'),('translation_items','t')]:
16	        f=f'data/{kind}_{topic}.json'
17	        if os.path.exists(f):
18	            d=json.load(open(f,encoding='utf-8'))
19	            n=len(d if isinstance(d,list) else d.get('questions',d.get('items',[])))
20	            if var=='g': g=n
21	            else: t=n
22	    return g,t
23	topics=[os.path.basename(f)[:-5] for f in glob.glob('data/buckets/*.json')
24	        if os.path.basename(f)[:-5] not in ('vocabulary','vocabulary_frequency','orthography')]
25	empty=[t for t in sorted(topics) if items(t)==(0,0)]
26	print("UNSTARTED topics:", empty or "none")
27	# usage wave-2 leaves (fresh dispatch, tracked separately since the tense topic already has items)
28	for t in ['future','gerundio','condizionale','congiuntivo','imperativo']:
29	    f=f'data/grammar_questions_verb_form.{t}.json'
30	    n=0
31	    if os.path.exists(f):
32	        d=json.load(open(f,encoding='utf-8')); its=d if isinstance(d,list) else d.get('questions',[])
33	        n=sum(1 for it in its if any('.usage.' in mp.get('bucket','') for mp in it.get('markpoints',[])))
34	    print(f"  verb_form.{t}.usage:", n, "items")
35	EOF
36	```
37	
38	**To answer "which chats to fire", a seat is fireable only if this shows real unstarted work for
39	it AND its `_status/<Seat>.md` does not say `waiting: closed/parked awaiting Architecture`.**
40	Delivered ≠ fireable. A closed seat waiting on my stamp is MY action, not a wake.
41	
```

1	# ROADMAP.md
2	
3	What gets built, in what order, and why this order. Each milestone has an output the author can use; nothing here is "build infra and then build features."
4	
5	## Milestone 0: Decisions and scaffolding (no code in `app/` yet)
6	
7	Before any backend work, the items that are cheap to settle and load-bearing later:
8	
9	- Confirm the bucket taxonomy's top-level groups (see DESIGN.md §4). The list of 15 groups should be reviewed and adjusted before any markpoints reference them.
10	- Decide whether the v1 deployment is Fly.io alongside memtool or something else.
11	- Decide whether to set up a fresh GCP OAuth client or reuse the memtool one with an additional redirect URI.
12	
13	Output: a list of confirmed bucket groups, a deployment target, an OAuth approach. Tracked in DECISIONS.md.
14	
15	## Milestone 1: Grammar-only, single learner, local
16	
17	The first usable build. No translation, no AI, no Memoriser bridge.
18	
19	- Backend scaffolding (FastAPI app, SQLite, migrations, schema_migrations).
20	- `users` and `oauth_tokens` tables; Google OAuth working with the same scope set memtool uses (or a subset: no Sheets read at this stage).
21	- `languages` table seeded with `it` and `en`.
22	- `grammar_questions`, `grammar_markpoints`, `grammar_question_tags`, `buckets` tables.
23	- A small seed set: 20-30 grammar questions in Italian on a single topic (e.g. present indicative of avere and essere). Authored by hand or by one batch-authoring chat. Bucket ids drawn from a starter taxonomy.
24	- Grammar marker: port PreIB's `norm()` + `markShortLong()` into `static/js/grammar_engine.js` and into `services/marker_grammar.py` (Python copy for server-authoritative scoring).
25	- `POST /grammar/attempts` endpoint; persists attempts and markpoint_events.
26	- `static/grammar.html`: prompt, input, mark-and-show flow. Chips for mark points coloured by outcome. No stats yet.
27	
28	Output: the author can do 20 minutes of Italian grammar practice and have the events stored. No AI cost yet.
29	
30	## Milestone 2: Stats v1
31	
32	The data is being recorded; now make it visible.
33	
34	- `GET /stats/buckets` aggregating from `markpoint_events`.
35	- `GET /attempts/{id}` returning one attempt with its events (already partly there from Milestone 1).
36	- A simple stats page: per-bucket hit/miss counts, sorted by recent misses. Click to see the timeline of recent attempts.
37	
38	Output: the author can see "I keep missing `verb_form.passato_prossimo_essere`."
39	
40	## Milestone 3: Translation strand, `literal` intent only
41	
42	Now the AI joins.
43	
44	- `translation_items` table; seed with 30-50 EN→IT and IT→EN sentences on the same topic as the grammar seed (so the per-bucket history aggregates meaningfully across strands).
45	- `services/marker_translation.py`: Anthropic API call, structured-output schema, schema validation, retry-once-on-malformed.
46	- `POST /translation/attempts` endpoint; persists.
47	- `static/translation.html`: prompt, intent toggle (only `literal` enabled for now), textarea, mark-and-show flow with evidence-rich mark-point chips.
48	- Bucket-draft workflow: AI-introduced novel bucket ids land in `draft_buckets`, with a simple `/authoring/buckets/drafts` review page.
49	
50	Output: the author can do 20 minutes of translation, see structured marking, and review what new buckets the AI is trying to introduce.
51	
52	## Milestone 4: Authoring tools
53	
54	The seed sets are exhausted; authoring at scale needs proper tools.
55	
56	- Google Sheets integration: pull grammar / translation / bucket sheets via the Sheets API, alias-tolerant header mapping, version+severity handling. Re-uses memtool's sheet_import where sensible.
57	- `authoring/editor.html` for paste-and-edit batch authoring (mirrors PreIB's editor).
58	- Bulk import endpoints (`POST /authoring/*/import`).
59	- Authoring-side tests: "this item should grade this attempt as 'full'; let's run the marker and confirm."
60	
61	Output: a third party can write a batch authoring chat, paste output into a sheet, hit import, and see the questions appear.
62	
63	## Milestone 5: Memoriser bridge
64	
65	The vocab strand becomes useful.
66	
67	- `services/memtool_bridge.py`: HTTP client of memtool's API. Read-only.
68	- `GET /vocab/word` enrichment in the translation marker: when a `vocabulary.*` bucket fires, look up the learner's score in memtool and include it as `evidence`.
69	- `static/vocab.html`: link out to memtool, plus the recent-activity summary.
70	
71	This requires that memtool exposes the right read endpoints. Coordinate with the memtool roadmap.
72	
73	Output: a learner who misses `pane` in translation sees "you marked yourself 5 on this word two weeks ago."
74	
75	## Milestone 6: `guess` and `sense` intents
76	
77	Once the `literal` intent is solid.
78	
79	- Marker prompt updated to handle all three intents.
80	- UI surfaces the score-or-no-score distinction for `guess`.
81	- Stats can filter by intent.
82	
83	Output: the learner can practise in all three modes; the system grades and reports accordingly.
84	
85	## Milestone 7: Multi-learner, deployment
86	
87	The shape is right; widen access.
88	
89	- Deployment to a public host (Fly.io most likely).
90	- Production database (Postgres, probably; SQLite on a Fly volume if scale stays small).
91	- First non-author learner invited.
92	- Per-user delete endpoint (privacy).
93	- Single sign-on with memtool if both apps share a parent domain.
94	
95	Output: an invited group can use the site in earnest.
96	
97	## Beyond v1
98	
99	- Multi-language: add French / German / Spanish / etc. Each gets its own normalisation extension and bucket registry.
100	- Extended writing: paragraph-level attempts, sentence-by-sentence marking, paragraph-level aggregation.
101	- Speech: audio input and synthesised output.
102	- Per-bucket spaced repetition: bias the next-question picker toward weak buckets, with rate/floor parameters like memtool's.
103	- Adaptive difficulty: pick items whose required_buckets match the learner's current weaknesses.
104	
105	## Sequencing notes
106	
107	The order above is the sequence that maximises author usefulness. After Milestone 3, the system is independently valuable to the author. Each later milestone is additive; none is a refactor of an earlier one.
108	
109	The risky milestone is 3 (the AI marker). Two specific risks:
110	
111	1. The structured-output validation rate isn't high enough. The marker would need a heavier validation+retry loop, or a fall-back to letting the user re-submit.
112	2. The AI's bucket-attribution is noisy, with the same conceptual miss being put in different buckets across attempts. The mitigation is the hybrid bucket rule (DESIGN.md §4): only the parent group's namespace is open for invention.
113	
114	Both can be measured during Milestone 3 and fed back into the marker's prompt.
115	
116	---
117	
118	## Verb-form content build (added 2026-06-08)
119	
120	The milestones above are the infrastructure roadmap (housing, marker, stats, AI), largely built. This section is the CONTENT roadmap for the verb system: which tenses and moods, in what order.
121	
122	**Strategy.** Formation first (the forms), then usage (when to use them), tense-choice last. Each tense is one tree with a formation branch (deepened and authored now) plus usage and discrimination stubs (deepened later). Compound tenses ride inside their parent-tense tree (futuro anteriore in future, condizionale passato in conditional, the subjunctive compounds in congiuntivo); the standalone pluperfect (trapassato prossimo), the subjunctive, and the past historic are their own trees. CEFR levels per `CEFR_GROUNDING.md`. Cue and breadcrumb discipline per AUTHOR_BRIEF criteria 13-15 and the Rev 9 cue-economy note.
123	
124	### Formation phase: all trees built; launch + authoring ongoing
125	
126	- present_indicative: live (authored, in manifest)
127	- passato_prossimo: live (pre-existing)
128	- imperfect: live (pre-existing)
129	- future: live (authored, in manifest)
130	- condizionale: live (authored, in manifest)
131	- gerundio: live (authored, in manifest)
132	- congiuntivo (subjunctive, three waves: presente, imperfetto, compounds): LAUNCHED 2026-06-09, batch authoring in progress (not yet returned / wired to manifest)
133	- passato_remoto (past historic): LAUNCHED 2026-06-09, batch authoring in progress (not yet returned / wired to manifest)
134	- trapassato_prossimo (pluperfect): tree + dispatch ready, NOT yet launched
135	- imperativo (imperative mood): tree + dispatch ready, NOT yet launched
136	
137	So two formation dispatches remain to launch (trapassato, imperativo); congiuntivo and passato_remoto are launched and authoring; the six earlier trees are live in the manifest. When the congiuntivo / passato_remoto batches return they get reviewed and wired (as future / condizionale / gerundio were).
138	
139	Non-finite / edge forms accounted for, not separate formation trees: the past participle lives in the passato_prossimo tree; the present participle (parlante) is rare and adjectival; the infinitive's present is the citation form (no formation to test) and its past (avere parlato) is a minor compound, so the infinitive is a usage-phase topic (its constructions: prepositions, modals, causative fare, perception verbs) rather than a formation gap.
140	
141	### Usage + tense-choice phase: NEXT
142	
143	After the remaining formation batches land and are reviewed:
144	
145	- Usage dispatches per tense (the `.usage` stubs): habitual / progressive / future-of-probability / polite conditional / subjunctive triggers and sequence-of-tenses / etc.
146	- TenseChoice work: deepen the `.discrimination` stubs across all trees (present vs imperfect, future vs present, indicative vs subjunctive, pluperfect vs simple past, passato remoto vs passato prossimo), carrying the `candidate_tenses` tick (see OPEN_QUESTIONS).
147	- The gerund's adverbial usage and the stare + gerundio progressive across tenses.
148	
149	### New non-verb grammar topics (added 2026-06-10)
150	
151	- `relative_pronoun`: tree (16 nodes) + dispatch ready to launch. che / cui / il quale / chi / neuter / dove + discrimination.
152	- `preposition`: tree (17 nodes) + dispatch ready to launch. Simple, articulated, a usage-weighted branch (place, time, motion, means, verb-governed, di, da) + discrimination.
153	- Present usage: `verb_form.present_indicative.usage` deepened (4 leaves) + dispatch ready. Light branch; the present's choice-based uses live in tense_choice. The meatier usage dispatches (future-of-probability, polite/reported conditional, subjunctive triggers) are the next usage prep.
154	
155	### Noun-phrase topics (added 2026-07-13, from the OnlineItalianClub coverage cross-reference)
156	
157	Coverage is verb-deep but the A1 noun phrase was missing. Scaffolded as trees + dispatches, ready to launch:
158	
159	- `article` (definite / indefinite forms + usage/omission)
160	- `noun` (gender + plural, with the spelling shifts and irregulars)
161	- `possessive` (possessive + article, family exception, pronouns)
162	- `demonstrative` (questo / quello, quello inflecting like the article)
163	
164	Next tranche from the same cross-reference, not yet scaffolded: c'e / ci sono (esserci), the piacere construction, comparatives and superlatives, adverbs, the passive, the si-constructions, reported speech, and idiomatic pronominal verbs. Full analysis in `REFERENCE_coverage_vs_onlineitalianclub.md`.
165	
166	### Cross-cutting: misconception aggregation
167	
168	Independent of the tense build. Registry seeded (`data/misconceptions.json`, DESIGN §15). A dedicated cross-cutting MisconceptionAnalyst chat (proposes suggestions, architect ratifies) bootstraps from the `common_miss` attributes and the `must_not_include` forms, and later mines learner miss-events for cross-kind patterns. Registry RATIFIED 2026-06-09 (17 families / 67 specifics, from the full common_miss harvest). Phase-3 per-topic tagging of `must_not_include` is next (analyst supplies tag-lists, architect coordinates authors). The aggregation drill-down view is a later housing build.
169	
```

#### Thinking

Now I have a thorough understanding of the project. Let me formulate my response to the user.

Key observations:
1. This is a very well-designed multi-chat collaborative project with extensive governance structures
2. The project is designed to survive chat resets - the wake instruction exists precisely for this
3. The current bottleneck is Architecture (38 threads waiting on it)
4. The user is "Smith" - the routing node and project owner
5. They've run out of Claude credits and want to know if I can take over

What I should address:
1. Yes, I can see and read the folder
2. My understanding of the project


3. What I can realistically help with - I can step into the Architecture role to process those 38 pending threads, take on author seats for content creation, work through Housing implementation tasks, run status checks and audits, and help with specific threads by reading the inter_chat files and drafting responses
4. The file-attribution concern - I should be transparent about which files I've edited
5. The chat-pasting concern - they don't need to paste full chats since the inter_chat files and status files give me everything I need to get oriented
6. Practical suggestions for how to proceed - the project's design is actually ideal for this handoff since the wake instruction protocol was built for exactly this scenario, and I can read all the coordination state directly from disk without needing chat history; the main constraint is that I work in single sessions rather than maintaining multiple parallel chats, but that's fine since they're the routing node anyway


Right, I've read through the core orientation files — PROJECT.md, DESIGN.md references, the STATUS_BOARD, OPERATING_MODEL, INTER_CHAT_PROTOCOL, WAKE_INSTRUCTION, ROADMAP, and ESTATE_STATE. I have a solid picture now. Let me answer your actual questions.

**Can I help, and is it too much for me?**

It's not too much. In fact, this project is unusually well-designed for exactly this kind of handoff. The whole inter-chat protocol exists because chats can't remember across sessions and can't talk to each other directly — everything load-bearing is on disk. The wake instruction was literally written for "a chat with no memory of prior turns picks up a seat." That's me, every time. You don't need to paste in chats. I can grep my own seat name across the six surfaces, read the relevant inter_chat threads, check DECISIONS and the brief, and reconstruct what's owed — the same way any seat does on wake.

The main structural difference is that I'm one conversation rather than twenty parallel ones. But you were always the routing node anyway — you open a seat, give it a task, it works, you move on. We'd do the same thing here: you tell me which seat to pick up, I wake it, do the work, write the files, report back.

**What I can concretely do:**

The biggest bottleneck on the status board is Architecture — 38 threads sitting with it, mostly stamps, ratifications, and rulings. I can take that seat: read each thread, apply the ruling or stamp per the protocol, write the inter_chat response, and give you the substance report. That alone would unblock most of the parked author seats.

I can also take author seats (wake one, run the self-check, author items against the brief and dispatch, validate), do Housing implementation work (the JS/CSS/HTML in `housing/`), run the estate-state script, do audits, or work the Cr17Sweep-style compliance tasks.

**On declaring files as mine:**

Absolutely — I'll mark anything I write or edit. The inter_chat protocol already has a convention for this (version sections carry the author name and date). For any file I touch, I'll note it clearly in my report to you, and if it's a data file or coordination file, I'll identify myself in the version header. If you want a specific marker format (a comment at the top, a particular tag), tell me and I'll follow it consistently.

**On pasting in chats:**

You shouldn't need to. If there's a specific ruling or preference you expressed in a Claude chat that never made it to disk, then yes, tell me and I'll record it properly. But the protocol's whole point is that the disk state IS the project state. If something only existed in a chat popup or an in-conversation remark and was never written to a file, it was already lost by design — the protocol explicitly warns about this.

**Practical suggestion for where to start:**

Given the board, the highest-leverage move is for me to take the Architecture seat and work through those 38 threads. Most are stamps and ratifications that are blocking real authors from proceeding. I'd read each thread, verify from disk (per the "re-read whenever you assert" rule), apply or query, and give you a plain-language substance report on each so you can spot anything off the rails. Want me to start there, or is there something more pressing — a specific seat you were mid-conversation with, or a decision you need made first?

### **You**

Well, I think what would be nice is we could do the first game, gender drill sorted, which would say, "Oh, I see it's all right."
If we look at this, there is no firing or anything. We do not want to have the examples. If we did want examples, we would want them to line up. We want a little bit of an outline around the object. They feel more like options.
We don't want shortening because we want the language a bit smaller. We don't want it to say two plurals with different meanings. We need that to be clearer: what that means.
We want:
1. 3 should say masculine or feminine, maybe that says M or F, depending on gender.
2. 4 says M or F with different meanings.
3. 5 says masculine singular, feminine plural.
4. 6 says feminine singular, masculine plural.
5. 7 says masculine singular, masculine plural, and feminine plural with different meanings.
Then we should have all of those words in play to have a far smaller number of the simple ones that end in O and OH. Yes, and we should go through and work out what all the weird ones are that need to be written in.do you think you can do that?

### **Assistant**

1. They want to look at the gender drill (noun gender exercise) in the housing
2. They're describing how the "options" or answer choices should be displayed
3. They don't want examples shown (or if shown, they should line up)
4. They want a little outline around options to make them feel more like options
5. They don't want "shortening" - they want the language/text a bit smaller


6. The answer options need clearer labels that specify exactly which gender and number combinations are in play—so instead of vague labels, each option explicitly states whether it's masculine singular, feminine plural, or whatever combination applies
7. They want to prioritize the irregular nouns (the "weird ones") in the drill and reduce the straightforward -o/-a nouns to keep the exercise focused on the trickier cases
8. I need to identify all those irregular nouns that require special handling

**Output:**

```json
Here's my report on the noun gender drill in the Linguics project.

---

## File Paths

| Purpose | Path |
|---------|------|
| Housing entry page | `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\index.html` |
| App controller (UI + drill logic) | `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js` |
| Grammar marking engine | `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\grammar_engine.js` |
| Noun bucket tree | `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\data\buckets\noun.json` |
| Noun grammar questions | `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\data\grammar_questions_noun.json` |

---

## 1. Bucket Tree for Noun Gender (`data/buckets/noun.json`)

The tree is a flat array of nodes with `id`/`parent_id` relationships:

```
noun (root, aggregate)
├── noun.gender (aggregate)
│   ├── noun.gender.regular      – "-o masculine, -a feminine"
│   ├── noun.gender.e_ending     – "-e nouns (gender to learn)"
│   └── noun.gender.irregular    – "Irregular gender" (la mano, il problema, la foto)
└── noun.plural (aggregate)
    ├── noun.plural.regular
    ├── noun.plural.spelling
    ├── noun.plural.invariable
    ├── noun.plural.irregular
    └── noun.plural.compound
```

Each leaf carries `cefr_importance` bands (A1–C2), a `canonical_example`, a `common_miss`, and `direction: "bidirectional"`.

---

## 2. Grammar Questions Structure (`data/grammar_questions_noun.json`)

All items are `"type": "short"` (free-text, substring-marked). There are **no MCQ-type items** and **no "Choose:" prompts** in this file. The gender items use subtopics `gender.regular`, `gender.e_ending`, and `gender.irregular`.

Each item has these fields:

- `external_id` – e.g. `"noun_gen_reg_01"`, `"noun_gen_e_01"`, `"noun_gen_irr_01"`
- `language_code` – `"it"`
- `topic` – `"noun"`
- `subtopic` – e.g. `"gender.regular"`
- `difficulty` – integer (1)
- `cefr_level_target` – e.g. `"A1"`, `"A2"`, `"B1"`
- `prompt` – Italian fill-in-the-blank, e.g. `"Completa con l'articolo giusto: 'Metti ____ libro sul tavolo.'"`
- `type` – always `"short"`
- `marks` – 1.0
- `wrong_answer_is_form_error_only` – boolean
- `markpoints` – array (usually 1 element), each with:
  - `order_index`, `credit`, `bucket` (e.g. `"noun.gender.regular"`), `label`
  - `any_phrases` – accepted answers (e.g. `{"phrase": "il", "match_at": "word"}`)
  - `must_not_include` – diagnostic wrong answers (e.g. `{"phrase": "la", "match_at": "word"}`)
- `vocab_help` – array of lemma glosses with reveal text and bucket ids
- `explanation` – full pedagogical explanation shown post-answer
- `examiner_note` – internal marking rationale
- `version` – 1

The prompts ask the learner to supply the correct article (il/la/un/una) or the correct adjective form (buona vs buono) into a blank. Marking is substring-based via `grammar_engine.js`.

---

## 3. The Gender Drill (Vocab Strand) — Distinct from Grammar Questions

There **is** a dedicated "Gender drill" mode, but it lives in the **Vocabulary strand**, not the Grammar strand. It's a choose-from-options classifier, completely separate from the fill-in-the-blank grammar flow.

**How to activate:** On the entry/welcome screen, under "Special drills," there's a toggle chip labelled **"Gender drill (nouns)"** (line ~7171 of app.js). Toggling it sets `vocabFilter.genderDrill = true`.

**What it does:**

- Filters the deck to **nouns only** with a known gender (`m`, `f`, or `mf`). Plain m/f nouns (classes 1–2) are thinned to every 20th rank; "interesting" classes (3+) are always included.
- Renders a card showing the **bare noun** (no article, no context) with the prompt: *"What gender is **<lemma>**?"*
- Presents a **fixed 7-option button list** (`GENDER_CLASSES`):

| # | Key | Label | Example |
|---|-----|-------|---------|
| 1 | masculine | Masculine | libro, tavolo |
| 2 | feminine | Feminine | casa, porta |
| 3 | either | Either gender | il/la giornalista |
| 4 | depends_meaning | Depends on meaning | il fine / la fine |
| 5 | m_sg_f_pl | Masc. singular, fem. plural | il braccio → le braccia |
| 6 | f_sg_m_pl | Fem. singular, masc. plural | l'eco → gli echi |
| 7 | two_plurals | Two plurals, diff. meaning | i bracci / le braccia |

- The correct answer is **derived at runtime** from the vocab entry's `gender_class` tag (or heuristically from `gender` + `plural` fields) — no per-noun authoring needed.
- Marking: exact class match = hit; wrong pick = miss, and the mistaken class is recorded as a misconception (`gender_class.thought_<key>`).
- Post-answer: the correct button gets `.gc-correct`, the wrong pick gets `.gc-wrong`, and a "Next" button advances.

**Key code locations in `app.js`:**
- `GENDER_CLASSES` constant: ~line 2456
- `nounGenderClass(entry)`: ~line 2468
- `markGenderDrill(entry, pickedClass)`: ~line 2482
- `renderGenderDrillCard(entry, keepAxes)`: ~line 2513
- Deck filtering for gender drill: ~line 2275
- Entry-screen toggle: ~line 7171
- Session start wiring: ~line 7187

---

## 4. Grammar Strand "Drill" (Bucket Drill) — Not Gender-Specific

The Grammar strand has a generic **bucket drill** mechanism: clicking a bucket tile in the live-stats panel sets `grammarFilter.bucketPath`, narrowing the grammar deck to items whose markpoints reference that bucket. This is labelled e.g. "Pronoun drill" or "Tense choice drill" in the filter bar. There's no noun-gender-specific entry in the `_drillLabels` map, so drilling into `noun.gender` would just show as "Drill" with the bucket's label. This drill still uses the same fill-in-the-blank `"short"` items and substring marking — it's a scope filter, not a different interaction mode.

The Grammar strand also supports an **Input mode** selector (`"type + tap"` / `"type only"` / `"tap only"`) for items whose prompt contains a stated choice set (e.g. "Choose: che / a cui"), but **no noun gender items use this pattern**.

---

## Summary

- The **grammar questions** for noun gender are traditional fill-in-the-blank items (supply the article or adjective form), marked by substring matching.
- The **vocab gender drill** is the true "game/drill mode" — a multiple-choice classifier where the learner sees a bare noun and picks its gender behaviour from 7 fixed options. It's architecturally separate (vocab strand, own rendering path, own marking function, no authored items).
```

28	    document.addEventListener('click', function(e){

33	      window.gtag('event', 'site_click', {

38	    }, true);
