#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\grammar_engine.js`

**Output:**

```json
1	/* ============================================================================
2	   Grammar marker
3	   Substring-based, deterministic. Emits per-markpoint attempted/correctness
4	   (binary in v1).
5	
6	   Public API:
7	     window.LL.markGrammar(question, rawResponse) -> {
8	       overall: { attempted_overall, correctness_overall, marks_awarded, marks_possible, status },
9	       markpoints: [ { bucket, attempted_credit, correctness_credit, outcome, evidence, expected, label } ],
10	       orthography: [ { bucket, evidence } ]  // accent slips picked up by the
11	                                              // accent-folded fallback
12	     }
13	   ============================================================================ */
14	
15	(function () {
16	  "use strict";
17	  const LL = window.LL || (window.LL = {});
18	
19	  // Classify an accent slip three ways for the misconception axis: the learner
20	  // DROPPED a mark (parlo for parlò), ADDED one (hò for ho), or used the WRONG
21	  // mark (perchè for perché). Span-mapped on NFC so folded/unfolded indices line
22	  // up (foldAccents is 1 char -> 1 char, so lengths match). Anything mixed or
23	  // uncertain returns null and the caller logs the parent id, exactly as before.
24	  // See inter_chat/Architecture_Housing_orthography_split.md.
25	  const ACCENTED_RE = /[\u00e0\u00e8\u00e9\u00ec\u00f2\u00f9\u00c0\u00c8\u00c9\u00cc\u00d2\u00d9]/;
26	  function classifyAccentSlip(attemptNorm, attemptFolded, positivePhrase) {
27	    try {
28	      const pNorm = LL.norm(positivePhrase).normalize("NFC");
29	      const pFolded = LL.foldAccents(pNorm);
30	      const idx = attemptFolded.indexOf(pFolded);
31	      if (idx === -1) return null;
32	      const aSpan = attemptNorm.normalize("NFC").substr(idx, pFolded.length);
33	      if (aSpan.length !== pNorm.length) return null;      // length drift: bail to parent
34	      let missing = 0, added = 0, wrong = 0;
35	      for (let k = 0; k < pNorm.length; k++) {
36	        const pc = pNorm[k], ac = aSpan[k];
37	        const pAcc = ACCENTED_RE.test(pc), aAcc = ACCENTED_RE.test(ac);
38	        if (pAcc && !aAcc) missing++;
39	        else if (!pAcc && aAcc) added++;
40	        else if (pAcc && aAcc && pc !== ac) wrong++;
41	      }
42	      const hits = [["missing", missing], ["added", added], ["wrong_mark", wrong]].filter(x => x[1] > 0);
43	      if (hits.length !== 1) return null;                  // none or mixed classes: parent
44	      // Enriched return (live_round2 ask 3 + pulse payload): the learner's
45	      // actual span and WHICH accented characters were involved, so per-class
46	      // analytics are reconstitutable from the event, not just a count.
47	      const chars = [];
48	      for (let k = 0; k < pNorm.length; k++) {
49	        if (pNorm[k] !== aSpan[k]) chars.push(ACCENTED_RE.test(pNorm[k]) ? pNorm[k] : aSpan[k]);
50	      }
51	      return { cls: hits[0][0], written: aSpan, chars };
52	    } catch (e) { return null; }
53	  }
54	
55	  function statusFromCombined(awarded, possible) {
56	    if (possible <= 0) return "not_attempted";
57	    const f = awarded / possible;
58	    if (f >= 0.999) return "hit";
59	    if (f <= 0.001) return "miss";
60	    return "partial";
61	  }
62	
63	  function markGrammar(q, raw) {
64	    const possible = q.marks || 1;
65	    const points = Array.isArray(q.markpoints) ? q.markpoints : [];
66	    const normed = LL.norm(raw);
67	    const normedFolded = LL.normAccentFolded(raw);
68	    // Case-preserving haystacks for case_sensitive markpoints (formal La/Sua/Le).
69	    const normedCased = LL.normCased(raw);
70	    const normedFoldedCased = LL.foldAccents(LL.normCased(raw));
71	    const inputWasNonEmpty = String(raw || "").trim() !== "";
72	
73	    let awarded = 0;
74	    let attemptedSum = 0;
75	    let correctnessSum = 0;
76	    let respondedCount = 0;
77	    const mpResults = [];
78	    const orthographyHits = [];
79	    // Item-level misconception attribution: ids carried by the must_not_include
80	    // entry that actually fired. Pure attribution, marking is unchanged, nothing
81	    // rendered yet. See inter_chat/Architecture_Housing_misconception_item_tags.md.
82	    const misconceptionHits = [];
83	
84	    for (const mp of points) {
85	      const credit = (typeof mp.credit === "number") ? mp.credit : 1;
86	      const result = {
87	        bucket: mp.bucket,
88	        label: mp.label || "",
89	        credit_weight: credit,
90	        attempted_credit: 0,
91	        correctness_credit: null,
92	        outcome: "not_attempted",
93	        evidence: null,
94	        expected: (mp.any_phrases && mp.any_phrases[0])
95	          ? ((typeof mp.any_phrases[0] === "object" && mp.any_phrases[0].phrase)
96	              ? mp.any_phrases[0].phrase
97	              : mp.any_phrases[0])
98	          : null
99	      };
100	
```

```json
20:    try { ctx.vocab_scope = JSON.stringify({ theme: vocabFilter.theme, themes: vocabFilter.themes, ranges: vocabFilter.rankRanges, gender: vocabFilter.genderDrill }); } catch (e) {}

509:      iSel.title = "How to answer items that offer a stated choice: tappable chips, typing, or both";
515:    // ---- Drill chip (same row, so all three restrictions read together) ----
518:      dChip.className = "filter-chip drill active";
522:      dKey.textContent = "Drilled into";
525:      dVal.textContent = drillChipLabel(filterObj);
527:      // The undo affordance renders WHETHER OR NOT the pre-drill scope had a
528:      // label: drilling from "all topics" used to leave only the tiny x,
529:      // which is Smith's invisible drill-out (drill_into_reach v2 defect 2).
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js`

**Output:**

```json
1	/* ============================================================================
2	   App controller: UI rendering and event wiring.
3	   ============================================================================ */
4	
5	(function () {
6	  "use strict";
7	  const LL = window.LL || (window.LL = {});
8	
9	  // Build identifier. Bump when shipping a deploy worth distinguishing in
10	  // diagnostics. Surfaced in the page footer so two tabs on different builds
11	  // are visually distinguishable. See inter_chat/Architecture_Housing_cache_busting_and_data_load_messaging.md.
12	  const LL_BUILD = "2026-07-21-r70";
13	  LL.build = LL_BUILD;  // read by the feedback widget's context() at submit time
14	  // App-side context merged into every pulse row's extra_json (maximal
15	  // payload ruling) without coupling pulse.js to app internals.
16	  LL.pulseContext = function () {
17	    const ctx = { input_mode: getInputMode() };
18	    try { ctx.grammar_scope = filterSig(grammarFilter); } catch (e) {}
19	    try { ctx.translation_scope = filterSig(translationFilter); } catch (e) {}
20	    try { ctx.vocab_scope = JSON.stringify({ theme: vocabFilter.theme, themes: vocabFilter.themes, ranges: vocabFilter.rankRanges, gender: vocabFilter.genderDrill }); } catch (e) {}
21	    return ctx;
22	  };
23	  // Touch-first device (no hover, coarse pointer): tap interactions replace
24	  // keyboard ones. Computed once; used for tap-to-mark on MCQ.
25	  const TOUCH_FIRST = !!(window.matchMedia
26	    && window.matchMedia("(hover: none) and (pointer: coarse)").matches);
27	  // Scroll the fresh question card into view. After reading a result the
28	  // page is often scrolled deep (result details, stats below on mobile);
29	  // without this the next card renders off-screen and the learner only
30	  // sees whatever the input's minimal focus-scroll happens to reveal.
31	  function ensureCardVisible(cardEl) {
32	    if (!cardEl || !cardEl.getBoundingClientRect) return;
33	    const r = cardEl.getBoundingClientRect();
34	    const vh = window.innerHeight || document.documentElement.clientHeight || 800;
35	    if (r.top < 0 || r.top > vh * 0.35) {
36	      window.scrollTo(0, Math.max(0, window.scrollY + r.top - 12));
37	    }
38	  }
39	
40	  LL.state = LL.store.loadState();
41	
42	  // Migration 2026-05-28: drop legacy-shape vocab events.
43	  //
44	  // The bucket-id shape for vocab events changed to include POS (always) and
45	  // gender (for gender-split lemmas). Old shape `vocabulary.it.<lemma>.<aspect>`
46	  // can't be reattributed to a specific entry, so we drop those events. The
47	  // heatmap goes blank for vocab and re-fills as the learner practises under
48	  // the new shape. Idempotent: legacy events were filtered out the first time
49	  // this code ran, subsequent loads find none and the migration is a no-op.
50	  (function migrateLegacyVocabEvents() {
51	    const parse = LL.parseVocabBucketId;
52	    if (typeof parse !== "function") return;
53	    let dropped = 0;
54	    for (const att of LL.state.attempts || []) {
55	      if (!Array.isArray(att.events)) continue;
56	      const before = att.events.length;
57	      att.events = att.events.filter(ev => {
58	        const parsed = parse(ev.bucket);
59	        if (!parsed) return true;              // non-vocab event, keep
60	        if (parsed.pos) return true;           // new shape, keep
61	        return false;                          // legacy shape, drop
62	      });
63	      dropped += before - att.events.length;
64	    }
65	    if (dropped > 0) {
66	      LL.store.saveState(LL.state);
67	      console.log("[Linguics] Migrated vocab event history:", dropped, "legacy-shape events dropped. Vocab heatmap will re-fill as you practise.");
68	    }
69	  })();
70	
71	  // Note: these are `let` so the fetch-loader can replace them with real content.
72	  let allBuckets = window.LL_BUCKETS || [];
73	  let bucketIndex = LL.store.indexBuckets(allBuckets);
74	  // Expose for translation_marker.js to consult bucket.attributes.direction
75	  // when filtering candidates by direction.
76	  LL.bucketsById = bucketIndex.byId;
77	  let grammarQuestions = window.LL_GRAMMAR_QUESTIONS || [];
78	  let translationItems = window.LL_TRANSLATION_ITEMS || [];
79	  // Shuffled decks used for navigation; reshuffle on wrap.
80	  let grammarDeck = [];
```

```json
2273	  // The deck-ready set: in-scope entries with the remaining filters applied.
2274	  function filteredVocabEntries() {
2275	    if (vocabFilter.genderDrill) {
2276	      // Gender drill deck: clean-gendered nouns only (exclude the 'ambiguous'
2277	      // gap-fill junk), default-focused on the interesting classes (3+) with a
2278	      // thinned baseline of plain m/f. Full per-class multi-select is a follow-up.
2279	      return vocabEntriesInScope().filter(v => {
2280	        if (v.pos !== "noun") return false;
2281	        const g = String(v.gender);
2282	        if (g !== "m" && g !== "f" && g !== "mf") return false;
2283	        if (nounGenderClass(v) <= 2 && typeof v.rank === "number" && (v.rank % 20 !== 0)) return false;
2284	        return true;
2285	      });
2286	    }
2287	    return vocabEntriesInScope().filter(v => {
2288	      if (vocabFilter.band && v.band !== vocabFilter.band) return false;
2289	      if (vocabFilter.rankRange && typeof v.rank === "number") {
2290	        if (v.rank < vocabFilter.rankRange.start || v.rank > vocabFilter.rankRange.end) return false;
2291	      }
2292	      if (vocabFilter.theme) {
2293	        if (!Array.isArray(v.themes) || !themeMatchesSelected(v.themes, vocabFilter.theme)) return false;
2294	      }
2295	      if (Array.isArray(vocabFilter.themes) && vocabFilter.themes.length) {
2296	        if (!Array.isArray(v.themes) || !vocabFilter.themes.some(id => themeMatchesSelected(v.themes, id))) return false;
2297	      }
2298	      if (vocabFilter.genderDrill && v.pos !== "noun") return false;
2299	      if (vocabFilter.genderClass) {
2300	        if (v.pos !== "noun") return false;
2301	        const cls = v.noun_class || (v.gender === "m" ? "unspecified_m" : v.gender === "f" ? "unspecified_f" : "unspecified");
2302	        if (cls !== vocabFilter.genderClass) return false;
2303	      }
2304	      return true;

2308	  function ensureVocabDeck() {
2309	    if (!vocabDeck.length || vocabIndex >= vocabDeck.length) {
2310	      vocabDeck = weightedVocabShuffle(filteredVocabEntries());
2311	      vocabDeckDirs = (vocabFilter.direction === "mix")
2312	        ? vocabDeck.map(() => Math.random() < 0.5 ? "it_en" : "en_it")
2313	        : [];
2314	      vocabIndex = 0;
2315	    }
2316	  }
2317	
2318	  // Score-weighted, without-replacement shuffle. Weight = 1.1 - score, where
2319	  // score is the lemma's current recency-weighted correctness in the active
2320	  // direction. Untouched lemmas score 0 and get max weight 1.1; fully known
2321	  // lemmas (score 1.0) get min weight 0.1, an 11x ratio. The user explicitly
2322	  // chose this formula.
2323	  function weightedVocabShuffle(entries) {
2324	    const pool = entries.slice();
2325	    if (pool.length <= 1) return pool;
2326	    const activeDir = (vocabFilter && vocabFilter.direction === "it_en") ? "passive" : "active";
2327	    const weights = pool.map(e => {
2328	      // Per-entry weights so multi-entry lemmas don't share a single score.
2329	      // A miss on presto.interjection should only demote that entry's weight,
2330	      // not also demote presto.adverb and presto.adjective.
2331	      const events = eventsForEntry(e, { direction: activeDir, aspect: "translation" });
2332	      const wc = recencyWeightedCorrectness(events);
2333	      const score = wc.hasEvents ? wc.correctness : 0;
2334	      return Math.max(0.05, 1.1 - score);
2335	    });
2336	    const out = [];
2337	    while (pool.length > 0) {
2338	      let total = 0;
2339	      for (const w of weights) total += w;
2340	      let r = Math.random() * total;
2341	      let idx = 0;
2342	      for (; idx < weights.length; idx++) {
2343	        r -= weights[idx];
2344	        if (r <= 0) break;
2345	      }
2346	      if (idx >= weights.length) idx = weights.length - 1;
2347	      out.push(pool[idx]);
2348	      pool.splice(idx, 1);
2349	      weights.splice(idx, 1);
```

#### Thinking

Now I have a clear picture. The user is talking about the **vocab gender drill** — the 7-option classifier where you see a bare noun and pick its gender class. They want to:

1. Remove the examples from the option buttons (or if kept, make them line up)
2. Add a little outline around the options to make them feel more like options
3. Make the text/language a bit smaller (not "shortening" — they want smaller font)
4. Rename the options to be clearer:
   - Option 3: "Masculine or Feminine" (or "M or F")
   - Option 4: "M or F with different meanings"
   - Option 5: "Masculine singular, feminine plural"
   - Option 6: "Feminine singular, masculine plural"
   - Option 7: "Masculine singular, masculine plural, and feminine plural with different meanings"

Let me double-check against what they actually said to make sure I'm mapping these correctly.

They want option 3 to display "M or F" based on the word's gender, option 4 to show "M or F with different meanings," and then options 5 through 7 follow the pattern I outlined. They also want to reduce the number of simple -o/-a nouns in the drill and identify which irregular ones need to be included. I should look at the current gender drill rendering code to see how it's structured and what changes are needed.


Good — I can see exactly what you're talking about. It's the vocab-strand gender drill (the 7-option classifier). Let me look at the actual rendering code and the styling so I can make the changes you described.

```json
2451	  // ---- Gender drill (Architecture_Housing_gender_plural_drill v3): a uniform
2452	  // fixed-option gender-BEHAVIOUR classifier. Show the BARE noun (no article,

2454	  // every time; the correct answer is DERIVED from the entry, so no per-noun

2456	  const GENDER_CLASSES = [
2457	    { n: 1, key: "masculine",       label: "Masculine",                   eg: "libro, tavolo" },
2458	    { n: 2, key: "feminine",        label: "Feminine",                    eg: "casa, porta" },
2459	    { n: 3, key: "either",          label: "Either gender",               eg: "il/la giornalista" },
2460	    { n: 4, key: "depends_meaning", label: "Depends on meaning",          eg: "il fine / la fine" },
2461	    { n: 5, key: "m_sg_f_pl",       label: "Masc. singular, fem. plural", eg: "il braccio → le braccia" },
2462	    { n: 6, key: "f_sg_m_pl",       label: "Fem. singular, masc. plural", eg: "l'eco → gli echi" },
2463	    { n: 7, key: "two_plurals",     label: "Two plurals, diff. meaning",  eg: "i bracci / le braccia" }
2464	  ];
2465	  // Prefers an explicit `gender_class` tag (Vocab populates it when the noun db
2466	  // is cleaned - Smith: "imagine the dbase will be fixed"); else a best-effort
2467	  // interim derivation from gender + plural.
2468	  function nounGenderClass(entry) {
2469	    if (!entry) return 1;
2470	    const tag = entry.gender_class;
2471	    if (tag != null) {
2472	      if (typeof tag === "number") return tag;
2473	      const f = GENDER_CLASSES.find(c => c.key === tag);
2474	      if (f) return f.n;
2475	    }
2476	    const lem = String(entry.lemma || ""), g = String(entry.gender), pl = String(entry.plural || "");
2477	    if (g === "mf") return 3;
2478	    if (g === "m") return (pl && /a$/.test(pl) && /o$/.test(lem)) ? 5 : 1;   // braccio -> braccia
2479	    if (g === "f") return 2;
2480	    return 1;
2481	  }
2482	  function markGenderDrill(entry, pickedClass) {
2483	    const actual = nounGenderClass(entry);
2484	    const correct = pickedClass === actual;
2485	    const bucket = (typeof LL.entryBucketId === "function")
2486	      ? LL.entryBucketId(entry, "gender", {})
2487	      : ("vocabulary.it." + entry.lemma + ".noun.gender.active");
2488	    const actualLabel = (GENDER_CLASSES[actual - 1] || {}).label || "?";
2489	    const pickedLabel = (GENDER_CLASSES[pickedClass - 1] || {}).label || String(pickedClass);
2490	    const result = {
2491	      overall: {
2492	        marks_awarded: correct ? 1 : 0, marks_possible: 1,
2493	        attempted_overall: 1, correctness_overall: correct ? 1 : 0,
2494	        status: correct ? "hit" : "miss",
2495	        summary: correct ? "Right" : ("Not quite - " + entry.lemma + " is " + actualLabel.toLowerCase())
2496	      },
2497	      raw_response: pickedLabel,
2498	      markpoints: [{
2499	        bucket: bucket, label: "Gender: " + entry.lemma,
2500	        attempted_credit: 1, correctness_credit: correct ? 1 : 0,
2501	        outcome: correct ? "hit" : "miss", evidence: pickedLabel, expected: actualLabel
2502	      }],
2503	      orthography: [],
2504	      misconceptions: []
2505	    };
2506	    if (!correct) {
2507	      const pk = (GENDER_CLASSES[pickedClass - 1] || {}).key || ("class_" + pickedClass);
2508	      // proposed id: MisconceptionAnalyst mints the canonical gender_class ids.
2509	      result.misconceptions.push({ id: "gender_class.thought_" + pk, bucket: bucket, evidence: pickedLabel, proposed: true });
2510	    }
2511	    return result;
2512	  }

2516	    host.innerHTML = "";

2521

2523	    meta.className = "meta faint";

2540	    choicesWrap.className = "mcq-choices gender-choices";
2541	    const resultHost = document.createElement("div");
2542	    const buttons = [];

2545

2549	      renderVocab(true);

2553	    next.addEventListener("click", doNext);

2555	
2556	    const commit = (picked) => {
2557	      if (marked || !picked) return;
2558	      marked = true;
2559	      const result = markGenderDrill(entry, picked);
2560	      const attempt = LL.store.recordAttempt("vocab",
2561	        { id: "genderdrill_" + (entry.rank || entry.lemma), prompt: entry.lemma },
2562	        (GENDER_CLASSES[picked - 1] || {}).label, result);
2563	      recentlyChangedBuckets = new Set(attempt.events.map(e => e.bucket));
2564	      buttons.forEach((b, i) => {
2565	        const nn = i + 1;
2566	        if (nn === actualClass) b.classList.add("gc-correct");
2567	        if (nn === picked && picked !== actualClass) b.classList.add("gc-wrong");
2568	        b.disabled = true;
2569	      });
```

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
# Check structure
if isinstance(data, list):
    entries = data
else:
    entries = data.get('entries', data.get('lemmas', []))
print(f'Total entries: {len(entries)}')
print(f'Sample keys: {list(entries[0].keys()) if entries else \"empty\"}')
# Count nouns
nouns = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun' or e.get('type') == 'noun']
print(f'Nouns: {len(nouns)}')
# Check gender_class field
with_gc = [e for e in nouns if e.get('gender_class')]
print(f'Nouns with gender_class tag: {len(with_gc)}')
# Distribution of gender_class
from collections import Counter
gc_dist = Counter(e.get('gender_class') for e in nouns if e.get('gender_class'))
print(f'Gender class distribution: {dict(gc_dist)}')
# Gender distribution
g_dist = Counter(e.get('gender') for e in nouns)
print(f'Gender distribution: {dict(g_dist)}')
" 2>&1 | head -30
```

#### Thinking

Interesting - no nouns have a `gender_class` tag yet. The heuristic in the code derives it from gender + plural fields. Let me look at what the heuristic would produce, and find the "weird" nouns (classes 3-7) versus the simple -o/-a ones.

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json, re
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])
nouns = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun']

# Replicate the nounGenderClass heuristic from app.js
def gender_class(e):
    gc = e.get('gender_class')
    if gc is not None:
        if isinstance(gc, int): return gc
        return {'masculine':1,'feminine':2,'either':3,'depends_meaning':4,'m_sg_f_pl':5,'f_sg_m_pl':6,'two_plurals':7}.get(gc, 1)
    lem = str(e.get('lemma',''))
    g = str(e.get('gender',''))
    pl = str(e.get('plural',''))
    if g == 'mf': return 3
    if g == 'm':
        if pl and re.search(r'a$', pl) and re.search(r'o$', lem):
            return 5
        return 1
    if g == 'f': return 2
    return 1

from collections import Counter
dist = Counter(gender_class(e) for e in nouns)
print('Heuristic gender class distribution:')
labels = {1:'Masculine', 2:'Feminine', 3:'Either (mf)', 4:'Depends on meaning', 5:'M sg / F pl', 6:'F sg / M pl', 7:'Two plurals'}
for k in sorted(dist):
    print(f'  Class {k} ({labels[k]}): {dist[k]}')

# The 'ambiguous' gender ones - what are they?
amb = [e for e in nouns if e.get('gender') == 'ambiguous']
print(f'\nAmbiguous gender nouns: {len(amb)}')
print('First 20:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('plural',''), e.get('translation_en','')[:30]) for e in amb[:20]])

# The 'mf' ones
mf = [e for e in nouns if e.get('gender') == 'mf']
print(f'\nMF (either) nouns: {len(mf)}')
print('Examples:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('translation_en','')[:30]) for e in mf[:15]])

# Class 5 candidates (masc -o with plural in -a)
c5 = [e for e in nouns if gender_class(e) == 5]
print(f'\nClass 5 (m sg, f pl): {len(c5)}')
print('Examples:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('plural','')) for e in c5[:20]])
" 2>&1
```

Let me fix the script to handle None translation_en values.

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json, re
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])
nouns = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun']

def gender_class(e):
    gc = e.get('gender_class')
    if gc is not None:
        if isinstance(gc, int): return gc
        return {'masculine':1,'feminine':2,'either':3,'depends_meaning':4,'m_sg_f_pl':5,'f_sg_m_pl':6,'two_plurals':7}.get(gc, 1)
    lem = str(e.get('lemma',''))
    g = str(e.get('gender',''))
    pl = str(e.get('plural',''))
    if g == 'mf': return 3
    if g == 'm':
        if pl and re.search(r'a$', pl) and re.search(r'o$', lem):
            return 5
        return 1
    if g == 'f': return 2
    return 1

from collections import Counter
dist = Counter(gender_class(e) for e in nouns)
labels = {1:'Masculine', 2:'Feminine', 3:'Either (mf)', 4:'Depends on meaning', 5:'M sg / F pl', 6:'F sg / M pl', 7:'Two plurals'}
print('Heuristic gender class distribution:')
for k in sorted(dist):
    print(f'  Class {k} ({labels[k]}): {dist[k]}')

# Ambiguous
amb = [e for e in nouns if e.get('gender') == 'ambiguous']
print(f'\nAmbiguous gender: {len(amb)} (these get class 1 by default heuristic)')
print('First 15:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('plural',''), (e.get('translation_en') or '')[:30]) for e in amb[:15]])

# MF
mf = [e for e in nouns if e.get('gender') == 'mf']
print(f'\nMF nouns: {len(mf)}')
print('Examples:', [(e['lemma'], (e.get('translation_en') or '')[:30]) for e in mf[:15]])

# Class 5
c5 = [e for e in nouns if gender_class(e) == 5]
print(f'\nClass 5 (m sg, f pl): {len(c5)}')
print('All:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('plural','')) for e in c5])

# What about nouns ending in -e? (gender unpredictable)
e_end = [e for e in nouns if str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('e') and e.get('gender') in ('m','f')]
print(f'\nNouns ending in -e (m or f): {len(e_end)}')
m_e = [e for e in e_end if e['gender']=='m']
f_e = [e for e in e_end if e['gender']=='f']
print(f'  masculine: {len(m_e)}, feminine: {len(f_e)}')

# Nouns NOT ending in -o or -a (the interesting ones for gender)
not_oa = [e for e in nouns if not str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith(('o','a')) and e.get('gender') in ('m','f','mf','ambiguous')]
print(f'\nNouns NOT ending in -o/-a: {len(not_oa)}')
" 2>&1
```

#### Thinking

Now I have a clear picture of the data. Let me summarize what I've found and address the user's requests:

1. The current heuristic only detects classes 1, 2, 3, and 5. Classes 4 (depends on meaning), 6 (f sg, m pl), and 7 (two plurals) are never detected because there's no `gender_class` tag on any noun yet.

2. The "weird" nouns that need to be identified:
   - Class 3 (either gender / mf): 104 nouns (giornalista, presidente, etc.)
   - Class 4 (depends on meaning): nouns like "fine" (il fine = purpose, la fine = end), "capitale" (il capitale = capital/money, la capitale = capital city), etc. These are currently hidden in the "ambiguous" bucket (1030) or just tagged m/f without noting the meaning distinction.
   - Class 5 (m sg, f pl): 11 nouns (braccio/braccia, dito/dita, uovo/uova, etc.)
   - Class 6 (f sg, m pl): nouns like "eco" (l'eco f → gli echi m), "mano" is actually just feminine... need to check
   - Class 7 (two plurals with different meanings): nouns like "braccio" (i bracci = arms of a machine, le braccia = human arms), "osso" (gli ossi = bones individually, le ossa = the skeleton), "corno" (i corni = musical horns, le corna = animal horns/cuckoldry)

3. The user wants:
   - UI changes: remove examples, add outline, smaller text, clearer labels
   - Data work: reduce simple -o/-a nouns in the drill, identify and tag all the "weird" ones with proper gender_class values

Actually, let me re-read their requirements more carefully:

"3 should say masculine or feminine, maybe that says M or F, depending on gender."
— Option 3 label: "M or F" (the "either" class — nouns that can be masculine or feminine depending on the person, like giornalista)

"4 says M or F with different meanings."
— Option 4 label: "M or F with different meanings" (like il fine/la fine)

"5 says masculine singular, feminine plural."
— Option 5: "Masculine singular, feminine plural" (il braccio → le braccia)

"6 says feminine singular, masculine plural."
— Option 6: "Feminine singular, masculine plural" (l'eco → gli echi)

"7 says masculine singular, masculine plural, and feminine plural with different meanings."
— Option 7: "Masculine singular, masculine plural, and feminine plural with different meanings" (il braccio, i bracci, le braccia)

Wait, option 7 is interesting. The current label is "Two plurals, diff. meaning" but the user wants it to say "masculine singular, masculine plural, and feminine plural with different meanings." That's describing nouns like braccio: il braccio (m sg), i bracci (m pl, mechanical arms), le braccia (f pl, human arms). So the noun is masculine singular, but has TWO plural forms — a masculine plural and a feminine plural — with different meanings.

Now for the data work:
- The heuristic currently only catches class 5 (m sg with -a plural). It doesn't catch 4, 6, or 7 at all.
- The 1030 "ambiguous" nouns are mostly junk (skip entries, abbreviations, foreign words) but some might be genuine class 4 nouns.
- Need to manually identify and tag the class 4, 6, and 7 nouns.

Let me think about what I can realistically do:
1. UI changes — yes, straightforward code edits
2. Identifying class 5 nouns — already done by heuristic (11 nouns)
3. Identifying class 3 nouns — already done (104 mf nouns)
4. Identifying class 4, 6, 7 nouns — this requires linguistic knowledge. I know Italian well enough to identify the main ones:
   - Class 4 (depends on meaning): il fine/la fine, il capitale/la capitale, il fronte/la fronte, il radio/la radio (actually radio is invariable), il manico/la manica, il mostro/la mostra (no, those are different words)... Let me think more carefully. Common ones: fine, capitale, fronte, radio, manico/manica, ordine (m only actually)... The classic list is: il fine (purpose) / la fine (end), il capitale (money) / la capitale (capital city), il fronte (front/movement) / la fronte (forehead), il manico (handle) / la manica (sleeve), il radio (radius bone) / la radio (radio device), il pianto (crying) / la pianta (plant)... hmm some of those are different words entirely.
   - Class 6 (f sg, m pl): l'eco (f) → gli echi (m), la mano (f, but regular plural le mani — so NOT class 6)... Actually class 6 is rare. The main example is eco. Maybe also: la specie → le specie (invariable, not class 6)...
   - Class 7 (two plurals): braccio (i bracci / le braccia), osso (gli ossi / le ossa), corno (i corni / le corna), dito (i diti / le dita — though diti is rare), ginocchio (i ginocchi / le ginocchia), labbro (i labbri / le labbra), sopracciglio (i sopraccigli / le sopracciglia), lenzuolo (i lenzuoli / le lenzuola), ciglio (i cigli / le ciglia), muro (i muri / le mura), filo (i fili / le fila), urlo (gli urli / le urla), gesto (i gesti / le gesta), membro (i membri / le membra), fondamento (i fondamenti / le fondamenta), grido (i gridi / le grida)

Actually, the distinction between class 5 and class 7 is:
- Class 5: the noun is masculine singular, and its ONLY plural is feminine (il braccio → le braccia). No masculine plural exists.
- Class 7: the noun is masculine singular, and it has BOTH a masculine plural AND a feminine plural, with different meanings (il braccio → i bracci [mechanical] / le braccia [human]).

So some of the 11 class-5 nouns might actually be class 7 (they DO have a masculine plural too, just less common). For instance:
- braccio: i bracci (of a machine/crane) / le braccia (human arms) → class 7
- osso: gli ossi (individual bones) / le ossa (the skeleton/framework) → class 7
- corno: i corni (musical instruments) / le corna (animal horns, cuckoldry) → class 7
- dito: i diti (rare, individual) / le dita (fingers collectively) → class 7 (or 5 if we consider diti archaic)
- ginocchio: i ginocchi (rare) / le ginocchia (knees) → class 7 or 5
- uovo: le uova (eggs) — no common masculine plural → class 5
- migliaio: le migliaia (thousands) — no masculine plural → class 5
- centinaio: le centinaia (hundreds) — no masculine plural → class 5
- labbro: i labbri (rare) / le labbra (lips) → class 7 or 5
- sopracciglio: i sopraccigli (rare) / le sopracciglia (eyebrows) → class 7 or 5
- lenzuolo: i lenzuoli (individual sheets) / le lenzuola (bed linen as a set) → class 7
- ciglio: i cigli (eyelashes, rare) / le ciglia (eyelashes, common; also roadside) → class 7 or 5

This is genuinely linguistic work. I can do a first pass and present it to the user for review.

I need to add a declension reveal after the answer. I'll add a helper function to derive Italian articles, and then insert the declension display into the commit function. Let me add the helper function just before `renderGenderDrillCard` and then modify the commit function to show the declension.

The articles I need to handle:
- Masculine singular: il (default), lo (before s+consonant, z, gn, ps, pn, x, y), l' (before vowel)
- Feminine singular: la (default), l' (before vowel)
- Masculine plural: i (default), gli (before s+consonant, z, gn, ps, pn, x, y, vowel)
- Feminine plural: le (always)

For the declension display, I want to show something like:
"la mano — the hand · le mani — the hands"

I need the entry's gender, lemma, plural, and translation_en. Let me write the helper and the reveal.

```json
      // proposed id: MisconceptionAnalyst mints the canonical gender_class ids.
      result.misconceptions.push({ id: "gender_class.thought_" + pk, bucket: bucket, evidence: pickedLabel, proposed: true });
    }
    return result;
  }
  function gdArticles(lemma, gender) {
    const l = (lemma || "").toLowerCase();
    const startsVowel = /^[aeiouàèéìòù]/.test(l);
    const startsImpure = /^(s[^aeiou]|z|gn|ps|pn|x|y|i[aeiou])/.test(l);
    if (gender === "f" || gender === "mf") {
      return { sg: startsVowel ? "l'" : "la", pl: "le" };
    }
    return { sg: startsVowel ? "l'" : (startsImpure ? "lo" : "il"), pl: (startsVowel || startsImpure) ? "gli" : "i" };
  }
  function renderGenderDrillCard(entry, keepAxes) {
    const host = document.getElementById("vocab-host");
    if (!host) return;
    host.innerHTML = "";
    LL._cardShownAt = Date.now();
...
```

#### Thinking

JS syntax is valid. Now let me move on to the data work — identifying and tagging the class 4/6/7 nouns. Let me also adjust the thinning so fewer simple -o/-a nouns appear.

First, let me identify the "weird" nouns that need gender_class tags. I'll write a script that:
1. Finds class 4 nouns (depends on meaning) — these are nouns where the same lemma can be masculine OR feminine with different meanings. Classic examples: il fine/la fine, il capitale/la capitale, il fronte/la fronte, il manico/la manica, il radio/la radio...
2. Finds class 6 nouns (f sg → m pl) — like eco (l'eco f → gli echi m)
3. Splits class 5 vs class 7 — some of the current class-5 nouns actually have BOTH a masculine plural and a feminine plural with different meanings (class 7)

Let me also look at what the "ambiguous" gender entries actually are — some might be genuine class 4 nouns.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The user said "we should go through and work out what all the weird ones are that need to be written in." This is linguistic work. Let me produce a curated list.

Known class 4 nouns (M or F depending on meaning):
- fine: il fine (purpose/aim) / la fine (end)
- capitale: il capitale (capital/money) / la capitale (capital city)
- fronte: il fronte (front/movement) / la fronte (forehead)
- manico: il manico (handle) / la manica (sleeve) — wait, these are different lemmas (manico vs manica). Not class 4.
- radio: il radio (radius bone) / la radio (radio device) — actually "radio" as device is feminine, as bone is masculine
- ordine: always masculine
- genere: always masculine
- clima: always masculine
- Some others: il/la cantante (depends on referent gender — but that's class 3, not 4)

Actually, class 3 vs class 4 distinction:
- Class 3: the gender changes based on the REFERENT (giornalista → il giornalista / la giornalista, same meaning "journalist")
- Class 4: the gender changes the MEANING (il fine ≠ la fine, different concepts)

Let me compile the proper list. The classic Italian "nomi che cambiano significato col genere" list:
- il fine (purpose) / la fine (end)
- il capitale (capital, money) / la capitale (capital city)
- il fronte (front, movement) / la fronte (forehead)
- il radio (radius bone) / la radio (radio)
- il manico (handle) / la manica (sleeve) — different lemmas, not class 4
- il pianto (crying) / la pianta (plant) — different lemmas
- il porto (port) / la porta (door) — different lemmas
- il pugno (fist) / la punga (slang) — not standard
- il segno (sign) / la insegna (sign/shop sign) — different lemmas (insegna)
- il frutto (fruit) / la frutta (fruit as a category) — hmm, this might be class 7 actually
- il grido (shout) / la grida (shouts, collective) — class 7 or 5
- il gesto (gesture) / la gesta (deeds) — class 7
- il membro (member) / la membra (limbs) — class 7
- il muro (wall) / la mura (walls, city walls) — class 7
- il filo (thread) / la fila (line/queue) — class 7 or different lemma
- il corno (horn) / la corna (horns, cuckoldry) — class 7
- il braccio (arm) / i bracci (mechanical arms) / le braccia (human arms) — class 7
- l'osso (bone) / gli ossi (individual bones) / le ossa (skeleton) — class 7
- il labbro (lip) / i labbri (lips, rare) / le labbra (lips) — class 7
- il ciglio (eyelash) / i cigli (rare) / le ciglia (eyelashes) — class 7
- il sopracciglio (eyebrow) / le sopracciglia (eyebrows) — class 5 (no common m pl)
- il ginocchio (knee) / le ginocchia (knees) — class 5 (i ginocchi is very rare)
- il dito (finger) / le dita (fingers) — class 5 (i diti is archaic)
- l'uovo (egg) / le uova (eggs) — class 5
- il migliaio (thousand) / le migliaia (thousands) — class 5
- il centinaio (hundred) / le centinaia (hundreds) — class 5
- il lenzuolo (sheet) / i lenzuoli (individual) / le lenzuola (bed linen) — class 7
- l'eco (echo, f) / gli echi (echoes, m) — class 6

Class 6 (feminine singular, masculine plural):
- eco: l'eco (f) → gli echi (m) — the main one
- Some sources also list: la specie → le specie (invariable, not class 6)
- Actually class 6 is extremely rare. Eco is really the only common one.

Let me also check: mano. La mano (f) → le mani (f pl). It's feminine despite ending in -o. That's class 2 (feminine) with an irregular ending, not a special gender_class. It's just an irregular noun that happens to be feminine. The gender_class system is about BEHAVIOUR, not about whether the ending is predictable. So mano is class 2 (feminine, regular plural).

Actually wait — the user's example was "il mano - the hand, le mani, the hands." They wrote "il mano" but mano is actually feminine (la mano). This is one of the irregular gender nouns — ends in -o but is feminine. In the current bucket tree, this would fall under `noun.gender.irregular`. For the gender drill, mano should be class 2 (feminine) — the drill asks "which gender is this noun?" and the answer is feminine. The irregularity is that it ends in -o but is feminine, which is exactly what the drill tests.

OK so for the data work, I need to:
1. Tag the class 4 nouns (depends on meaning) — probably 5-10 common ones
2. Tag eco as class 6
3. Split the current class-5 list: some are true class 5 (only f pl), some are class 7 (both m pl and f pl with different meanings)
4. Adjust the thinning in the deck filter

Let me also check what nouns in the vocab data match these. Let me search for the specific lemmas.

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])
nouns = {e['lemma']: e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun'}

# Check specific lemmas for class 4 (depends on meaning)
class4_candidates = ['fine', 'capitale', 'fronte', 'radio', 'manico', 'manica', 'ordine', 'genere', 'clima', 'segno', 'frutto', 'frutta']
print('=== Class 4 candidates (M or F depending on meaning) ===')
for lem in class4_candidates:
    if lem in nouns:
        e = nouns[lem]
        print(f'  {lem}: gender={e.get(\"gender\")}, plural={e.get(\"plural\")}, tr={e.get(\"translation_en\",\"\")[:50]}')
    else:
        print(f'  {lem}: NOT IN DB')

# Check class 6 candidates
print('\n=== Class 6 candidates (F sg -> M pl) ===')
for lem in ['eco', 'specie', 'superficie']:
    if lem in nouns:
        e = nouns[lem]
        print(f'  {lem}: gender={e.get(\"gender\")}, plural={e.get(\"plural\")}, tr={e.get(\"translation_en\",\"\")[:50]}')

# Check class 5/7 split
print('\n=== Current class 5 nouns (need 5 vs 7 split) ===')
import re
c5 = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos')=='noun' and str(e.get('gender'))=='m' and str(e.get('plural','')).endswith('a') and str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('o')]
for e in c5:
    print(f'  {e[\"lemma\"]}: pl={e.get(\"plural\")}, tr={e.get(\"translation_en\",\"\")[:40]}')

# Also check: nouns ending in -o that are feminine (irregular gender)
print('\n=== Nouns ending in -o but feminine (irregular, still class 2) ===')
irr = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos')=='noun' and str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('o') and e.get('gender')=='f']
print(f'  Count: {len(irr)}')
print('  First 15:', [(e['lemma'], e.get('plural','')) for e in irr[:15]])
" 2>&1
```

#### Thinking

Interesting findings:

1. **Class 4 (depends on meaning)**: The DB has these nouns but only records ONE gender per entry. For example, `fine` is tagged `f` (end), but `il fine` (purpose) is a different meaning. `capitale` is tagged `f` (capital city), but `il capitale` (money) exists. `fronte` is tagged `f` (forehead), but `il fronte` (front/movement) exists. These need to be tagged as class 4.

2. **Class 6**: `eco` is tagged as `m` in the DB, which is wrong — it's feminine (l'eco). Its plural is `gli echi` (masculine). So it's class 6: f sg → m pl. The DB has it wrong (gender=m). I need to fix that AND tag it class 6.

3. **Class 5 vs 7 split**: 
   - True class 5 (only f pl, no common m pl): uovo/uova, migliaio/migliaia, centinaio/centinaia, sopracciglio/sopracciglia, dito/dita (i diti is archaic), ginocchio/ginocchia (i ginocchi is very rare)
   - Class 7 (both m pl and f pl with different meanings): braccio (i bracci = mechanical arms / le braccia = human arms), osso (gli ossi = individual bones / le ossa = skeleton), labbro (i labbri / le labbra), lenzuolo (i lenzuoli / le lenzuola), ciglio (i cigli / le ciglia)

4. **Irregular gender (feminine ending in -o)**: mano, foto, auto, moto, radio, eco — these are class 2 (feminine) but with irregular endings. For the drill, they're still "Feminine" (class 2), but they're the interesting ones that should appear more often.

#### Thinking

Now I can see the thinning logic. Currently:
- Classes 1-2 (plain m/f): only every 20th rank is included (`v.rank % 20 !== 0` filters them out)
- Classes 3+ (interesting): always included

The user wants "a far smaller number of the simple ones that end in O and OH" — so I should tighten the thinning for class 1-2. Maybe every 50th or even 100th. And the "weird ones" (classes 3-7) should always be in play.

But there's a problem: the heuristic only detects classes 1, 2, 3, and 5. Classes 4, 6, 7 are never detected because no noun has a `gender_class` tag. So I need to:
1. Tag the nouns in the data with explicit `gender_class` values
2. Then the heuristic will pick them up (it checks `entry.gender_class` first)

2. Adjust the thinning (change % 20 to something larger, like % 50)
3. Also handle the "irregular gender" nouns (feminine ending in -o like mano) — these are class 2 but should always appear because they're tricky

Actually, for the irregular-ending nouns (mano, foto, auto, etc.), they're still class 2 (feminine) but they're interesting because they end in -o. The drill tests whether you know they're feminine. So maybe I should give them a special flag or just ensure they always appear. The simplest approach: add a `gender_class` tag of 2 to them (explicit), and modify the thinning to also include nouns where the ending doesn't match the expected pattern (feminine -o, masculine -a, etc.).

Actually, the simplest approach that matches the user's intent: 
- Thin class 1-2 much more aggressively (every 50th or 100th)
- BUT always include nouns where the ending is "surprising" (feminine -o, masculine -a, masculine -e is not surprising since -e is ambiguous)
- Always include classes 3-7

- sopracciglio → le sopracciglia

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json, os, tempfile

with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])

# Gender class assignments (QoderWork, 2026-07-21)
# Class 4: M or F depending on meaning
# Class 5: M singular, F plural (no common masculine plural)
# Class 6: F singular, M plural
# Class 7: M singular, F plural OR M plural depending on meaning
tags = {
    # Class 4 - depends on meaning
    'fine': 4,        # il fine (purpose) / la fine (end)
    'capitale': 4,    # il capitale (money) / la capitale (capital city)
    'fronte': 4,      # il fronte (front/movement) / la fronte (forehead)
    'radio': 4,       # il radio (radius bone) / la radio (radio device)
    # Class 5 - M sg -> F pl (no common m pl)
    'uovo': 5,        # l'uovo -> le uova
    'migliaio': 5,    # il migliaio -> le migliaia
    'centinaio': 5,   # il centinaio -> le centinaia
    'sopracciglio': 5, # il sopracciglio -> le sopracciglia
    'dito': 5,        # il dito -> le dita (i diti archaic)
    'ginocchio': 5,   # il ginocchio -> le ginocchia (i ginocchi rare)
    # Class 6 - F sg -> M pl
    'eco': 6,         # l'eco (f) -> gli echi (m)
    # Class 7 - two plurals, different meanings
    'braccio': 7,     # il braccio, i bracci (mechanical) / le braccia (human)
    'osso': 7,        # l'osso, gli ossi (individual) / le ossa (skeleton)
    'labbro': 7,      # il labbro, i labbri / le labbra
    'lenzuolo': 7,    # il lenzuolo, i lenzuoli / le lenzuola
    'ciglio': 7,      # il ciglio, i cigli / le ciglia
}

# Also fix eco gender (currently m, should be f) and set plural
fixes = {
    'eco': {'gender': 'f', 'plural': 'echi'},
}

tagged = 0
fixed = 0
for e in entries:
    lem = e.get('lemma', '')
    if lem in tags:
        e['gender_class'] = tags[lem]
        tagged += 1
    if lem in fixes:
        for k, v in fixes[lem].items():
            e[k] = v
        fixed += 1

print(f'Tagged {tagged} nouns with gender_class')
print(f'Fixed {fixed} nouns (gender/plural corrections)')

# Atomic write: tmp + rename
tmp = 'data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json.tmp_qw'
with open(tmp, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    json.dump(data if isinstance(data, list) else {'entries': entries}, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=None)
    f.write('\n')
os.replace(tmp, 'data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json')
print('Written (atomic replace).')
" 2>&1
```

**Output:**

```json
Tagged 23 nouns with gender_class
Fixed 2 nouns (gender/plural corrections)
Written (atomic replace).
```

```json
  function filteredVocabEntries() {
    if (vocabFilter.genderDrill) {
      // Gender drill deck: clean-gendered nouns only (exclude the 'ambiguous'
      // gap-fill junk), focused on the interesting classes (3+) with a heavily
      // thinned baseline of plain m/f. Surprising-ending nouns (fem -o, masc -a)

        const g = String(v.gender);
        if (g !== "m" && g !== "f" && g !== "mf") return false;
        const cls = nounGenderClass(v);
        if (cls >= 3) return true;  // interesting classes always in play
        // Classes 1-2: include surprising endings always, else thin to every 50th
        const lem = String(v.lemma || "");
```

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json, re
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])
nouns = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun']

def gender_class(e):
    gc = e.get('gender_class')
    if gc is not None:
        if isinstance(gc, int): return gc
        return {'masculine':1,'feminine':2,'either':3,'depends_meaning':4,'m_sg_f_pl':5,'f_sg_m_pl':6,'two_plurals':7}.get(gc, 1)
    lem = str(e.get('lemma',''))
    g = str(e.get('gender',''))
    pl = str(e.get('plural',''))
    if g == 'mf': return 3
    if g == 'm':
        if pl and re.search(r'a$', pl) and re.search(r'o$', lem): return 5
        return 1
    if g == 'f': return 2
    return 1

# Simulate the new filter
deck = []
for v in nouns:
    g = str(v.get('gender',''))
    if g not in ('m','f','mf'): continue
    cls = gender_class(v)
    if cls >= 3:
        deck.append(v); continue
    lem = str(v.get('lemma',''))
    surprising = (g == 'f' and lem.endswith('o')) or (g == 'm' and lem.endswith('a') and not lem.endswith('ista'))
    if surprising:
        deck.append(v); continue
    rank = v.get('rank')
    if isinstance(rank, int) and rank % 50 != 0: continue
    deck.append(v)

from collections import Counter
dist = Counter(gender_class(e) for e in deck)
labels = {1:'Masculine', 2:'Feminine', 3:'Either', 4:'Depends meaning', 5:'M sg/F pl', 6:'F sg/M pl', 7:'Two plurals'}
print(f'Total deck size: {len(deck)}')
for k in sorted(dist):
    print(f'  Class {k} ({labels[k]}): {dist[k]}')
print(f'\nRatio interesting/simple: {sum(v for k,v in dist.items() if k>=3)}/{sum(v for k,v in dist.items() if k<=2)}')
" 2>&1
```

**Output:**

```json
Total deck size: 459
  Class 1 (Masculine): 248
  Class 2 (Feminine): 87
  Class 3 (Either): 104
  Class 4 (Depends meaning): 7
  Class 5 (M sg/F pl): 6
  Class 6 (F sg/M pl): 2
  Class 7 (Two plurals): 5

Ratio interesting/simple: 124/335
```

Let me check how many of the 335 simple ones are surprising-ending vs truly regular -o/-a:

#### Tool: Bash

```bash
cd "C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics" && python -c "
import json, re
with open('data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
entries = data if isinstance(data, list) else data.get('entries', [])
nouns = [e for e in entries if e.get('pos') == 'noun']

def gender_class(e):
    gc = e.get('gender_class')
    if gc is not None:
        if isinstance(gc, int): return gc
        return {'masculine':1,'feminine':2,'either':3,'depends_meaning':4,'m_sg_f_pl':5,'f_sg_m_pl':6,'two_plurals':7}.get(gc, 1)
    lem = str(e.get('lemma',''))
    g = str(e.get('gender',''))
    pl = str(e.get('plural',''))
    if g == 'mf': return 3
    if g == 'm':
        if pl and re.search(r'a$', pl) and re.search(r'o$', lem): return 5
        return 1
    if g == 'f': return 2
    return 1

# Of the class 1-2 in the deck, how many are surprising-ending vs regular?
surprising_count = 0
regular_thinned = 0
for v in nouns:
    g = str(v.get('gender',''))
    if g not in ('m','f','mf'): continue
    cls = gender_class(v)
    if cls >= 3: continue
    lem = str(v.get('lemma',''))
    surprising = (g == 'f' and lem.endswith('o')) or (g == 'm' and lem.endswith('a') and not lem.endswith('ista'))
    if surprising:
        surprising_count += 1
    else:
        rank = v.get('rank')
        if isinstance(rank, int) and rank % 50 == 0:
            regular_thinned += 1

print(f'Surprising-ending (always in): {surprising_count}')
print(f'Regular -o/-a (thinned, every 50th): {regular_thinned}')
print(f'Total simple in deck: {surprising_count + regular_thinned}')
print()
print('Sample surprising fem -o:', [e['lemma'] for e in nouns if e.get('gender')=='f' and str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('o')][:12])
print('Sample surprising masc -a:', [e['lemma'] for e in nouns if e.get('gender')=='m' and str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('a') and not str(e.get('lemma','')).endswith('ista')][:12])
" 2>&1
```

```json
Regular -o/-a (thinned, every 50th): 172
Total simple in deck: 335

Sample surprising fem -o: ['mano', 'foto', 'auto', 'moto', 'radio', 'quattro', 'eco', 'eco', 'lampo', 'pallavolo', 'casco', 'o']
Sample surprising masc -a: ['problema', 'via', 'programma', 'media', 'collega', 'tema', 'papa', 'cinema', 'pianeta', 'sistema', 'schema', 'pilota']
```

#### Thinking

So the deck is:
- 163 surprising-ending nouns (feminine -o like mano/foto/auto, masculine -a like problema/programma/tema) — these are genuinely tricky and good for the drill
- 172 regular -o/-a nouns (thinned, every 50th) — these are the "easy" ones the user wants fewer of
- 124 interesting classes 3-7

Total: 459. The ratio of "interesting or surprising" to "truly regular" is 287:172, which is about 63% interesting. That's pretty good actually.

But the user said "a far smaller number of the simple ones that end in O and OH." The 172 regular ones are still there. I could thin to every 100th which would give about 86. Let me leave it at 50 for now — the user can always adjust. The key improvement is that the weird ones (classes 3-7) are always in play and the surprising-ending ones are always in play.

Done. Here's what I changed, and which files are mine:

**Files edited (QoderWork, 2026-07-21):**

1. `housing/js/app.js` — gender drill logic
2. `housing/css/style.css` — gender drill styling
3. `data/vocabulary_it_frequency.json` — gender_class tags + eco fix

**What changed:**

The drill now says "Gender drill" in the header, asks "Which gender is **mano**?" (lemma coloured), and the seven options sit inside a bordered box with small numbers and smaller text. No examples. Labels are:

1. Masculine
2. Feminine
3. M or F, depending on gender
4. M or F, depending on meaning
5. M singular, / F plural
6. F singular, / M plural
7. M singular / F plural OR M plural, / depending on meaning

After you answer, it reveals the full declension: *la mano — the hand · le mani* (with correct articles derived from gender and first letter).

The data now has 23 nouns explicitly tagged: 4 class-4 (fine, capitale, fronte, radio), 6 class-5 (uovo, migliaio, centinaio, sopracciglio, dito, ginocchio), 1 class-6 (eco — also fixed its gender from m to f), and 5 class-7 (braccio, osso, labbro, lenzuolo, ciglio). The deck thins regular -o/-a nouns to every 50th rank but always includes surprising endings (feminine -o, masculine -a) and all classes 3+.

```json
6707	  function renderEntryScreen() {
6708	    const host = document.getElementById("entry-screen");
```

#### Thinking

I can see the category expander logic at line 7189. The `open` variable determines whether a category is expanded. Currently it's open if:
- `v._catExpanded[chip.label]` is true (user manually expanded it)
- OR `v.catLabel === chip.label` (the category itself is selected)
- OR any of its subs is selected

The user wants these to "shut down again" — meaning they should default to closed. The issue is that `v._catExpanded` persists across renders (it's stored on the entrySel.vocab object). So once a user opens one, it stays open. The user wants them closed by default.

The simplest fix: change the default so that expanders are closed unless the user has explicitly opened them AND the category is currently selected. Actually, re-reading: "These cells that can open up need to shut down again." — I think they just want them to start closed when the page loads / when you first look at the vocab config. The `_catExpanded` object starts as `{}` (line 7163), so they DO start closed. But maybe the issue is that selecting a category auto-expands it (the `v.catLabel === chip.label` condition).

Actually, I think the user might be saying: after you've expanded some and then come back, they're still open and it looks messy. They want them to collapse back. The simplest fix: remove the auto-expand when a category is selected. Only expand when the user explicitly clicks the expander.

Let me change line 7189 to only use the explicit `_catExpanded` flag, not auto-expand on selection.

```json
1190	  // -------------------- accent bar (shared) --------------------
1191	  // opts.rewriteApostrophes: if false, don't auto-rewrite `e'` -> `è` etc.
1192	  // (Set this to false for English-target inputs, where "I've" should stay
1193	  // as "I've" and not get autocorrected to "Ìve".)
1194	  function buildAccentBar(input, opts) {
1195	    opts = opts || {};
1196	    const rewriteApostrophes = opts.rewriteApostrophes !== false;
1197	
1198	    const bar = document.createElement("div");
1199	    bar.className = "accent-bar";
1200	    const accents = ["à", "è", "é", "ì", "ò", "ù", "À", "È", "É", "Ì", "Ò", "Ù"];
1201	    for (const a of accents) {
1202	      const btn = document.createElement("button");
1203	      btn.type = "button";
1204	      btn.textContent = a;
1205	      btn.addEventListener("click", e => {
1206	        e.preventDefault();
1207	        const start = input.selectionStart, end = input.selectionEnd;
1208	        const v = input.value;
1209	        input.value = v.slice(0, start) + a + v.slice(end);
1210	        input.selectionStart = input.selectionEnd = start + a.length;
1211	        input.focus();
1212	      });
1213	      bar.appendChild(btn);
1214	    }
1215	    if (rewriteApostrophes) {
1216	      input.addEventListener("input", () => {
1217	        const start = input.selectionStart;
1218	        const rewritten = LL.normaliseAccentInput(input.value);
1219	        if (rewritten !== input.value) {
1220	          const delta = rewritten.length - input.value.length;
1221	          input.value = rewritten;
1222	          input.selectionStart = input.selectionEnd = Math.max(0, start + delta);
1223	        }
1224	      });
1225	      // Inline hint so users know about the apostrophe shortcut.
1226	      const hint = document.createElement("span");
1227	      hint.className = "accent-hint";
1228	      hint.textContent = "or type a' e' i' o' u' for à è ì ò ù  ·  e'' for é";
1229	      bar.appendChild(hint);
1230	    }
1231	    return bar;
1232	  }
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\norm.js`

**Output:**

```json
60	  const APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES = /(^|[^a-zà-ùA-ZÀ-Ù])(va'|da'|fa'|sta'|di')/gi;
61	  // Private-use sentinel that never appears in learner input; swapped in for the
62	  // apocope apostrophe so the accent rules (which key off ') skip it, then
63	  // restored. One char in, one char out, so overall length is preserved and the
64	  // caller's caret-delta maths is unaffected.
65	  const APOCOPE_MARK = String.fromCharCode(0xE000);
66	
67	  function normaliseAccentInput(s) {
68	    if (s == null) return "";
69	    let t = String(s);
70	    // Tradeoff: the apostrophe shortcut can no longer produce da-grave / di-grave
71	    // for these exact spellings; those stay typeable via the accent bar.
72	    t = t.replace(APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES, function (m, pre, word) {
73	      return pre + word.slice(0, -1) + APOCOPE_MARK;
74	    });
75	    for (const [re, repl] of ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE) t = t.replace(re, repl);
76	    t = t.split(APOCOPE_MARK).join("'");
77	    return t;
78	  }
79	
80	  // Core normalisation. `lower` is false for the case-preserving variant used
81	  // by case_sensitive markpoints (formal-capitalisation items: La/la, Sua/sua).
82	  // See inter_chat/Architecture_Housing_case_sensitive_markpoints.md.
83	  function normCore(s, lower) {
84	    if (s == null) return "";
85	    let t = String(s);
86	    if (lower) t = t.toLowerCase();
87	    t = t.replace(/[‘’‚‛]/g, "'")
88	         .replace(/[“”„‟]/g, '"');
89	    t = t.replace(/-/g, " ");
90	    for (const [c, e] of CONTRACTIONS) {
91	      const re = new RegExp("(^|[^a-z'])" + c.replace("'", "'") + "(?=[^a-z']|$)", "g");
92	      t = t.replace(re, "$1" + e);
93	    }
94	    for (const [re, repl] of SPELLING_FOLD) t = t.replace(re, repl);
95	    t = t.replace(/([\.,;:!?])(?=\s|$)/g, " ");
96	    t = t.replace(/[\t\n\r]+/g, " ").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
97	    // NOTE: the PreIB engine stripped a leading definite/indefinite article
98	    // (the/a/an/il/lo/la/i/gli/le/un/uno/una) plus a space from the start.
99	    // That works for physics ("the proton" == "proton") but breaks Italian:
100	    // "lo" and "la" are clitic object pronouns at least as often as articles,
101	    // and stripping turned must_not_include="lo me" into "me", which then
102	    // substring-matched "melo" and fired a false positive. Article stripping
103	    // is removed; authors must include both forms in any_phrases if they
104	    // want article-less acceptance.
105	    // Italian elision: l'amico -> l amico
106	    t = t.replace(/'/g, " ").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
107	    return t;
108	  }
109	  function norm(s) { return normCore(s, true); }
```

#### Thinking

Let me look at the ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE rules to understand the current rewriting:

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\norm.js`

**Output:**

```json
1	/* ============================================================================
2	   Normalisation pre-pass
3	   Ported from the PreIB Physics engine, with Italian-specific extensions.
4	
5	   Public API:
6	     window.LL.norm(text)               - canonical normalisation (strict)
7	     window.LL.normAccentFolded(text)   - accents folded out, for typo-tolerance
8	     window.LL.normaliseAccentInput(t)  - rewrite e' -> è, a' -> à, etc.
9	     window.LL.includesNeedle(haystack, needle)
10	     window.LL.includesAny(haystack, anyArr)
11	   ============================================================================ */
12	
13	(function () {
14	  "use strict";
15	  const LL = window.LL || (window.LL = {});
16	
17	  const CONTRACTIONS = [
18	    ["can't", "cannot"], ["doesn't", "does not"], ["isn't", "is not"],
19	    ["won't", "will not"], ["wouldn't", "would not"], ["shouldn't", "should not"],
20	    ["couldn't", "could not"], ["didn't", "did not"],
21	    ["haven't", "have not"], ["hasn't", "has not"], ["hadn't", "had not"],
22	    ["aren't", "are not"], ["weren't", "were not"]
23	  ];
24	
25	  const SPELLING_FOLD = [
26	    [/\bionize\b/g, "ionise"], [/\bionizing\b/g, "ionising"],
27	    [/\bcenter\b/g, "centre"], [/\bcolor\b/g, "colour"]
28	  ];
29	
30	  // Apostrophe-style accent input: e' -> è, e'' -> é, etc.
31	  //
32	  // The è' and È' rules are listed first because the live-rewriter fires on
33	  // every keystroke. Typing "e''" in sequence is staged as e -> e' -> è -> è'
34	  // (the second apostrophe is appended after è has already replaced e'). The
35	  // è' rule then upgrades the grave to acute so the user gets é. Without this,
36	  // typing e'' interactively gets stuck at è' because nothing matches that
37	  // two-character sequence.
38	  const ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE = [
39	    [/è'/g, "é"], [/È'/g, "É"],
40	    [/e''/g, "é"], [/e'/g, "è"],
41	    [/E''/g, "É"], [/E'/g, "È"],
42	    [/a'/g, "à"],
43	    [/i'/g, "ì"],
44	    [/o'/g, "ò"],
45	    [/u'/g, "ù"],
46	    [/A'/g, "À"],
47	    [/I'/g, "Ì"],
48	    [/O'/g, "Ò"],
49	    [/U'/g, "Ù"]
50	  ];
51	
52	  // Apocopated tu-imperatives whose trailing apostrophe is an apocope marker,
53	  // NOT an accent shortcut: va' da' fa' sta' di'. Word-start anchored so a
54	  // mid-word coincidence isn't caught. See inter_chat/
55	  // Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md.
56	  // Apocopated tu-imperatives whose trailing apostrophe is an apocope marker,
57	  // NOT an accent shortcut: va' da' fa' sta' di'. Word-start anchored so a
58	  // mid-word coincidence isn't caught. See inter_chat/
59	  // Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md.
60	  const APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES = /(^|[^a-zà-ùA-ZÀ-Ù])(va'|da'|fa'|sta'|di')/gi;
```

#### Thinking

Now I understand the current accent system:
- `e'` → è (grave)
- `e''` → é (acute) — via the staged rewriting (e' → è, then è' → é)
- Same for other vowels (a' → à, i' → ì, o' → ò, u' → ù)
- Capital versions too (E' → È, A' → À, etc.)

The user wants:
- One apostrophe = grave (already works: e' → è)
- Two apostrophes = acute (already works: e'' → é)
- Three apostrophes = literal apostrophe (NOT currently implemented)

So I need to add: `e'''` → `e'` (literal apostrophe). This means if someone types three apostrophes, they get the vowel followed by a literal apostrophe character.

- But explain that Shift-click gives a capital
- Update the hint text to explain: 1 apostrophe = grave, 2 = acute, 3 = literal apostrophe

- After first `'`: `e'` → `è` (grave fires)
- After second `'`: `è'` → `é` (acute fires)
- After third `'`: `é'` → ??? 

I need to add a rule: `é'` → `e'` (literal). And similarly for other vowels with acute + apostrophe → vowel + literal apostrophe.

Wait, but that would mean you can never type é followed by an apostrophe legitimately. Hmm. Actually in Italian, you'd never have é followed by an apostrophe in normal text. So it's safe.

Let me add: `[/é'/g, "e'"], [/á'/g, "a'"], [/í'/g, "i'"], [/ó'/g, "o'"], [/ú'/g, "u'"]` and the capital versions.

#### Thinking

Wait, I have a problem with the triple-apostrophe logic. Let me think through the staged rewriting more carefully:

Typing `e'''` interactively:
1. Type `e` → stays `e`
2. Type `'` → `e'` matches rule → becomes `è` (grave)
3. Type `'` → now we have `è'` → matches the `è'` → `é` rule (acute)
4. Type `'` → now we have `é'` → matches the new `é'` → `e'` rule (literal)

That works! But wait — there's a conflict. The rules `[/à'/g, "a'"]` etc. will fire BEFORE the `[/è'/g, "é"]` rule. So when you type `a''`:
1. Type `a` → stays `a`
2. Type `'` → `a'` matches → becomes `à` (grave)
3. Type `'` → now we have `à'` → matches the NEW `[/à'/g, "a'"]` rule → becomes `a'` (literal)

But the user wants `a''` to give acute! The problem is that for a/i/o/u, there's no acute form in the current system (only e has both grave and acute). Let me re-read the current rules:

Current rules only give acute for `e` (è' → é). For a/i/o/u, one apostrophe gives grave and that's it. There's no `a''` → á rule currently.

So the user's spec "two apostrophes for acute" only applies to `e` (since Italian only uses acute on e, and occasionally on o in some words). For a/i/o/u, there's only grave in Italian.

But wait — the user said "one apostrophe for graph, two apostrophes for acute, three apostrophes for an apostrophe." This might mean:
- For ALL vowels: 1 = grave, 2 = acute, 3 = literal
- But in Italian, á/í/ú don't really exist (only à/ì/ù for grave, and é/è for e)

Hmm, but the user might want the system to support it anyway for flexibility. Let me re-read: "one apostrophe for graph, two apostrophes for acute, three apostrophes for an apostrophe, and a single apostrophe for a single apostrophe."

I think the simplest interpretation that works for Italian:
- `e'` → è (grave), `e''` → é (acute), `e'''` → e' (literal) — this is the main one
- `a'` → à (grave), `a''` → a' (literal) — since á doesn't exist in Italian
- Same for i, o, u

But my current implementation has a problem: `[/à'/g, "a'"]` fires when you type the SECOND apostrophe after `a`, which means you can never get à followed by an apostrophe. But you also can't get á (which doesn't exist in Italian anyway). So for a/i/o/u, the sequence is:
- 1 apostrophe → grave (à)
- 2 apostrophes → literal (a')

That's actually fine for Italian! The only vowel that needs three stages is `e` (grave → acute → literal).

But wait — my rule `[/à'/g, "a'"]` will also fire if someone types `à` (from the accent button) followed by an apostrophe. That would turn `à'` into `a'`, which might not be desired. But in practice, who would type à then an apostrophe? It's an edge case.

Actually, there's a bigger problem. The rules are applied in order with `.replace()`. The rule `[/à'/g, "a'"]` is listed BEFORE `[/è'/g, "é"]`. So when processing `è'`:
- First it checks `é'` → no match (it's è', not é')
- Then `à'` → no match
- Then `ì'` → no match
- Then `ò'` → no match
- Then `ù'` → no match
- Then `è'` → MATCH → becomes `é`

OK that works. And for `é'`:
- First rule `é'` → MATCH → becomes `e'`

Good. And for `à'`:
- First rule `é'` → no match
- Second rule `à'` → MATCH → becomes `a'`

So the sequence for `a'''`:
1. `a` → `a`
2. `a'` → `à` (grave, from the `a'` → `à` rule)
3. `à'` → `a'` (literal, from the new `à'` → `a'` rule)

So for a/i/o/u, it's: 1 = grave, 2 = literal. No acute stage. That's correct for Italian.

For `e`:
1. `e'` → `è` (grave)
2. `è'` → `é` (acute)
3. `é'` → `e'` (literal)

That's the full three-stage sequence. 

But wait — there's still a problem. The `[/à'/g, "a'"]` rule will also catch the case where someone has typed `à` (from the button) and then types an apostrophe for a legitimate reason (like `città'` — though that's not a real word). In practice this is fine because Italian words ending in accented vowel + apostrophe don't really exist (the apostrophe in Italian is for elision, which comes BEFORE a vowel, not after an accented one).

Actually, I realize there might be an issue with the apocope imperatives. `va'`, `da'`, `fa'`, `sta'`, `di'` — these are protected by the APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES regex which runs first and swaps the apostrophe for a sentinel. So `va'` won't be caught by the `a'` → `à` rule. Good.

But what about `di'` → the `i'` → `ì` rule? The apocope protection handles `di'` specifically. Good.

```json
        input.focus();
      });
```

#### Thinking

Now I understand the marker setup. The worker:
- Proxies to OpenRouter at `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions`
- Default model is `deepseek/deepseek-chat` (DeepSeek V3, cheap)
- The 429 error is coming FROM OpenRouter (upstream), meaning the DeepSeek model is rate-limited on OpenRouter's side
- The worker has its own rate limiter too (line 363), but the error in the screenshot says "upstream_error" which means it's OpenRouter returning 429, not the worker's own limiter

So the issue is: OpenRouter is rate-limiting the deepseek/deepseek-chat model. This isn't a code bug — it's an external service being rate-limited. The options are:
1. Wait and retry (the rate limit will reset)
2. Switch to a different model (the worker supports multiple models via MODEL_PRICING)
3. Add a custom OpenRouter API key with higher limits

Let me check what other models are available in the worker's MODEL_PRICING map.

- A noun ending in -a with gender f → regular_a_fem

Let me think about the best approach. The fallback logic at those three lines is:
```javascript
const cls = v.noun_class || (v.gender === "m" ? "unspecified_m" : v.gender === "f" ? "unspecified_f" : "unspecified");
```

I should improve this to also consider the ending:
- ends in -o and gender m → `regular_o_masc`

Actually, let me think about what makes sense. The vocab analysis shows gender class categories. If a noun ends in -o and is masculine, it's `regular_o_masc`. If it ends in -a and is feminine, it's `regular_a_fem`. If it ends in -e, it's `e_ambiguous`. The "unspecified" ones are the truly irregular/uncategorised ones that don't fit any pattern.

So the improved fallback should be:
```javascript
const cls = v.noun_class || nounClassFallback(v);
```

Where `nounClassFallback(v)` does:
```javascript
function nounClassFallback(v) {
  const lem = String(v.lemma || "");
  const g = String(v.gender);
  if (/o$/.test(lem) && g === "m") return "regular_o_masc";
  if (/a$/.test(lem) && g === "f") return "regular_a_fem";
  if (/e$/.test(lem)) return "e_ambiguous";
  if (g === "m") return "unspecified_m";
  if (g === "f") return "unspecified_f";
  return "unspecified";
}
```

And change the labels:
- `unspecified_m` → "Masculine (other)"
- `unspecified_f` → "Feminine (other)"
- `unspecified` → "Unknown"

- `unspecified_m` → "Masculine"
- `unspecified_f` → "Feminine"
- `unspecified` → "—"

Let me also check: are there nouns ending in -o that are feminine, or -a that are masculine? Yes! Those are the "surprising endings" (fem -o like "mano", masc -a like "problema"). Those should NOT be classified as regular_o_masc or regular_a_fem. The fallback should only assign regular_o_masc if gender is m AND ends in -o, and regular_a_fem if gender is f AND ends in -a. The surprising ones (fem -o, masc -a) would fall through to unspecified_m/f, which is correct — they're irregular.

Actually wait, "mano" is feminine ending in -o. With my fallback: /o$/.test("mano") is true but g === "f" not "m", so it won't match regular_o_masc. It would fall to unspecified_f → "Feminine". That's fine.

"problema" is masculine ending in -a. /a$/.test("problema") is true but g === "m" not "f", so it won't match regular_a_fem. Falls to unspecified_m → "Masculine". Fine.

```json
  // Fallback noun-class assignment for the vocab analysis display.  QoderWork 2026-07-22
  // Classifies by ending so -o/m, -a/f and -e nouns land in the right bucket
  // instead of everything falling through to "unspecified".
  function nounClassFallback(v) {
    const lem = String(v.lemma || ""), g = String(v.gender);
    if (/o$/.test(lem) && g === "m") return "regular_o_masc";
    if (/a$/.test(lem) && g === "f") return "regular_a_fem";
    if (/e$/.test(lem)) return "e_ambiguous";
    if (g === "m") return "unspecified_m";
    if (g === "f") return "unspecified_f";
    return "unspecified";
  }

  const GENDER_CLASS_LABELS = {
    regular_o_masc:            "regular -o (m)",
    regular_a_fem:             "regular -a (f)",
    e_ambiguous:               "ends -e: some M, some F",
    greek_ma_masc:             "greek -ma (m)",
    ista_common_gender:        "-ista (common gender)",
    irregular_gender:          "irregular gender",
    gender_shift_plural:       "gender shifts in plural",
    invariable_accented_final: "invariable (final accented)",
    invariable_loanword:       "invariable loanword",
    unspecified_m:             "Masculine",          // QoderWork 2026-07-22
    unspecified_f:             "Feminine",           // QoderWork 2026-07-22
    unspecified:               "—"                   // QoderWork 2026-07-22
  };
```

#### Tool: Read

> Read: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\DISPATCH_spelling.md`

**Output:**

```json
1	# DISPATCH: SpellingAuthor (orthography beyond accent)
2	
3	**From:** Architecture, 2026-07-21 (Smith: "get more orthographic errors; spin out a chat to pick up spelling mistakes").
4	**Seat:** SpellingAuthor. **Classes:** all-seats, all-authors.
5	
6	## Remit
7	Own the SPELLING axis - non-accent Italian orthography. Three jobs:
8	1. Supply the AI translation marker's Spelling error-CLASS taxonomy (below). The marker names a class freely; this is the enumerated backbone it normalises to, and the label set the panel's Spelling category shows.
9	2. Author items that probe each class (typed + error-ID MCQ; the over-regularisation and spelling banks are natural feedstock).
10	3. Propose `orthography.spelling.*` leaves for coverage (Architecture mints; the tree today has ~9 orthography leaves, mostly accent).
11	
12	## Boundary (do not cross)
13	- **Accent** (written accents, grave/acute, accent placement) stays with **AccentAuthor** - ratified taxonomy already live. You reference it, you do not re-author it.
14	- **Stress** (spoken stress) stays with **StressAuthor**.
15	- You own everything orthographic that is NOT the accent mark itself.
16	
17	## The taxonomy (Italian spelling error classes)
18	1. **doubling** - the doppie. Missing double (bellisimo -> bellissimo; ano -> anno), spurious double (dopo -> doppo), wrong consonant doubled. Phonemic in Italian, the single biggest source.
19	2. **digraph** - gl (famiglia), gn (bagno), sc+e/i = /sh/ (pesce, scienza), gli trigraph. Confusing the digraph with a plain letter.
20	3. **c_g_softening** - hard vs soft c/g: chi/che vs ci/ce; plural amico->amici (soft) vs ricco->ricchi (add h); **-care/-gare insert h before e/i** (cercare->cerchi/cercherò, pagare->paghi/pagherò); silent-i in cia/cio/ciu, gia/gio/giu (arancia, mangio, formaggio); ce/cie, ge/gie lexical cases (cielo keeps i).
21	4. **qu_cu_cqu** - qu (quando) vs cu (cuore, scuola) vs the cqu family (acqua, acquisto). squola -> scuola.
22	5. **silent_h** - avere's h: ho/hai/ha/hanno vs o/ai/a/anno (dropped or added wrongly). Exclamatives ah/oh.
23	6. **apostrophe_elision** - elision lo/la+vowel -> l' (l'amico), una+vowel -> un' (un'amica) BUT un+masc vowel = un (no apostrophe); d'accordo; po' (truncated poco); the classic **qual è** takes NO apostrophe (qual'è is the error).
24	7. **capitalization** - Italian lowercases days (lunedì), months (gennaio), languages/nationalities (italiano). English-transfer over-capitalisation.
25	8. **vowel_confusion** - i/e, o/u in unstressed syllables; minor, lexical.
26	
27	(Accent classes - missing accent, wrong direction, placement - are AccentAuthor's; the panel shows them under the Accent category, not Spelling.)
28	
29	## Marker integration
30	The AI marker fires a Spelling markpoint with a `class` from this set + an evidence span + the correction (was -> now). Free AI wording normalises to these 8. This resolves the earlier free-vs-enumerated question: enumerated backbone, AI specifics on top.
31	
32	## Gates
33	Standard: local validation 0 errors; marker-replica if you author typed markpoints; crit-13 (no rule-naming in cues) and Rev-25 (cue citation forms at/below item level) if your items carry cues; MCQ positives full verbatim.
34	
35	## Deliverable
36	Taxonomy confirmation + refinements; an item bank per class; proposed `orthography.spelling.*` leaves (thread to Architecture to mint). Open a thread `Architecture_SpellingAuthor_batch_delivery.md`.
37	
38	## Authoring directions (READ FIRST)
39	**Primary instrument: MULTIPLE-CHOICE discrimination. Author LOTS of them - volume is the point (reps build the eye).** One contrast per item: show 3-4 spellings of the same target, one correct, the rest the plausible errors FROM its class; the learner picks. Index-scored, so no marker ambiguity.
40	
41	**Why MCQ, not typed:** the grammar marker's norm() folds the apostrophe (and partly the accent) to a space, so un' vs un, l' vs lo, città vs citta are NOT deterministically gradable when typed - "un amica" and "un'amica" collapse to the same string. MCQ sidesteps this and is the CORRECT instrument for apostrophe/accent-adjacent spelling. Where typed DOES survive norm() (doppie: bellisimo != bellissimo) typed is allowed, but MCQ stays the uniform default for volume.
42	
43	**Distractors ARE the error classes** - tag each wrong option with its class/misconception (per-choice tags, opt-in, already ruled), so a chosen distractor records WHICH slip. The drill becomes diagnostic data.
44	
45	**Worked templates (one per class):**
46	- apostrophe/elision: "the correct form:" [un amica] [un'amica] [una amica] -> **un'amica** (feminine elision takes the apostrophe: un' not un, and not the un-elided una amica)
47	- doubling: [belissimo] [bellisimo] [bellissimo] -> **bellissimo**
48	- c/g + -care h: "you look for (tu ___, cercare):" [cerci] [cerchi] [cerchii] -> **cerchi**
49	- qu/cu/cqu: [squola] [scuola] [scquola] -> **scuola**  ;  [aqua] [acqua] [acua] -> **acqua**
50	- silent h: "he has:" [a] [à] [ha] -> **ha**
51	- digraph: [familia] [famillia] [famiglia] -> **famiglia**
52	- capitalization: mid-sentence [ ... Lunedì ... ] [ ... lunedì ... ] -> **lunedì**
53	
54	Aim for many items per class across A1-C1 vocabulary. Propose orthography.spelling.<class> leaves so each class gets its own coverage cell.
55	
```

3. Use the existing MCQ marking infrastructure (buildMcqResult)
4. Sit in the vocab entry screen, similar to the gender drill

```json
856	        for (;;) {

863	            continue;
864	          }
865	          const isBucketsOnly = BUCKETS_ONLY_TOPICS.has(topic);

868	            [g, t] = await Promise.all([

870	              Foptional(`../data/translation_items_${topic}.json`)
871	            ]);
872	          }
873	          perTopic.set(topic, { tree, g, t, isBucketsOnly });
```
