```json
889	      await Promise.all(workers);
890	      const [glossary, vocab, parts, themes, misconceptions, lenses, markerMenu] = await tail;
891	
892	      // Flatten strictly in manifest order.
893	      const buckets = [];
894	      const grammar = [];
895	      const translation = [];
896	      const perTopicCounts = [];
897	      for (const topic of topics) {
898	        const r = perTopic.get(topic);

900	        for (const b of r.tree) buckets.push(b);

902	        if (r.t) for (const it of r.t) translation.push(it);
903	        perTopicCounts.push({
904	          topic,
905	          buckets: r.tree.length,
906	          grammar: r.g ? r.g.length : 0,
907	          translation: r.t ? r.t.length : 0,
```

```json
1638	      //   - the item is NOT a conjugation drill that hands the learner the

1640	      //     wrong answer is a form / spelling error of the cued verb, not a

1642	      //     present-formation batch (and future / conditional to come) ships
1643	      //     with this flag set.
1644	      //   - the outcome is a clean miss (zero marks),
1645	      //   - no must_not_include matched (catalogued wrong forms are known
1646	      //     errors the item was built to catch; no rescue),
1647	      //   - the chip has an extractable lemma AND the answer's stem doesn't

1649	      // Field renamed per Architecture_Housing_cue_placement v3/v4: the old
1650	      // name (prompt_supplies_base_form) asked a descriptive question about
1651	      // the prompt, which 1,083 items answered honestly while meaning the
1652	      // wrong thing. The new name states the author's actual claim: a wrong
1653	      // answer here can only be a form/spelling error of the cued word, so
1654	      // the cue-misread rescue must not fire. Legacy read is a tripwire, not
1655	      // a compat layer - it warns so unmigrated items surface.
```

#### Thinking

Now I have a good understanding of the architecture. Let me look at how the vocab strand renders its cards and how the gender drill mode works, so I can model the spelling driller similarly. The spelling driller should:

1. Be a mode in the vocab entry screen (like gender drill)
2. Load orthography MCQ items from the already-loaded grammar questions (they're in `LL.grammar` or similar)
3. Filter by spelling category (the 8 error classes from subtopic field: "spelling.doubling", "spelling.digraph", etc.)
4. Render MCQ cards using the existing MCQ infrastructure
5. Mark using buildMcqResult

Let me check how the grammar questions are stored and how the vocab strand currently works. I need to understand:
- Where grammar questions are stored after loading (LL.grammar?)
- How the vocab strand renders (renderVocab, renderVocabCard)
- How the gender drill mode intercepts the vocab rendering

```json
2660	    }
2661	    ensureVocabDeck();
2662	    if (!vocabDeck.length) {
2663	      const msg = document.createElement("p");
2664	      msg.className = "muted";
2665	      msg.style.cssText = "padding:18px;font-style:italic";
2666	      msg.textContent = "No entries match the current filter. Loosen the band filter above.";
2667	      host.appendChild(msg);
2668	      return;
2669	    }
2670	    const entry = vocabDeck[vocabIndex];
2671	    if (vocabFilter.genderDrill) { renderGenderDrillCard(entry, keepAxes); return; }
2672	    const effDir = (vocabFilter.direction === "mix")
2673	      ? (vocabDeckDirs[vocabIndex] || "it_en")
2674	      : vocabFilter.direction;
2675	    const isItEn = effDir === "it_en";
2676	
2677	    LL._cardShownAt = Date.now();   // attempt duration for the pulse payload
2678	    const card = document.createElement("div");
2679	    card.className = "qcard";
2680	
2681	    const meta = document.createElement("div");
2682	    meta.className = "meta faint";
2683	    const friendly = bandFriendly(entry.band);
2684	    const bits = [];
2685	    if (entry.pos) bits.push(entry.pos);
2686	    if (friendly) bits.push("band " + friendly);
2687	    if (entry.rank) bits.push("rank " + entry.rank);
2688	    meta.textContent = bits.join(" · ");
2689	    card.appendChild(meta);
2690	
2691	    // If this lemma has multiple curated entries WITH DIFFERENT POS values
2692	    // (homographs across POS), disambiguate the prompt by stating the POS.
2693	    // Saves the learner from having to guess which sense of "fisico" we mean.
2694	    //
2695	    // Same-POS-different-gender splits (fine.f vs fine.m) get article-prepend
2696	    // in IT->EN: "What does la fine mean?" not "What does fine mean?".
2697	    const sameLemmaSiblings = vocabEntries.filter(v =>
2698	      v && v.lemma === entry.lemma && v.translation_en && String(v.translation_en).trim()
2699	    );
2700	    const distinctPosCount = new Set(
2701	      sameLemmaSiblings.map(v => v.pos || "")
2702	    ).size;
2703	    const showPos = distinctPosCount > 1 && entry.pos;
2704	    // Article-prepend triggers when the lemma is part of a gender-split set
2705	    // (same lemma + same POS but multiple genders), the direction is IT->EN,
2706	    // and this entry has a definable gender. The article hard-disambiguates.
2707	    const isGenderSplitHere = (typeof LL.isGenderSplit === "function") &&
2708	      LL.isGenderSplit(entry.lemma, entry.pos);
2709	    const showArticle = isItEn && isGenderSplitHere && entry.pos === "noun" && entry.gender;
2710	    let articleForDisplay = "";
2711	    let displayedLemma = entry.lemma;
2712	    if (showArticle) {
2713	      const arts = expectedArticlesFor(entry.lemma, entry.gender);
2714	      if (arts && arts.definite && arts.definite[0]) {
2715	        articleForDisplay = arts.definite[0];
2716	        // Article ending in "'" concatenates with no space (l'amico); otherwise space.
2717	        displayedLemma = articleForDisplay.endsWith("'")
2718	          ? articleForDisplay + entry.lemma
2719	          : articleForDisplay + " " + entry.lemma;
```

```json
    drills.appendChild(dNote);
    panel.appendChild(drills);

    // ---- spelling drill: MCQ orthography practice (Smith spelling ask)  QoderWork 2026-07-22 ----
    const spDrills = document.createElement("div");
    spDrills.className = "entry-special-drills";
    const hSp = document.createElement("h3");
    hSp.className = "entry-config-head";
    hSp.textContent = "Spelling drill";
    spDrills.appendChild(hSp);
    const spRow = document.createElement("div");
    spRow.className = "entry-chip-row";
    spRow.appendChild(entryChip("Spelling drill", v.spellingDrill,
      () => { v.spellingDrill = !v.spellingDrill; renderEntryScreen(); },
      { title: "Pick the correct spelling from plausible distractors" }));
    spDrills.appendChild(spRow);
    // Category chips (the 8 spelling error classes)
    if (v.spellingDrill) {
      const catRow = document.createElement("div");
      catRow.className = "entry-chip-row";
      catRow.style.cssText = "flex-wrap:wrap";
      catRow.appendChild(entryChip("All classes", !v.spellingClass,
        () => { v.spellingClass = ""; renderEntryScreen(); }));
      for (const sc of SPELLING_CLASSES) {
        catRow.appendChild(entryChip(sc.label, v.spellingClass === sc.id,
          () => { v.spellingClass = (v.spellingClass === sc.id ? "" : sc.id); renderEntryScreen(); }));
      }
      spDrills.appendChild(catRow);
    }
    const spNote = document.createElement("p");
    spNote.className = "entry-config-hint";
    spNote.textContent = v.spellingDrill
      ? "multiple-choice spelling discrimination; pick the correct form from plausible errors"
      : "uses the SpellingAuthor's MCQ bank across 8 error classes";
    spDrills.appendChild(spNote);
    panel.appendChild(spDrills);

    appendStart(panel, () => startVocabSession());
  }
```

```json
    vocabFilter.spellingDrill = !!v.spellingDrill;   // QoderWork 2026-07-22
    vocabFilter.spellingClass = v.spellingClass || "";   // QoderWork 2026-07-22
    vocabFilter.direction = v.genderDrill ? "en_it" : v.direction;   // gender drill: direction unused by the classifier
    // The builder's frequency selection replaces the legacy subBand/topN caps.
    vocabFilter.subBand = "";
    vocabFilter.topN = 0;
    vocabFilter.rankRange = null;
    vocabFilter.rankRanges = (Array.isArray(v.rankRanges) && v.rankRanges.length)
      ? v.rankRanges.map(r => ({ start: r.start, end: r.end })) : null;
    vocabFilter.themes = (v.catIds && v.catIds.length) ? v.catIds.slice() : null;
    vocabFilter.theme = (v.catIds && v.catIds.length === 1) ? v.catIds[0] : "";  // single-id picks light the in-session themes axis
    vocabDeck = []; vocabDeckDirs = []; vocabIndex = 0;
    if (vocabFilter.spellingDrill) buildSpellingDeck();   // QoderWork 2026-07-22
    saveLastSession("vocab");
    track("session_start", { strand: "vocab", source: "welcome", direction: vocabFilter.direction, gender_drill: vocabFilter.genderDrill, spelling_drill: vocabFilter.spellingDrill, spelling_class: vocabFilter.spellingClass, lens: v.lens || "numeric", category: v.catLabel || "", ranges: (v.rankRanges || []).map(r => r.start + "-" + r.end).join("+") });
    renderVocab();
    showStrand("vocab");
  }

  // ---- translation config (grammar-point narrowing when available; else just start) ----
...
```

#### Thinking

Now I'll insert the spelling drill functions right after renderGenderDrillCard (after line 2660) and before renderVocab. I need:

1. `spellingDeck` and `spellingIndex` variables
2. `buildSpellingDeck()` - filters grammarQuestions to orthography/spelling items, optionally by class
3. `renderSpellingDrillCard(keepAxes)` - renders an MCQ card for the current spelling item

The spelling items are MCQ items from grammarQuestions with topic === "orthography" and subtopic starting with "spelling.". They have choices, answer_index, choice_tags, explanation, markpoints with bucket "orthography.spelling.<class>".

For marking, I can reuse `buildMcqResult(q, pickedIdx)` which already handles MCQ scoring including choice_tags misconception firing (that was added in r68).

Let me write the code.


#### Tool: Edit

> Modified: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js`

**Output:**

```json
Successfully modified file: C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js (1 replacements). Here is the updated code:
...
    host.appendChild(card);
    setTimeout(() => { ensureCardVisible(card); card.focus(); }, 0);
    if (!keepAxes) renderVocabAxes();
  }

  // ---- Spelling drill (Smith spelling ask; SpellingAuthor MCQ bank)  QoderWork 2026-07-22 ----
  let spellingDeck = [];
  let spellingIndex = 0;

  function buildSpellingDeck() {
    const cls = vocabFilter.spellingClass;
    spellingDeck = shuffle(grammarQuestions.filter(q =>
      q.topic === "orthography" &&
      typeof q.subtopic === "string" && q.subtopic.indexOf("spelling.") === 0 &&
      q.type === "mcq" &&
      (!cls || q.subtopic === "spelling." + cls)
    ));
    spellingIndex = 0;
  }

  function renderSpellingDrillCard(keepAxes) {
    const host = document.getElementById("vocab-host");
    if (!host) return;
    host.innerHTML = "";
    if (!spellingDeck.length) {
      const msg = document.createElement("p");
      msg.className = "muted";
      msg.style.cssText = "padding:18px;font-style:italic";
      msg.textContent = "No spelling items match the current filter.";
      host.appendChild(msg);
      return;
    }
    if (spellingIndex >= spellingDeck.length) { spellingDeck = shuffle(spellingDeck); spellingIndex = 0; }
    const q = spellingDeck[spellingIndex];
    LL._cardShownAt = Date.now();

    const card = document.createElement("div");
    card.className = "qcard spelling-drill-card";
    card.tabIndex = -1;

    // Meta line: class label
    const clsId = (q.subtopic || "").replace("spelling.", "");
    const clsLabel = (SPELLING_CLASSES.find(c => c.id === clsId) || {}).label || clsId;
    const meta = document.createElement("div");
    meta.className = "meta faint";
    meta.textContent = "Spelling drill · " + clsLabel;
    card.appendChild(meta);

    // Prompt
    const prompt = document.createElement("div");
    prompt.className = "prompt";
    prompt.textContent = q.prompt || "Choose the correct spelling:";
    card.appendChild(prompt);

    // Choices
    const choicesWrap = document.createElement("div");
    choicesWrap.className = "mcq-choices spelling-choices";
    const resultHost = document.createElement("div");
    const buttons = [];
    let marked = false;
    let selectedIdx = null;

    const doNext = () => {
      spellingIndex++;
      if (spellingIndex >= spellingDeck.length) { spellingDeck = shuffle(spellingDeck); spellingIndex = 0; }
      renderVocab(true);
    };
    const next = document.createElement("button");
    next.type = "button"; next.className = "btn"; next.textContent = "Skip"; next.title = "Next item";
    next.addEventListener("click", doNext);
    next.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => { if (e.key === "Enter") { e.preventDefault(); doNext(); } });

    const commit = (pickedIdx) => {
      if (marked) return;
      marked = true;
      const result = buildMcqResult(q, pickedIdx);
      const attempt = LL.store.recordAttempt("vocab",
        { id: q.external_id || ("spelling_" + spellingIndex), prompt: q.prompt },
        q.choices[pickedIdx], result);
      recentlyChangedBuckets = new Set(attempt.events.map(e => e.bucket));
      buttons.forEach((b, i) => {
        if (i === q.answer_index) b.classList.add("gc-correct");
        if (i === pickedIdx && pickedIdx !== q.answer_index) b.classList.add("gc-wrong");
        b.disabled = true;
      });
      resultHost.innerHTML = "";
      resultHost.appendChild(renderResult(result));
      // Show explanation if present
      if (q.explanation) {
        const expl = document.createElement("div");
        expl.className = "gd-declension";
        expl.textContent = q.explanation;
        resultHost.appendChild(expl);
      }
      renderLiveStats();
      next.textContent = "Next";
      setTimeout(() => next.focus({ preventScroll: true }), 0);
    };

    (q.choices || []).forEach((ch, i) => {
      const b = document.createElement("button");
      b.type = "button";
      b.className = "mcq-choice spelling-choice";
      const num = document.createElement("span"); num.className = "gc-num"; num.textContent = i + 1;
      const lbl = document.createElement("span"); lbl.className = "gc-label"; lbl.textContent = ch;
      b.appendChild(num); b.appendChild(lbl);
      b.addEventListener("click", () => commit(i));
      choicesWrap.appendChild(b);
      buttons.push(b);
    });
    card.appendChild(choicesWrap);

    const actions = document.createElement("div");
    actions.className = "actions";
    actions.appendChild(next);
    const hint = document.createElement("span");
    hint.className = "kbd-hint";
    hint.textContent = "Press 1-" + (q.choices || []).length + " or click. Enter to advance.";
    actions.appendChild(hint);
    card.appendChild(actions);
    card.appendChild(resultHost);

    card.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
      if (marked) { if (e.key === "Enter") { e.preventDefault(); doNext(); } return; }
      const n = parseInt(e.key, 10);
      if (n >= 1 && n <= (q.choices || []).length) { e.preventDefault(); commit(n - 1); }
    });

    host.appendChild(card);
    setTimeout(() => { ensureCardVisible(card); card.focus(); }, 0);
    if (!keepAxes) renderVocabAxes();
  }

  function renderVocab(keepAxes) {
    renderVocabFilterBar();
    const host = document.getElementById("vocab-host");
    if (!host) return;
    host.innerHTML = "";
...
```

```json
.gd-declension { margin-top: 8px; padding: 8px 12px; font-size: 15px; background: var(--cream, #faf9f6); border-radius: 6px; border: 1px solid var(--border); }

/* Spelling drill: MCQ discrimination choices.  QoderWork 2026-07-22 */
.spelling-choices { border: 1.5px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; padding: 8px; background: var(--cream, #faf9f6); }
.spelling-choice { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 6px; text-align: left; font-size: 15px; padding: 7px 10px; }
.spelling-choice .gc-num { font-weight: 700; color: var(--accent); font-size: 10px; min-width: 1.1em; opacity: 0.7; }
.spelling-choice .gc-label { font-weight: 600; line-height: 1.3; }
.spelling-choice.gc-correct { outline: 2px solid #2e7d4f; background: rgba(46,125,79,0.09); }
.spelling-choice.gc-wrong { outline: 2px solid #b03030; background: rgba(176,48,48,0.09); }

/* Tick row */
.freq-tick-row {
  display: flex;
  align-items: flex-start;
  gap: 4px;
...
```

Actually, looking at my code:
```javascript
function renderVocab(keepAxes) {
    renderVocabFilterBar();
    const host = document.getElementById("vocab-host");
    if (!host) return;
    host.innerHTML = "";
    // Spelling drill: uses its own MCQ deck, not vocab entries.
    if (vocabFilter.spellingDrill) { renderSpellingDrillCard(keepAxes); return; }
```

So renderVocabFilterBar() IS called before the spelling drill branch. That's fine — it'll show the vocab filter bar which may not be relevant for spelling drill. But it's harmless. Actually, the gender drill also shows the filter bar (it branches after ensureVocabDeck). So this is consistent.

2. The `renderVocabAxes()` call at the end of renderSpellingDrillCard — this renders the vocab heatmap axes. For spelling drill, this might not be relevant. But the gender drill also calls it. Let me keep it for consistency — it shows the vocab practice history which is still relevant context.

Actually wait, for the gender drill, the axes show gender class practice history. For spelling drill, the axes would show... vocab frequency/theme/gender axes which aren't relevant to spelling. Let me think about whether to skip the axes for spelling drill.

Looking at the gender drill: `if (!keepAxes) renderVocabAxes();` — it renders the vocab axes. The axes show the frequency grid, gender axis, and theme axis. For the gender drill, the gender axis is relevant. For spelling drill, none of the axes are directly relevant. But they're not harmful either — they show the learner's overall vocab practice history.

I'll keep it for now. If Smith wants it removed, it's a one-line change.

3. The `renderLiveStats()` call in commit — this updates the live stats panel. Good, spelling practice should update stats.

4. The `recentlyChangedBuckets` variable — used for the pulse animation. Should work fine.

Done. Here's what shipped this round (all QoderWork 2026-07-22, syntax-verified):

**Uncategorised noun cleanup** — new `nounClassFallback()` helper classifies nouns by ending: -o/m → "regular -o (m)", -a/f → "regular -a (f)", -e → "ends -e: some M, some F". Only truly irregular nouns (fem -o like *mano*, masc -a like *problema*, consonant-final, etc.) fall through, and their labels now just say "Masculine" / "Feminine" / "—" instead of "uncategorised (m/f)". All three fallback sites updated.

**Spelling driller** — new vocab-strand drill mode using the SpellingAuthor's 141 MCQ items. On the entry screen, below Gender drill: a toggle chip + 8 category chips (Doubling, Digraphs, C/G softening, qu/cu/cqu, Silent h, Apostrophe & elision, Capitalization, Vowel confusion). In-session: numbered MCQ cards with keyboard shortcuts (1–N), correct/wrong highlighting, explanation reveal after marking, and `buildMcqResult` scoring — so the r68 choice_tags misconception firing inherits automatically. Mutually exclusive with gender drill. CSS styled to match the gender drill's bordered-box look.

```json
590	  const STRIP_STRANDS = ["grammar", "translation"];
591	  function buildPulseStrip(strandName) {
592	    // Mounted in the stats pane's sticky header now (Smith 2026-07-21): the
593	    // vocab strip belongs top-right and must not vanish on scroll. One shared strip.
594	    const host = document.querySelector("#live-stats .live-header");
595	    if (!host) return null;
596	    let strip = host.querySelector(".pulse-strip");
597	    if (strip) return strip;
598	    strip = document.createElement("div");
599	    strip.className = "pulse-strip";
600	    const cap = document.createElement("span");
601	    cap.className = "strip-caption";
602	    cap.textContent = "Vocabulary";
603	    strip.appendChild(cap);
604	    const vocabMap = document.createElement("span");
605	    vocabMap.className = "strip-vocab";
606	    vocabMap.title = "Words 1\u20135,000 in five boxes of 1,000, then 1,000-word slivers to 20,000. Click for your vocab coverage.";
607	    vocabMap.addEventListener("click", () => showStrand("vocab"));
608	    for (let b = 0; b < 5; b++) {
609	      const box = document.createElement("span");
610	      box.className = "strip-box";
611	      for (let c = 0; c < 9; c++) {
612	        const idx = b * 9 + c;
613	        const cell = document.createElement("i");
614	        cell.className = "strip-cell";
615	        cell.dataset.idx = String(idx);
616	        cell.title = "words ~" + (Math.round(idx * STRIP_CELL_SPAN) + 1) + "\u2013" + Math.round((idx + 1) * STRIP_CELL_SPAN);
617	        box.appendChild(cell);
618	      }
619	      vocabMap.appendChild(box);
620	    }
621	    const slivers = document.createElement("span");
622	    slivers.className = "strip-slivers";
623	    for (let i2 = 0; i2 < 15; i2++) {
624	      const sl = document.createElement("i");
625	      sl.className = "strip-sliver";
626	      sl.dataset.idx = String(i2);
627	      sl.title = "words " + (5001 + i2 * 1000) + "\u2013" + (5000 + (i2 + 1) * 1000);
628	      slivers.appendChild(sl);
629	    }
630	    vocabMap.appendChild(slivers);
631	    strip.appendChild(vocabMap);
632	    const mk = (cls, label, title) => {
633	      const el = document.createElement("span");
634	      el.className = "strip-mini " + cls;
635	      el.textContent = label;
636	      el.title = title;
637	      strip.appendChild(el);
638	    };
639	    mk("strip-acc", "ACC", "Accents: fires green when an accented answer lands right, amber on a slip.");
640	    mk("strip-str", "STR", "Stress: waiting for the stress drill - this light will fire when it exists.");
641	    mk("strip-gen", "GEN", "Gender: fires with each gender verdict.");
642	    host.insertBefore(strip, host.firstChild);
643	    return strip;
644	  }
645	  function eachStrip(fn) {
646	    for (const s of STRIP_STRANDS) {
647	      const strip = buildPulseStrip(s);
648	      if (strip) fn(strip);
649	    }
650	  }
651	  function paintStripBase() {
652	    const spans = new Set();
653	    for (const a of ((LL.state && LL.state.attempts) || [])) {
654	      for (const ev of (a.events || [])) {
655	        if (typeof ev.bucket === "string" && ev.bucket.indexOf("vocabulary.it.") === 0) {
656	          const cell = stripCellForRank(lemmaMinRank(ev.bucket.split(".")[2]));
657	          if (cell) spans.add(cell.kind + cell.index);
658	        }
659	      }
660	    }
661	    eachStrip(strip => {
662	      strip.querySelectorAll(".strip-cell").forEach(el => el.classList.toggle("seen", spans.has("cell" + el.dataset.idx)));
663	      strip.querySelectorAll(".strip-sliver").forEach(el => el.classList.toggle("seen", spans.has("sliver" + el.dataset.idx)));
664	    });
665	  }
666	  function pulseStripFlash() {
667	    const a = LL.lastAttempt;
668	    if (!a) return;
669	    const doFlash = (el, cls) => {
670	      if (!el) return;
671	      el.classList.remove("flash-good", "flash-bad", "flash-warm");
672	      void el.offsetWidth;
673	      el.classList.add(cls);
674	    };
675	    let sawAccentRight = false, sawSlip = false, genderGood = null;
676	    const vocabFires = [];
677	    for (const ev of (a.events || [])) {
678	      const b = String(ev.bucket || "");
679	      if (b.indexOf("vocabulary.it.") === 0) {
680	        const cell = stripCellForRank(lemmaMinRank(b.split(".")[2]));
681	        if (cell) vocabFires.push({ cell, good: (ev.correctness_credit || 0) > 0 });
682	        if (b.indexOf(".gender") >= 0) {
683	          const right = (ev.correctness_credit || 0) > 0;
684	          genderGood = (genderGood === null) ? right : (genderGood && right);
685	        }
686	      } else if (b.indexOf("orthography.") === 0) {
687	        sawSlip = true;
688	      } else {
689	        const right = (ev.correctness_credit || 0) >= (ev.attempted_credit || 1);
690	        if (right && ACCENTED_STRIP_RE.test(String(ev.evidence || ""))) sawAccentRight = true;
691	      }
692	    }
693	    eachStrip(strip => {
694	      for (const f of vocabFires) {
695	        const el = strip.querySelector(f.cell.kind === "cell"
696	          ? '.strip-cell[data-idx="' + f.cell.index + '"]'
697	          : '.strip-sliver[data-idx="' + f.cell.index + '"]');
698	        doFlash(el, f.good ? "flash-good" : "flash-bad");
699	        if (el) el.classList.add("seen");
700	      }
701	      if (sawSlip) doFlash(strip.querySelector(".strip-acc"), "flash-warm");
702	      else if (sawAccentRight) doFlash(strip.querySelector(".strip-acc"), "flash-good");
703	      if (genderGood !== null) doFlash(strip.querySelector(".strip-gen"), genderGood ? "flash-good" : "flash-bad");
704	    });
705	  }
706	  function renderPulseStrip() {
707	    paintStripBase();
708	    pulseStripFlash();
709	  }
```

```json
  const ACCENTED_STRIP_RE = /[\u00e0\u00e8\u00e9\u00ec\u00f2\u00f9\u00c0\u00c8\u00c9\u00cc\u00d2\u00d9]/;
  const STRIP_STRANDS = ["grammar", "translation"];
  // 9-box pulse strip: 5 vocab frequency boxes (1k-5k) + ACC + STR + GEN + SP.  QoderWork 2026-07-22
  // Each box shows a gradient background reflecting mastery, and fires individually on relevant events.
  const STRIP_BOX_DEFS = [
    { id: "vocab1", label: "1k",    kind: "vocab", lo: 1,    hi: 1000 },
    { id: "vocab2", label: "2k",    kind: "vocab", lo: 1001, hi: 2000 },
    { id: "vocab3", label: "3k",    kind: "vocab", lo: 2001, hi: 3000 },
    { id: "vocab4", label: "4k",    kind: "vocab", lo: 3001, hi: 4000 },
    { id: "vocab5", label: "5k",    kind: "vocab", lo: 4001, hi: 5000 },
    { id: "acc",    label: "ACC",   kind: "accent" },
    { id: "str",    label: "STR",   kind: "stress" },
    { id: "gen",    label: "GEN",   kind: "gender" },
    { id: "sp",     label: "SP",    kind: "spelling" }
  ];
  function buildPulseStrip(strandName) {
    const host = document.querySelector("#live-stats .live-header");
    if (!host) return null;
    let strip = host.querySelector(".pulse-strip");
    if (strip) return strip;
    strip = document.createElement("div");
    strip.className = "pulse-strip";
    for (const def of STRIP_BOX_DEFS) {
      const box = document.createElement("div");
      box.className = "strip-box9 strip-" + def.id;
      box.dataset.kind = def.kind;
      box.dataset.id = def.id;
      if (def.kind === "vocab") { box.dataset.lo = def.lo; box.dataset.hi = def.hi; }
      box.title = def.kind === "vocab"
        ? "Words " + def.lo + "\u2013" + def.hi + ": click for vocab coverage"
        : def.label + " mastery";
      if (def.kind === "vocab") box.addEventListener("click", () => showStrand("vocab"));
      const lbl = document.createElement("span");
      lbl.className = "strip-box9-label";
      lbl.textContent = def.label;
      box.appendChild(lbl);
      strip.appendChild(box);
    }
    host.insertBefore(strip, host.firstChild);
    return strip;
  }
  function eachStrip(fn) {
    for (const s of STRIP_STRANDS) {
      const strip = buildPulseStrip(s);
      if (strip) fn(strip);
    }
  }
  // Mastery ratio for a set of events: correctness of attempted.
  function stripMastery(events) {
    let att = 0, cor = 0;
    for (const ev of events) {
      att += (ev.attempted_credit || 1);
      cor += (ev.correctness_credit || 0);
    }
    return att > 0 ? cor / att : 0;
  }
  function paintStripBase() {
    const allEvents = [];
    for (const a of ((LL.state && LL.state.attempts) || [])) {
      for (const ev of (a.events || [])) allEvents.push(ev);
    }
    // Bucket events by box kind.
    const byBox = {};
    for (const def of STRIP_BOX_DEFS) byBox[def.id] = [];
    for (const ev of allEvents) {
      const b = String(ev.bucket || "");
      if (b.indexOf("vocabulary.it.") === 0) {
        const lemma = b.split(".")[2];
        const rank = lemmaMinRank(lemma);
        if (rank != null) {
          for (const def of STRIP_BOX_DEFS) {
            if (def.kind === "vocab" && rank >= def.lo && rank <= def.hi) { byBox[def.id].push(ev); break; }
          }
        }
        if (b.indexOf(".gender") >= 0) byBox.gen.push(ev);
      } else if (b.indexOf("orthography.accent") === 0) {
        byBox.acc.push(ev);
      } else if (b.indexOf("orthography.spelling") === 0) {
        byBox.sp.push(ev);
      }
      // STR: no events yet (stress drill not built)
    }
    eachStrip(strip => {
      for (const def of STRIP_BOX_DEFS) {
        const el = strip.querySelector('.strip-box9[data-id="' + def.id + '"]');
        if (!el) continue;
        const m = stripMastery(byBox[def.id]);
        const n = byBox[def.id].length;
        // Gradient: base cream -> green by mastery; opacity scales with attempts.
        const alpha = n === 0 ? 0 : Math.min(0.15 + m * 0.55, 0.7);
        el.style.background = n === 0
          ? ""
          : "linear-gradient(to top, rgba(47,143,78," + alpha.toFixed(2) + ") 0%, rgba(47,143,78," + (alpha * 0.3).toFixed(2) + ") 100%)";
        el.title = (def.kind === "vocab"
          ? "Words " + def.lo + "\u2013" + def.hi
          : def.label) + ": " + n + " events, " + Math.round(m * 100) + "% correct";
      }
    });
  }
  function pulseStripFlash() {
    const a = LL.lastAttempt;
    if (!a) return;
...
      if (!el) return;
      el.classList.remove("flash-good", "flash-bad", "flash-warm");
      void el.offsetWidth;
      el.classList.add(cls);
    };
    // Collect which boxes fire.
    const vocabFires = {};   // boxId -> good|bad
    let accFire = null, genFire = null, spFire = null;
    for (const ev of (a.events || [])) {
      const b = String(ev.bucket || "");
      const good = (ev.correctness_credit || 0) > 0;
      if (b.indexOf("vocabulary.it.") === 0) {
        const lemma = b.split(".")[2];
        const rank = lemmaMinRank(lemma);
        if (rank != null) {
          for (const def of STRIP_BOX_DEFS) {
            if (def.kind === "vocab" && rank >= def.lo && rank <= def.hi) {
              vocabFires[def.id] = (vocabFires[def.id] === "bad") ? "bad" : (good ? "good" : "bad");
              break;
            }
          }
        }
        if (b.indexOf(".gender") >= 0) genFire = (genFire === "bad") ? "bad" : (good ? "good" : "bad");
      } else if (b.indexOf("orthography.accent") === 0) {
        accFire = good ? "good" : "warm";
      } else if (b.indexOf("orthography.spelling") === 0) {
        spFire = good ? "good" : "bad";
      }
    }
    eachStrip(strip => {
      for (const [id, verdict] of Object.entries(vocabFires)) {
        doFlash(strip.querySelector('.strip-box9[data-id="' + id + '"]'), verdict === "good" ? "flash-good" : "flash-bad");
      }
      if (accFire) doFlash(strip.querySelector('.strip-box9[data-id="acc"]'), accFire === "good" ? "flash-good" : "flash-warm");
      if (genFire) doFlash(strip.querySelector('.strip-box9[data-id="gen"]'), genFire === "good" ? "flash-good" : "flash-bad");
      if (spFire) doFlash(strip.querySelector('.strip-box9[data-id="sp"]'), spFire === "good" ? "flash-good" : "flash-bad");
    });
  }
  function renderPulseStrip() {
    paintStripBase();
    pulseStripFlash();
```

#### Thinking

Smith has given me a lot of feedback from looking at the live app. Let me parse through his stream-of-consciousness instructions:

1. **Gender drill card too big** — "it's just a bit too big. The gender drill, for a number of reasons, should get level with the competition, with the questions on the left." — The gender drill card should be more compact, aligned with other question cards, with questions on the left side.

2. **Vocabulary should shift up** — The vocab content should move up (less whitespace at top?).

3. **Feedback on the left not visible** — "The feedback on the left: you just don't see it. You see masculine, and you don't see what it says, basically what it means. We go through it too fast to tell." — After answering the gender drill, the feedback/explanation is not prominent enough. You see "Masculine" but don't see the explanation of what it means.

4. **Gender axes stats** — "They all say 'average 20%,' which is just... something nobody will understand. I think we just run some ticks and crosses through it, and still it will colour if we get enough words. We just run ticks and crosses through those things" — The gender class stats showing "avg 20%" are confusing. Instead of showing average percentage, show ticks (✓) and crosses (✗) for correct/incorrect answers. The cells should still colour with enough data.

5. **Gender axes shouldn't start on this** — "it shouldn't start on this. I didn't know if it did or it was just me going back to where I started." — The gender axes view might be scrolling to a position that's confusing, or the initial view shouldn't default to showing the gender breakdown.

6. **Can't access spelling/gender drill mid-session** — "It doesn't look like one can access the spelling drill or the gender drill unless you go there from the front screen, and that's not right." — Need a way to switch to spelling/gender drill from within an active vocab session (not just from the entry/welcome screen).

7. **Thin white line above the bow cap** — "We also have a little thin white line above the bow cap." — There's a visual artifact (thin white line) above something (probably the header or a card). "Bow cap" might be a voice-to-text garble — maybe "below cap" or "the top" or "the box cap"?

8. **Spelling drill header gives away the answer** — "On the spelling drill, you can't give away what it's about on the top there" — The spelling drill header shows "Spelling drill · Digraphs (gl/gn/sc)" which tells the learner what error class they're dealing with, potentially giving away the answer pattern. Need to hide or genericize the class label during the drill.

9. **Spelling drill answer position bias** — "We need a split of all the misconceptions. It all seems that they are all number two. The right answer was number 2 for lots and lots of the right ones." — The correct answer is always in position 2 (or heavily biased). #### Thinking

I can see the issue. The choices are rendered in their original order from the data (`q.choices`), and `q.answer_index` points to the correct one. If the data always has the correct answer at index 1 (position 2), then it'll always be in position 2.

The fix is to shuffle the choices at render time, keeping track of where the correct answer ended up. I need to:
1. Create a shuffled index array
2. Render choices in shuffled order
3. Track the new position of the correct answer
4. Update the `commit` function to use the shuffled mapping

Let me also look at how `buildMcqResult` works to make sure I pass the right index.

#### Thinking

I see — `buildMcqResult` uses `q.answer_index` and `q.choices[pickedIdx]` directly. So if I shuffle the choices, I need to either:
1. Create a shuffled copy of the question with updated `answer_index` and `choices`, OR
2. Map the picked display index back to the original index before calling `buildMcqResult`

Option 2 is cleaner — I'll create a shuffle mapping at render time, display choices in shuffled order, and when the user picks display position `i`, I map it back to the original index before calling `buildMcqResult`.

Now I have the full picture. I need to:

1. Create a shuffled order for the choices
2. Render them in shuffled order
3. Map the display index back to the original index when committing
4. Update the correct/wrong highlighting to use the shuffled positions

Let me rewrite the choices section. The key changes:
- Before rendering choices, create `order` = shuffled array of indices [0, 1, ..., n-1]
- Render choices in `order` sequence
- When user picks display position `displayIdx`, the original index is `order[displayIdx]`
- For highlighting: the correct answer's display position is `order.indexOf(q.answer_index)`

```json
    const next = document.createElement("button");
    next.type = "button"; next.className = "btn"; next.textContent = "Skip"; next.title = "Next item";
    next.addEventListener("click", doNext);
    next.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => { if (e.key === "Enter") { e.preventDefault(); doNext(); } });

    // Shuffle choice positions so the correct answer isn't always #2.  QoderWork 2026-07-22
    const choiceCount = (q.choices || []).length;
    const order = shuffle([...Array(choiceCount).keys()]); // e.g. [1,0] or [0,2,1]
    const correctDisplayIdx = order.indexOf(q.answer_index);

    const commit = (displayIdx) => {
      if (marked) return;
      marked = true;
      const origIdx = order[displayIdx];
      const result = buildMcqResult(q, origIdx);
      const attempt = LL.store.recordAttempt("vocab",
        { id: q.external_id || ("spelling_" + spellingIndex), prompt: q.prompt },
        q.choices[origIdx], result);
      recentlyChangedBuckets = new Set(attempt.events.map(e => e.bucket));
      buttons.forEach((b, i) => {
        if (i === correctDisplayIdx) b.classList.add("gc-correct");
        if (i === displayIdx && displayIdx !== correctDisplayIdx) b.classList.add("gc-wrong");
        b.disabled = true;
      });
      resultHost.innerHTML = "";
      resultHost.appendChild(renderResult(result));
      // Show explanation if present
...
      renderLiveStats();
      next.textContent = "Next";
      setTimeout(() => next.focus({ preventScroll: true }), 0);
    };

    order.forEach((origIdx, displayIdx) => {
      const b = document.createElement("button");
      b.type = "button";
      b.className = "mcq-choice spelling-choice";
      const num = document.createElement("span"); num.className = "gc-num"; num.textContent = displayIdx + 1;
      const lbl = document.createElement("span"); lbl.className = "gc-label"; lbl.textContent = q.choices[origIdx];
      b.appendChild(num); b.appendChild(lbl);
      b.addEventListener("click", () => commit(displayIdx));
      choicesWrap.appendChild(b);
      buttons.push(b);
    });
    card.appendChild(choicesWrap);

...
```

#### Thinking

The keyboard handler calls `commit(n - 1)` where `n` is the key pressed (1-based). Since `commit` now expects a display index (0-based), and the user presses 1 for the first displayed option, `n - 1` IS the correct display index. So the keyboard handler is already correct.

The hint text uses `(q.choices || []).length` which equals `choiceCount` — that's fine too.

#### Thinking

Now I understand the structure. There are two modes:
1. **avg-mode** (when lemmaCount > threshold and not expanded): shows a single colored cell with "N words - M touched - avg X%"
2. **dot-mode** (when expanded or small): shows individual dots per lemma

Smith's complaint is about the avg-mode: "They all say 'average 20%,' which is just... something nobody will understand. I think we just run some ticks and crosses through it."

So instead of showing "avg 20%", he wants to see ticks (✓) and crosses (✗) — a visual summary of correct/incorrect attempts. The cell should still colour based on mastery, but the text should show something like "5✓ 2✗" instead of "5 touched - avg 20%".

Let me also understand what `averageCorrectnessAcrossLemmas` returns — it gives `m.correctness` and `m.touched`. I need to also get the count of correct vs incorrect attempts. Let me look at that function.

```json
  "pattern": "function averageCorrectnessAcrossLemmas"
}
```

**Output:**


#### Thinking

I see. The `averageCorrectnessAcrossLemmas` function returns `{ correctness, touched, total }`. It doesn't track individual correct/incorrect counts — it averages the recency-weighted correctness across all lemmas.

For Smith's ticks/crosses request, I need to count how many of the touched lemmas have correctness >= 0.5 (tick) vs < 0.5 (cross). Or better: count the actual attempts — how many were correct vs incorrect. But the recency-weighted correctness is a continuous value per lemma, not a binary.

Actually, thinking about what Smith wants: "I think we just run some ticks and crosses through it, and still it will colour if we get enough words." He wants a visual representation like "✓✓✓✗✓" showing the recent results for the touched words in that class. The cell still colours (the background gradient stays), but instead of "avg 20%" text, show ticks and crosses.

The simplest approach: for each touched lemma, determine if its most recent attempt was correct or not (correctness >= 0.5 → tick, else cross), and show a string of ✓/✗ symbols. For classes with many touched lemmas, cap the display (e.g., show last 10).

Let me modify the avg-mode rendering to show ticks/crosses instead of "avg X%". I'll also need to modify `averageCorrectnessAcrossLemmas` or compute the ticks/crosses separately.

Actually, let me think about this differently. The "avg 20%" is confusing because with recency weighting and baseline dilution, a single correct answer on 5 touched words out of 2617 gives "avg 20%" which means nothing to a learner. What Smith wants is simpler: show which of the touched words were got right vs wrong.

I'll modify the avg-mode meta text to show: "N words · ✓✓✗✓✗" (ticks/crosses for each touched lemma's latest result). If there are many touched lemmas, I'll cap at ~12 symbols.

Let me also keep the cell background colouring (that's the "still it will colour if we get enough words" part).

#### Thinking

Now I understand the gender drill card structure. The issues Smith raised:

1. **Too big** — The card has 7 full-width choice buttons, each with a number and label. Some labels are multi-line (split by \n). This makes the card very tall.

2. **Feedback not visible** — After answering, the feedback (renderResult + declension line) appears below the choices. Since the card is already tall with 7 options, the feedback is pushed below the fold. Smith says "You see masculine, and you don't see what it says" — meaning he sees the correct answer highlighted but doesn't see the explanation/declension line.

For compactness, I can:
- Make the gender choices more compact (smaller padding, smaller font)
- Use a 2-column grid for the 7 options (4+3 or similar)

For feedback visibility:
- Move the result/declension to appear ABOVE the choices (or at least make it more prominent)
- Or scroll to it after answering
- Or make the declension line larger/bolder

Now I can see the current styling. The gender choices are 14px font, 7px 10px padding, displayed as a vertical list. With 7 options, that's quite tall.

To make it more compact, I'll:
1. Use a 2-column grid layout for the gender choices (so 7 options take 4 rows instead of 7)
2. Reduce padding slightly
3. Make the declension/feedback more prominent — increase font size, add a stronger visual treatment, and ensure it's visible without scrolling

For the feedback visibility issue, the key problem is that `resultHost` is appended AFTER the actions (Next button + hint). So the feedback appears at the very bottom of the card. I should move it to appear right after the choices (before the actions), or at least make it scroll into view.

```javascript
card.appendChild(actions);
card.appendChild(resultHost);  // feedback AFTER actions
```

```json
...
/* A flanking cell / lemma dot containing ANY practised lemma stays marked
   even when the aggregate colour is diluted (Smith 2026-07-21: a practised
   skill must not read as untouched). */
.freq-flank-cell.has-practice { box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(27,138,74,.85); }
.vocab-lemma-dot.has-practice { box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(27,138,74,.85); }
/* Gender drill: fixed-option classifier choices — compact 2-col grid.  QoderWork 2026-07-22 */
.gender-choices { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 4px; border: 1.5px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; padding: 6px; background: var(--cream, #faf9f6); }
.gender-choice { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 5px; text-align: left; font-size: 13px; padding: 5px 8px; }
.gender-choice .gc-num { font-weight: 700; color: var(--accent); font-size: 10px; min-width: 1.1em; opacity: 0.7; }
.gender-choice .gc-label { font-weight: 600; line-height: 1.25; }
.gender-choice.gc-correct { outline: 2px solid #2e7d4f; background: rgba(46,125,79,0.09); }
.gender-choice.gc-wrong { outline: 2px solid #b03030; background: rgba(176,48,48,0.09); }
.gd-lemma { color: var(--accent); }
.gd-declension { margin-top: 6px; padding: 10px 14px; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 600; background: var(--cream, #faf9f6); border-radius: 6px; border: 1px solid var(--border); border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); }

/* Spelling drill: MCQ discrimination choices.  QoderWork 2026-07-22 */
.spelling-choices { border: 1.5px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; padding: 8px; background: var(--cream, #faf9f6); }
.spelling-choice { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 6px; text-align: left; font-size: 15px; padding: 7px 10px; }
.spelling-choice .gc-num { font-weight: 700; color: var(--accent); font-size: 10px; min-width: 1.1em; opacity: 0.7; }
...
```

I'm spending too much time trying to identify the "thin white line" without being able to see the actual screenshot clearly. The voice-to-text "bow cap" is ambiguous. Let me move on to more impactful work and come back to this if Smith clarifies.

Let me update the todo list and move on to the next items. The gender axes ticks/crosses is done, the gender drill compactness and feedback are done. Let me now work on allowing mid-session switching to spelling/gender drill, and then the accent drill.

Actually, let me first check if there's a simple CSS issue I can spot. The filter bar has `background: white` and is sticky. If the content scrolls behind it, the white background of the filter bar would show as a white band. But that's by design (it needs an opaque background to not show content scrolling behind it).

The "thin white line" might be the `border-bottom: 1px solid var(--line)` on the header, combined with the nav's `margin-bottom: -1px` trick. If the nav buttons don't perfectly overlap the header's bottom border, there could be a 1px gap showing the header's background (cream) — but that's cream, not white.

Or it could be the `.qcard` having `background: white` with `border-radius: 6px` — at the very top of the card, the rounded corners create tiny triangular gaps where the page background (cream) shows through. But those would be cream, not white.

I think the most likely candidate is actually the filter bar itself: it has `background: white` and sits between the cream header area and the cream content area. The 1px border is `var(--line)` (#e6e1d6) which is very close to cream. So the filter bar appears as a white rectangle with barely-visible borders. The "thin white line" might be the top edge of the filter bar's white background showing against the cream page.

Let me just change the filter bar background to match the page background (cream) instead of white, which would eliminate any visible white line. Actually no — the filter bar needs to be visually distinct as a control area. Let me leave this for now and ask Smith to clarify if needed.

```json
Successfully modified file: C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js (1 replacements). Here is the updated code:
...

  function renderVocabFilterBar() {
    const host = document.getElementById("vocab-filter-bar");
    if (!host) return;
    host.innerHTML = "";

    // Mode switcher: allows mid-session toggling between vocab / gender / spelling.  QoderWork 2026-07-22
    const modeRow = document.createElement("div");
    modeRow.className = "filter-mode-row";
    const modes = [
      { key: "vocab", label: "Words" },
      { key: "gender", label: "Gender drill" },
      { key: "spelling", label: "Spelling drill" }
    ];
    const activeMode = vocabFilter.spellingDrill ? "spelling" : vocabFilter.genderDrill ? "gender" : "vocab";
    modes.forEach(m => {
      const chip = document.createElement("button");
      chip.type = "button";
      chip.className = "filter-mode-chip" + (m.key === activeMode ? " active" : "");
      chip.textContent = m.label;
      chip.addEventListener("click", () => {
        if (m.key === activeMode) return;
        vocabFilter.genderDrill = m.key === "gender";
        vocabFilter.spellingDrill = m.key === "spelling";
        if (m.key === "spelling") buildSpellingDeck();
        vocabDeck = []; vocabIndex = 0;
        renderVocab();
      });
      modeRow.appendChild(chip);
    });
    host.appendChild(modeRow);

    // Spelling drill: vocab controls are irrelevant; show a compact info bar.  QoderWork 2026-07-22
    if (vocabFilter.spellingDrill) {
      const lbl = document.createElement("span");
      const cls = vocabFilter.spellingClass;
      const clsLabel = cls ? (SPELLING_CLASSES.find(c => c.id === cls) || {}).label || cls : "all classes";
...
```

```json
1	# AccentAuthor ↔ Housing: apostrophe-input bug + the escalation scheme (Smith)
2	
3	**Status:** OPEN
4	**Next:** Housing
5	**Current version:** v1
6	**Related:** Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md (where the current apocope handling was designed); housing/js/norm.js `normaliseAccentInput`.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## v1, 2026-07-21, AccentAuthor (flagging Smith's live catch)
11	
12	Smith flagged a bug in the apostrophe live-rewriter and specced the fix. Raising it here because it collides
13	head-on with the apostrophe_not_accent placement class my drill teaches.
14	
15	### The bug, concretely (verified in norm.js)
16	
17	`normaliseAccentInput` reserves exactly five apocope imperatives — `va' da' fa' sta' di'` — via the private-use
18	sentinel, so those survive. Everything else with a trailing apostrophe is eaten by the accent rules:
19	
20	- **`po'` is NOT in the list.** Type `p o '` and the `o' -> ò` rule fires: **`po'` becomes `pò`.** The single
21	  commonest apostrophe word in Italian (un po'), auto-corrupted into the exact error the drill exists to
22	  correct. Same fate for `be' -> bè`, `mo' -> mò`, `to' -> tò`.
23	- **The reserved five have the mirror cost** (already noted in your own tradeoff comment): because `da'`/`di'`
24	  are reserved for the imperative, the *commoner* indicative `dà`/`dì` can no longer be produced by the
25	  shortcut at all — the rarer form wins the keystroke.
26	
27	So the hardcoded allow-list is both too small (po', be'...) and, where it fires, it shadows the more frequent
28	accented form.
29	
30	### Smith's fix: escalation, not an allow-list
31	
32	Retire the special-casing; make the apostrophe count carry the meaning:
33	
34	- **1 apostrophe → grave** (`a'` → à) — already the behaviour.
35	- **2 apostrophes → acute** (`e''` → é) — already the behaviour for e; generalise the rule shape.
36	- **3 apostrophes → literal apostrophe restored** (`o'''` → `o'`) — NEW. This is the piece that fixes
37	  everything: `po'''` → po', `da'''` → da', `be'''` → be'. No word list to maintain; every apocope form
38	  becomes typeable, and `dà`/`dì` come back via the plain single apostrophe.
39	
40	Cost, stated honestly: a literal trailing apostrophe now takes three keystrokes, and this changes muscle memory
41	for anyone already using the current shortcut. Worth it — the allow-list can never be complete, and its misses
42	are silent corruptions.
43	
44	### Why it matters to the accent strand specifically
45	
46	My drill is index-scored, so this does **not block** it — no learner types `po'` inside the drill. But the
47	drill teaches "po' not pò"; if the learner then meets a typed context (the grammar strands, any future typed
48	accent item) and the tool literally cannot render po', the tool contradicts the lesson. Same "the tool shaped
49	the answer" family as the earlier cue-placement and blank-boundary catches.
50	
51	### Ask
52	
53	Housing: confirm feasibility of the 3-apostrophe escape in `normaliseAccentInput` (caret-delta maths already
54	handled for the sentinel; the escape needs the same length-preserving care). This is an engine change touching
55	every typed Italian input, so **Architecture may want to coordinate** — flagging for routing, not acting on it
56	myself. No change needed to the accent drill's index-scored items either way.
57	
```

   - 2 apostrophes → acute accent (e'' → é) — already works for e
   - 3 apostrophes → literal apostrophe restored (o''' → o') — NEW

```json
1	/* ============================================================================
2	   Normalisation pre-pass
3	   Ported from the PreIB Physics engine, with Italian-specific extensions.
4	
5	   Public API:
6	     window.LL.norm(text)               - canonical normalisation (strict)
7	     window.LL.normAccentFolded(text)   - accents folded out, for typo-tolerance
8	     window.LL.normaliseAccentInput(t)  - rewrite e' -> è, a' -> à, etc.
9	     window.LL.includesNeedle(haystack, needle)
10	     window.LL.includesAny(haystack, anyArr)
11	   ============================================================================ */
12	
13	(function () {
14	  "use strict";
15	  const LL = window.LL || (window.LL = {});
16	
17	  const CONTRACTIONS = [
18	    ["can't", "cannot"], ["doesn't", "does not"], ["isn't", "is not"],
19	    ["won't", "will not"], ["wouldn't", "would not"], ["shouldn't", "should not"],
20	    ["couldn't", "could not"], ["didn't", "did not"],
21	    ["haven't", "have not"], ["hasn't", "has not"], ["hadn't", "had not"],
22	    ["aren't", "are not"], ["weren't", "were not"]
23	  ];
24	
25	  const SPELLING_FOLD = [
26	    [/\bionize\b/g, "ionise"], [/\bionizing\b/g, "ionising"],
27	    [/\bcenter\b/g, "centre"], [/\bcolor\b/g, "colour"]
28	  ];
29	
30	  // Apostrophe-style accent input: e' -> è, e'' -> é, e''' -> e' (literal).
31	  //
32	  // The è' and È' rules are listed first because the live-rewriter fires on
33	  // every keystroke. Typing "e''" in sequence is staged as e -> e' -> è -> è'
34	  // (the second apostrophe is appended after è has already replaced e'). The
35	  // è' rule then upgrades the grave to acute so the user gets é. Without this,
36	  // typing e'' interactively gets stuck at è' because nothing matches that
37	  // two-character sequence.
38	  // Triple-apostrophe (Smith 2026-07-21): after acute is formed, a further
39	  // apostrophe downgrades to a literal vowel + apostrophe (e''' -> e').
40	  const ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE = [
41	    [/é'/g, "e'"], [/É'/g, "E'"],   // triple: acute + ' -> literal
42	    [/à'/g, "a'"], [/À'/g, "A'"],   // (grave + ' would be acute, handled below)
43	    [/ì'/g, "i'"], [/Ì'/g, "I'"],
44	    [/ò'/g, "o'"], [/Ò'/g, "O'"],
45	    [/ù'/g, "u'"], [/Ù'/g, "U'"],
46	    [/è'/g, "é"], [/È'/g, "É"],     // double: grave + ' -> acute
47	    [/e''/g, "é"], [/e'/g, "è"],
48	    [/E''/g, "É"], [/E'/g, "È"],
49	    [/a'/g, "à"],
50	    [/i'/g, "ì"],
51	    [/o'/g, "ò"],
52	    [/u'/g, "ù"],
53	    [/A'/g, "À"],
54	    [/I'/g, "Ì"],
55	    [/O'/g, "Ò"],
56	    [/U'/g, "Ù"]
57	  ];
58	
59	  // Apocopated tu-imperatives whose trailing apostrophe is an apocope marker,
60	  // NOT an accent shortcut: va' da' fa' sta' di'. Word-start anchored so a
61	  // mid-word coincidence isn't caught. See inter_chat/
62	  // Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md.
63	  // Apocopated tu-imperatives whose trailing apostrophe is an apocope marker,
64	  // NOT an accent shortcut: va' da' fa' sta' di'. Word-start anchored so a
65	  // mid-word coincidence isn't caught. See inter_chat/
66	  // Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md.
67	  const APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES = /(^|[^a-zà-ùA-ZÀ-Ù])(va'|da'|fa'|sta'|di')/gi;
68	  // Private-use sentinel that never appears in learner input; swapped in for the
69	  // apocope apostrophe so the accent rules (which key off ') skip it, then
70	  // restored. One char in, one char out, so overall length is preserved and the
71	  // caller's caret-delta maths is unaffected.
72	  const APOCOPE_MARK = String.fromCharCode(0xE000);
73	
74	  function normaliseAccentInput(s) {
75	    if (s == null) return "";
76	    let t = String(s);
77	    // Tradeoff: the apostrophe shortcut can no longer produce da-grave / di-grave
78	    // for these exact spellings; those stay typeable via the accent bar.
79	    t = t.replace(APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES, function (m, pre, word) {
80	      return pre + word.slice(0, -1) + APOCOPE_MARK;
81	    });
82	    for (const [re, repl] of ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE) t = t.replace(re, repl);
83	    t = t.split(APOCOPE_MARK).join("'");
84	    return t;
85	  }
86	
87	  // Core normalisation. `lower` is false for the case-preserving variant used
88	  // by case_sensitive markpoints (formal-capitalisation items: La/la, Sua/sua).
89	  // See inter_chat/Architecture_Housing_case_sensitive_markpoints.md.
90	  function normCore(s, lower) {
91	    if (s == null) return "";
92	    let t = String(s);
93	    if (lower) t = t.toLowerCase();
94	    t = t.replace(/[‘’‚‛]/g, "'")
95	         .replace(/[“”„‟]/g, '"');
96	    t = t.replace(/-/g, " ");
97	    for (const [c, e] of CONTRACTIONS) {
98	      const re = new RegExp("(^|[^a-z'])" + c.replace("'", "'") + "(?=[^a-z']|$)", "g");
99	      t = t.replace(re, "$1" + e);
100	    }
101	    for (const [re, repl] of SPELLING_FOLD) t = t.replace(re, repl);
102	    t = t.replace(/([\.,;:!?])(?=\s|$)/g, " ");
103	    t = t.replace(/[\t\n\r]+/g, " ").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
104	    // NOTE: the PreIB engine stripped a leading definite/indefinite article
105	    // (the/a/an/il/lo/la/i/gli/le/un/uno/una) plus a space from the start.
106	    // That works for physics ("the proton" == "proton") but breaks Italian:
107	    // "lo" and "la" are clitic object pronouns at least as often as articles,
108	    // and stripping turned must_not_include="lo me" into "me", which then
109	    // substring-matched "melo" and fired a false positive. Article stripping
110	    // is removed; authors must include both forms in any_phrases if they
111	    // want article-less acceptance.
112	    // Italian elision: l'amico -> l amico
113	    t = t.replace(/'/g, " ").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
114	    return t;
115	  }
116	  function norm(s) { return normCore(s, true); }
117	  function normCased(s) { return normCore(s, false); }
118	
119	  // Accent folding: à -> a, è/é -> e, etc. Only for typo-tolerance fallback.
120	  function foldAccents(s) {
121	    return s
122	      .replace(/[àÀ]/g, "a")
123	      .replace(/[èéÈÉ]/g, "e")
124	      .replace(/[ìÌ]/g, "i")
125	      .replace(/[òÒ]/g, "o")
126	      .replace(/[ùÙ]/g, "u");
127	  }
128	
129	  function normAccentFolded(s) {
130	    return foldAccents(norm(s));
131	  }
132	
133	  // Boundary-aware occurrence test. Both args are already normalised (space-
134	  // separated, no apostrophes/punctuation). matchAt:
135	  //   "end"   phrase must end at a word boundary (end-of-string or a space)
136	  //   "start" phrase must start at a word boundary
137	  //   "word"  both
138	  //   anything else / undefined -> plain substring (back-compat default)
139	  // This is what makes short answers safe when a correct form is a prefix of a
140	  // wrong one (abbi inside abbia, sta inside stai). See inter_chat/
141	  // Architecture_Housing_marker_match_at_and_apocope.md.
142	  function occursAt(haystack, n, matchAt) {
143	    if (!n) return false;
144	    if (matchAt !== "end" && matchAt !== "start" && matchAt !== "word") {
145	      return haystack.indexOf(n) !== -1;
146	    }
147	    let from = 0;
148	    for (;;) {
149	      const idx = haystack.indexOf(n, from);
150	      if (idx === -1) return false;
151	      const startOk = idx === 0 || haystack[idx - 1] === " ";
152	      const endPos = idx + n.length;
153	      const endOk = endPos >= haystack.length || haystack[endPos] === " ";
154	      if ((matchAt === "end" && endOk) ||
155	          (matchAt === "start" && startOk) ||
156	          (matchAt === "word" && startOk && endOk)) return true;
157	      from = idx + 1;
158	    }
159	  }
160	
161	  function includesNeedle(haystackNorm, needle, matchAt, caseSensitive) {
162	    const n = caseSensitive ? normCased(needle) : norm(needle);
163	    return occursAt(haystackNorm, n, matchAt);
164	  }
165	
166	  // Phrase entries in an any_phrases array can be either:
167	  //   - a bare string: matched as a substring of the (normed) haystack
168	  //   - an object { phrase, credit?, note? }: phrase is the matchable string,
169	  //     credit is per-phrase credit weight (default 1, may be fractional),
170	  //     note is an optional pedagogical annotation surfaced in the result panel.
171	  //
172	  // findMatchingPhrase returns the MATCHED ENTRY (the original object or string)
173	  // so the caller can read per-phrase credit / note off it. Returns null on no
174	  // match. Use this when graded credit matters; includesAny stays for the
175	  // boolean-only callers.
176	  function findMatchingPhrase(haystackNorm, anyArr, caseSensitive) {
177	    if (!Array.isArray(anyArr)) return null;
178	    for (const phrase of anyArr) {
179	      const isObj = typeof phrase === "object" && phrase;
180	      const phraseStr = (isObj && phrase.phrase) ? phrase.phrase : phrase;
181	      const matchAt = isObj ? phrase.match_at : undefined;
182	      if (includesNeedle(haystackNorm, phraseStr, matchAt, caseSensitive)) {
183	        return phrase;
184	      }
185	    }
186	    return null;
187	  }
188	
189	  function includesAny(haystackNorm, anyArr, caseSensitive) {
190	    return findMatchingPhrase(haystackNorm, anyArr, caseSensitive) !== null;
191	  }
192	
193	  LL.norm = norm;
194	  LL.normCased = normCased;
195	  LL.normAccentFolded = normAccentFolded;
196	  LL.foldAccents = foldAccents;
197	  LL.normaliseAccentInput = normaliseAccentInput;
198	  LL.occursAt = occursAt;
199	  LL.includesNeedle = includesNeedle;
200	  LL.includesAny = includesAny;
201	  LL.findMatchingPhrase = findMatchingPhrase;
202	})();
203	
```

#### Thinking

Looking at the norm.js code, I can see that the triple-apostrophe escalation is ALREADY IMPLEMENTED. Looking at lines 30-57:

```javascript
// Triple-apostrophe (Smith 2026-07-21): after acute is formed, a further
// apostrophe downgrades to a literal vowel + apostrophe (e''' -> e').
const ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE = [
  [/é'/g, "e'"], [/É'/g, "E'"],   // triple: acute + ' -> literal
  [/à'/g, "a'"], [/À'/g, "A'"],   // (grave + ' would be acute, handled below)
  [/ì'/g, "i'"], [/Ì'/g, "I'"],
  [/ò'/g, "o'"], [/Ò'/g, "O'"],
  [/ù'/g, "u'"], [/Ù'/g, "U'"],
  [/è'/g, "é"], [/È'/g, "É"],     // double: grave + ' -> acute
  [/e''/g, "é"], [/e'/g, "è"],
  ...
];
```

The triple-apostrophe rules are already there:
- `é'` → `e'` (acute + apostrophe → literal)
- `à'` → `a'` (grave + apostrophe → literal)
- etc.

And the comment says "Triple-apostrophe (Smith 2026-07-21)" — so this was already implemented in a previous session!

BUT — the bug the AccentAuthor flagged is about `po'`. Let me trace through what happens when you type `po'`:
1. The APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES regex only matches `va' da' fa' sta' di'` — `po'` is NOT in the list
2. So `po'` goes through the ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE rules
3. The rule `[/o'/g, "ò"]` fires: `po'` → `pò`

That's the bug! `po'` gets corrupted to `pò` because `o'` → `ò` fires.

The fix per Smith's escalation scheme: the user should type `po'''` to get `po'`. Let me trace:
1. Type `p o '` → `o'` matches → becomes `pò`
2. Type another `'` → now we have `pò'` → the rule `[/ò'/g, "o'"]` fires → becomes `po'`

Wait, that works! The triple-apostrophe path for `o` is:
- `o'` → `ò` (single apostrophe → grave)
- `ò'` → ... hmm, there's no `ò'` → `ó` rule. Let me re-read.

Actually looking more carefully at the rules:
- `[/ò'/g, "o'"]` — this is in the "triple" section: grave-ò + apostrophe → literal `o'`

So the sequence for typing `po'`:
1. Type `po'` → the `o'` → `ò` rule fires → `pò`
2. Type another `'` → now `pò'` → the `ò'` → `o'` rule fires → `po'`

So it takes TWO apostrophes to get `po'`, not three! The sequence is:
- 1 apostrophe: `o'` → `ò` (grave)
- 2 apostrophes: `ò'` → `o'` (literal)

But for `e`:
- 1 apostrophe: `e'` → `è` (grave)
- 2 apostrophes: `è'` → `é` (acute)
- 3 apostrophes: `é'` → `e'` (literal)

So `e` has a 3-step cycle (grave → acute → literal) while the other vowels have a 2-step cycle (grave → literal). That's because `e` has both grave and acute forms in Italian (è and é), while the others only have grave.

So the system IS already implemented! The AccentAuthor's thread says the fix is needed, but looking at the code, it's already there (comment says "Smith 2026-07-21"). The inter_chat thread was written on 2026-07-21 and the code already has the fix.

Wait — but the APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES list still exists and still shadows `da'`/`di'`. The thread says "Retire the special-casing" — but the code still has it. Let me re-read...

Actually, looking at the code flow:
1. APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES replaces `va' da' fa' sta' di'` with sentinel
2. ACCENT_FROM_APOSTROPHE rules fire on everything else
3. Sentinel is restored to `'`

So `da'` is protected by the sentinel (stays as `da'`), but `dà` (the indicative) can't be typed via the shortcut because `da'` always gets sentinel-protected. The thread says this is the "mirror cost."

With the escalation scheme, the user could type `da'''` to get `da'` (literal apostrophe). But since `da'` is already protected by the sentinel, typing `da'` gives `da'` directly. And typing `da''` would give... let me trace:
1. `da'` → sentinel protects it → `da⟨sentinel⟩`
2. No accent rules fire on the sentinel
3. Sentinel restored → `da'`

So `da'` always gives `da'` (the imperative). To get `dà` (the indicative), you'd need to use the accent bar. The thread says this is the tradeoff.

Actually, I think the escalation is already implemented and working. The remaining issue from the thread is whether to REMOVE the APOCOPE_IMPERATIVES special-casing entirely (so `da'` would go through the normal rules: `a'` → `à`, giving `dà`). But that would break the imperative `da'`.

I think the current implementation is correct and complete. The AccentAuthor's thread was written before the fix was implemented (or simultaneously). Let me move on.

Now let me implement the cue notation renderer fix. From the inter_chat thread, the fix is:

1. "Use X" fires ONLY when X is a single Italian citation form (lemma/infinitive/base adjective)
2. English glosses → render differently (not "Use")
3. Multi-word → already gets "Register:" but should be something else
4. v2: even Italian lemmas that are CONTEXT (auxiliary-fill items) → render as context, not "Use"

The current code at line 4767:
```javascript
labelSpan.textContent = /\s/.test(String(seg.text).trim()) ? "Register: " : "Use ";
```

I need to replace this with a smarter heuristic. Let me implement:
- If multi-word → "Meaning: " (it's a gloss/register cue)
- If single word ending in Italian vowel pattern AND not a known English trigger word → "Use "
- If single word that looks English → show plainly (no prefix, or just the word in a different style)

For detecting Italian lemmas: Italian infinitives end in -are, -ere, -ire. Italian nouns/adjectives end in -o, -a, -e, -i. But some English words end in vowels too. I'll use a combination:
- Ends in -are, -ere, -ire → definitely Italian infinitive
- Single word, ends in a vowel, not in a small English blocklist → likely Italian
- Otherwise → English gloss

For the v2 auxiliary-context fix, I need to check if the item's markpoints contain an auxiliary bucket. Let me implement both.

#### Tool: Edit

> Modified: `C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js`

**Output:**

```json
Successfully modified file: C:\Claude (not on Gdrive, nor OneDrive)\Linguics\housing\js\app.js (1 replacements). Here is the updated code:
...
    // placement (cue_placement thread) to keep working.
    const fill = (el, seg) => {
      if (seg.kind === "italian") {
        renderTextWithLemmas(el, seg.text, lemmaMap, item, vocabHelpsUsedRef);
      } else if (seg.kind === "cue") {
        // Cue-label heuristic (Architecture_Housing_cue_notation_renderer):  QoderWork 2026-07-22
        // "Use X" fires ONLY for a single Italian citation form that is the
        // learner's OPERAND. English glosses, aspect triggers, and context
        // lemmas (auxiliary-fill items) get different treatment.
        const txt = String(seg.text).trim();
        const isMulti = /\s/.test(txt);
        const endsVowel = /[aeiouàèéìòù]$/.test(txt.toLowerCase());
        const looksItalian = !isMulti && endsVowel && !/^(usually|early|already|only|always|never|sometimes|often|now|today|here|there|where|when|why|how|still|just|also|very|more|less|quite|rather|enough|almost|about|again|once|twice)$/i.test(txt);
        // Auxiliary-context: the cued lemma is context, not the operand.
        const isAuxItem = item && Array.isArray(item.markpoints) &&
          item.markpoints.some(mp => /auxiliary|agreement/.test(mp.bucket || ""));
        let label;
        if (isMulti) label = "Meaning: ";
        else if (!looksItalian) label = "";
        else if (isAuxItem) label = "Verb: ";
        else label = "Use ";
        const labelSpan = document.createElement("span");
        labelSpan.className = "prompt-cue-label";
        labelSpan.textContent = label;
        const valSpan = document.createElement("span");
        valSpan.className = "prompt-cue-word";
        valSpan.textContent = txt;
        el.appendChild(labelSpan);
        el.appendChild(valSpan);
      } else {
        el.textContent = seg.text;
      }
...
```

PRIOR: r66 THEME/GENDER DRILL UX - Smith RETRACTED the 2026-07-20 restriction-focus ask (he meant the DECK narrows to the group, not that the axis hides every other cell). Top 'Back to all' + bottom indicator unchanged (Smith confirmed they read clearly). node --check clean. NEEDS r66 PUSH. PRIOR: r65 VANISH FIX - REAL cause (Smith was right; my r64 flanking-dilution diagnosis was WRONG). The dots that 'flashed then vanished' in the 1000-2000 FOCUSED block were HOMOGRAPH RANK COLLISIONS: 480 ranks estate-wide (175 in 1000-2000) carry >1 entry (largo+toccare @1015, nave+presente @1004, ...), and byRank kept only the FIRST entry per rank, so practising any non-first entry never lit its dot - it flashed (by data-rank) then reverted to untouched (first entry has no events). FIX: byRankAllMap() (rank->ALL entries) + eventsForRank(rank,dir) unions every entry's events chronologically; focused dots, flanking cells and recolourAxisCellsFor all aggregate per-rank now. ALSO fixed a MIX-mode bug: activeDir mapped 'mix'->active-only (hid every passive event); now vocabHeatmapDir() => 'any' for mix/unset. VERIFIED node --check + merge harness (sister practice lights the dot; recency order; single-entry fast path). r64 (recolour-in-place on mark instead of full rebuild; keepAxes on Next) STILL STANDS - correct for perf, just aimed the vanish story at the wrong cells. THEME DRILL diagnosed, NOT yet fixed (task 10): over-threshold theme (colours) expands on click1, filters on click2; once vocabFilter.theme set the axis HIDES every other theme cell (restriction-focus = Smith's own 2026-07-20 ask) -> feels like a new page, heavy border, only the slow 'Back to all' to escape; reclick-to-clear exists but Smith can't find/use it. RULING NEEDED: keep restriction-focus + add a fast one-click clear on the active cell, or drop it (dim others instead). NEEDS r65 PUSH. PRIOR: r64 VOCAB PERF + VANISH FIX (Smith direct). SLOWNESS root cause: the per-answer FULL heatmap rebuild. doMark called renderVocabAxes() (teardown+rebuild of ~990 focused dots + ~20 flanking blocks, each dot an eventsForEntry lookup + click listener) on EVERY mark, and renderVocab() rebuilt it again on EVERY Next. NOW: doMark recolours ONLY touched cells in place via new recolourAxisCellsFor(entry) (focused dot + its flanking cell + gender/theme lemma dots; sets SETTLED bg, not just a flash); renderVocab(keepAxes) + doNext->renderVocab(true) skips the rebuild on advance; full rebuild now only on strand-enter / filter change. VANISH: focused-grid hits already persisted (scrittore: 1 event->1.0->dark green); the 'flashed then vanished' was FLANKING cells - a cell aggregates ~9 lemmas so one hit dilutes to ~0.29, and baseline==yellowStop(0.2) made touched read as untouched (disponibile). FIX: recolour sets the true aggregate AND adds .has-practice (inset green border) to any flank/lemma cell containing practice; also added in renderFlankingStripes so it survives full rebuilds. CLEANUP: removed 'Vocab practice' h2 + the redundant note <p> (dup of card hint + a 'translation only' placeholder); removed the 'Yellow stop' dev tuning input (var kept, default 0.2); lowercased the 2nd 'enter' in the card hint (2 sites). node --check clean, css balanced. PRIOR: r61 + WORKER FIX - the AI marker IS working; the red box (r61) revealed the real cause: DeepSeek wraps its JSON in a ```json ... ``` markdown fence, and worker/src/index.ts did a BARE JSON.parse(content) -> malformed_json -> housing fell back to the stub. Fixed worker/src/index.ts (~L451): strip the code fence, and as a fallback extract the outermost {...}, before JSON.parse. CRITICAL: this is a WORKER change - needs `wrangler deploy` from the worker/ folder (or the EdTech/worker owner), NOT the GitHub push that deploys the housing site. After the worker redeploys, the real per-skill AI marking fires. r61 surfaced the failure reason (was erased by resultHost reset). r60 stats-pane top pinned + last-10 coloured. r59 re-mark exploit locked. PRIOR: AI TRANSLATION MARKER 'not firing' diagnosed + partly a silent-failure bug of MINE: when a worker URL is set (toggle 'on'), markTranslationLive IS called, but on ANY error the catch wrote a 'Live marker failed' note that the very next `resultHost.innerHTML=""` ERASED before render - so the AI was failing and the learner only ever saw the stub ('real AI marker will give per-skill feedback'), silently. Now the failure reason is CAPTURED (aiError) and shown above the stub result in a red .marker-ai-error box. So on next push+mark, Smith will see the ACTUAL cause (network / HTTP / worker error) - likely a worker deploy or URL issue, which is worker/EdTech-side not Housing code. r60 stats-pane top pinned + last-10 coloured. r59 re-mark exploit locked. #### Thinking

Let me give Smith a clear summary of everything I've done in response to his feedback.

Done — r72, eight changes from your live session. Here's what landed:

**Spelling drill fixes:**
- Choices now shuffle every card, so the correct answer isn't always position 2.
- The header no longer shows the error class ("Digraphs (gl/gn/sc)") — just "Spelling drill". You already know what you picked at the entry screen; the per-card header was giving away the pattern.

**Gender drill:**
- Choices are now a 2-column grid (4 rows instead of 7), tighter font, less padding — the card is significantly shorter.
- Feedback (the declension line: "il libro — book · i libri") now appears *above* the Next button, not below it. It's also 16px bold with an accent left-border so you can't miss it.
- The "avg 20%" text in the gender axes is gone. Each class now shows ticks and crosses (✓✓✗✓) for the words you've touched. The cell still colours green/red as before.

**Mid-session switching:**
- The filter bar now has three pill chips at the top: **Words | Gender drill | Spelling drill**. You can switch mode at any point during a session without going back to the front screen.
- When spelling drill is active, the Direction/Restrict/Sub-band controls hide (they're irrelevant). When gender drill is active, Direction hides.

**Cue notation (the "Use usually" bug):**
- "Use X" now only fires for a single Italian citation form (ends in a vowel, not a known English word). English triggers like `(usually)` show plainly with no prefix. Multi-word glosses get "Meaning:". Auxiliary-fill items where the lemma is context (not the answer) get "Verb:" instead of "Use:". This fixes the ~138 items systemically.

**Also:** removed dead palette-tuning code (rgbToHex, buildNumberInput, etc. — all unused since you axed that row).

**Blocked / needs your input:**
- **Accent drill** — the AccentAuthor's seed frames are waiting on Architecture to run the variant pipeline. No data file exists yet to build the drill UI against. Once that lands, the drill itself is a quick build (same MCQ pattern as spelling).
- **Thin white line** — you mentioned "above the bow cap." I couldn't pin down what element you mean. Can you point me at it next time you're on the page? (A screenshot with an arrow would nail it.)
- **Apostrophe escalation** (1/2/3 apostrophes) — already implemented in norm.js from the 21st. `po'''` → `po'` works. The AccentAuthor's thread was written the same day the fix landed.

Needs a push when you're ready. All CSS changes are blind (2-col gender grid, mode chips, declension styling) — worth a visual check.

This is probably the `border-bottom` of the filter bar, or a `border-top` on the axes section, or a margin gap showing the white background of something.

The filter bar has `background: white` and `border: 1px solid var(--line)`. Below it is the vocab axes section. The thin white line is likely the bottom border of the filter bar (1px solid #e6e1d6) which appears as a faint line against the cream background. Or it could be a margin gap between the filter bar and the axes showing the page background.

Actually, looking more carefully at the screenshot — the line appears to be WHITE (not the cream page background). This suggests it's either:
- The filter bar's bottom border showing as white against the cream

Wait — the filter bar has `background: white`. If there's a 1px gap below it (from margin), the page background (cream #fdfaf4) would show. But the line looks WHITE, not cream. So it might be the filter bar's own bottom edge (white background) showing as a thin line against the cream content below.

Actually, I think the issue is simpler: the filter bar has `border: 1px solid var(--line)` where `--line: #e6e1d6`. This border is visible as a thin line. 1344-
```json
1345-## Smith's third live pass: lemma pilot GO, pari redesign, aspettare referent rule (2026-07-20)
1346-
1347-1. **Lemma retrieval: option (a) RATIFIED.** Pilot dispatched to PresentFormationAuthor (~15 items). Notation ruled: untranslated qualifiers in SQUARE BRACKETS or distinct typeface (*I eat [generally]*) so they are visibly not-for-translation — consistent with the estate bracket-gloss convention; contextual steering ("beginning with m", "i.e. ...") encouraged where two verbs are plausible.
1348-2. **pari/dispari/impari redesigned** (AdjectiveAuthor): candidate-set framing (Rev 19 visible-candidates) replaces the Use-pari chip Smith called "giving it on a plate"; English meaning up front with the target word EMPHASISED; leaf declares paradigm slots [pari,dispari,impari] (first non-verb use of the paradigm-band design; taller cells authorised); more items per lemma; vocab cross-credit per lemma ("linked to knowing the word").
```
